This inhibition of PRV replication is not relieved by antioxidants such as N-acetyl-L-cysteine or reduced glutathione, indicating a non-oxidative mechanism of action. Beyond its antiviral properties, gossypol exhibits multiple therapeutic applications including treatment of endometriosis, uterine fibroids, and dysfunctional uterine bleeding, alongside documented anti-inflammatory, anti-parasitic, and antioxidant effects. Gossypol achieves its broader antitumor properties through induction of mitochondrial dysfunction, autophagy, and apoptosis in target cells. At effective antiviral concentrations (≤3 µM), gossypol demonstrates no cytotoxicity to mammalian cells, making it a promising therapeutic candidate with a favorable safety profile.

title: Dietary Flavonoid Intake and Anemia Risk in Children and Adolescents: Insights from National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that elevated dietary flavonoid intake is significantly associated with reduced anemia risk in children and adolescents, with flavan-3-ols showing the strongest protective effects. Oxidative stress and reactive oxygen species (ROS) contribute substantially to anemia pathogenesis by damaging red blood cell membranes through lipid peroxidation and activating inflammatory signaling pathways that upregulate hepcidin, thereby impairing iron absorption and bioavailability. Flavonoids exert their protective mechanisms through multiple pathways: they neutralize free radicals, inhibit oxidative signaling, improve red blood cell survival, and enhance antioxidant defense systems. Notable sex and ethnic disparities were observed, with females representing 74% of anemic participants and Black individuals overrepresented at 40%, likely due to menstruation-related iron loss in women and differential ROS production between sexes modulated by estrogen's antioxidant properties. The findings highlight that inadequate dietary flavonoid intake from plant-based sources including tea, fruits, vegetables, and cocoa represents a modifiable risk factor for anemia, particularly in vulnerable populations with developing antioxidant defenses such as adolescents during puberty.

title: Baicalin, Amoxicillin, and Probenecid Provide Protection in Mice Against Glaesserella parasuis Challenge
summary: This study demonstrates that baicalin, amoxicillin, and probenecid provide protective effects against *Glaesserella parasuis* infection in mice through complementary mechanisms. The bacterial pathogen triggers Glässer's disease characterized by fibrinous polyserositis, polyarthritis, and meningitis, which involves activation of the NLRP3 inflammasome and elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-18, TNF-α, and HMGB1). All three compounds inhibit NLRP3 inflammasome activation and suppress these inflammatory mediators, with baicalin and amoxicillin additionally upregulating CD3+ and CD3+CD4+ T cell populations to restore adaptive immunity suppressed by infection. Treatment with amoxicillin and probenecid substantially reduced bacterial burden in organs and achieved up to 100% survival protection, while baicalin achieved 90% protection through its anti-inflammatory properties. These findings establish multiple therapeutic targets for combating *G. parasuis* infection and suggest that combining antimicrobial therapy with anti-inflammatory agents may provide enhanced clinical benefit.

title: Synthesis Mechanism and Therapeutic Effects of Thiosulfinates and Polysulfides of Different Species of Garlic from the Allium Genus
summary:
# Summary

Garlic species from the Allium genus synthesize bioactive sulfur compounds through a well-characterized enzymatic pathway initiated when the enzyme alliinase encounters its substrate alliin, catalyzing a beta-elimination reaction that generates allicin and pyruvate. Allicin serves as a central hub for downstream synthesis of diverse therapeutic compounds including thiosulfinates, polysulfides, and ajoene through various hydrolysis and condensation reactions. These sulfur-derived compounds exhibit broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity against pathogenic bacteria and fungi, antiviral effects against influenza and HIV, and potent anticancer properties through mechanisms including cell proliferation inhibition, apoptosis promotion, and enhancement of carcinogen detoxification pathways via glutathione and glutathione-S-transferase upregulation. Additionally, allicin modulates metabolic pathways by upregulating AMPK phosphorylation and downregulating SREBP-1, while inhibiting inflammatory enzymes cyclooxygenase and lipoxygenase. These findings establish garlic phytochemicals as multifaceted therapeutic agents with significant potential for treating microbial infections, cancer, metabolic disorders, and inflammatory conditions.

title: Bioactivity of Synthesized Trifluoromethyl Thioxanthone Analogues
summary:
# Summary

This study synthesized and evaluated trifluoromethyl thioxanthone derivatives for multiple biological activities relevant to inflammation-related diseases. The synthesized compounds demonstrated potent antioxidant properties, with compound 3 exhibiting the highest activity at 46.6% inhibition in the DPPH assay, while compound 1 showed the strongest anticancer activity against HeLa cells with an IC50 of 87.8 nM, exceeding the standard drug doxorubicin. All tested compounds displayed significant COX-1 and COX-2 inhibitory activities with IC50 values in the nanomolar range, indicating strong anti-inflammatory potential comparable to or superior to the clinical reference Celecoxib. Notably, the presence of cysteine moieties enhanced both antioxidant and COX-2 inhibitory activities, while tertiary alcohol structures without cysteine favored anticancer potency. Additional metabolic enzyme inhibitory activities were observed, with compound 2 showing moderate alpha-amylase inhibition and compound 4 demonstrating pancreatic lipase inhibition, suggesting these derivatives may have broader therapeutic applications in managing inflammation-associated disorders including cancer and metabolic diseases.

title: Dihydromyricetin improving myocardial function in the mice induced by CCl4
journal: International Journal of Immunopathology and Pharmacology
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that dihydromyricetin (DMY), a polyphenolic flavonoid, protects against carbon tetrachloride (CCl₄)-induced myocardial dysfunction in mice through inhibition of the NLRP3 inflammasome and IL-33/ST2 signaling pathways. CCl₄ exposure triggered myocardial injury by activating NLRP3 inflammasome, which recruited caspase-1 to process pro-IL-33 into active IL-33, subsequently activating downstream NF-κB and MAPK inflammatory signaling and causing cardiomyocyte hypertrophy and dysfunction. DMY treatment suppressed this pathogenic cascade by downregulating NLRP3 inflammasome activation, reducing IL-33 and ST2 expression, and inhibiting the pro-inflammatory iNOS pathway while upregulating the protective Arg-1 pathway. Additionally, DMY demonstrated potent antioxidant effects, decreasing oxidative markers (H₂O₂ and MDA) and increasing antioxidant enzymes (SOD) to restore cardiac function. The therapeutic benefits were reflected in improved left ventricular ejection fraction and fractional shortening, with NLRP3 knockout further enhancing DMY's cardioprotective effects, confirming the central role of this inflammasome in the mechanism.

title: Considering Long-Acting Synthetic Cannabidiol for Chronic Pain: A Narrative Review
summary:
# Summary

This narrative review evaluates cannabidiol (CBD) and related cannabinoids as therapeutic agents for chronic pain management. CBD, a major constituent of Cannabis sativa, treats multiple pain conditions including chronic pain, neuropathic pain, cancer pain, and endometriosis-associated chronic pelvic pain through multiple molecular mechanisms. CBD exerts its analgesic effects via interactions with the endocannabinoid system and numerous other receptors including CB-1 and CB-2 receptors, the 5-HT-1A serotonin receptor, and transient receptor potential vanilloid 1, while enhancing endocannabinoid signaling by anandamide and 2-arachidonoylglycerol. Beyond receptor binding, CBD reduces neural inflammation and oxidative stress by inhibiting pro-inflammatory cytokine production (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-8), suppressing COX-1 and COX-2 expression, and blocking neutrophil activation. Clinical evidence demonstrates that CBD reduces opioid consumption in chronic pain patients and improves comorbid depression, anxiety, and sleep disturbances, with potential synergistic benefits when combined with palmitoylethanolamide. These multifaceted mechanisms position synthetic long-acting CBD formulations as promising alternatives for managing treatment-resistant chronic pain conditions.

title: Action and mechanisms of neferine in inflammatory diseases (Review)
summary:
# Summary

Neferine, a natural polyphenolic compound, exhibits potent anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties across multiple inflammatory disease models. The compound reduces symptoms of inflammation by simultaneously inhibiting key signaling pathways including NF-κB, MAPK, TLR4, and the NLRP3 inflammasome, while suppressing the production of pro-inflammatory cytokines such as TNF-α, IL-6, IL-8, and IL-1β. Neferine also activates antioxidant defense mechanisms through the Nrf2/HO-1 pathway and promotes expression of antioxidant enzymes including SOD1 and NQO1, thereby reducing reactive oxygen species levels. In disease models, neferine demonstrates therapeutic efficacy in treating ulcerative colitis, pulmonary fibrosis, liver fibrosis, atrial fibrillation, asthma, atopic dermatitis, acute kidney injury, and various neurological conditions including seizures and hypoxic-ischemic brain injury. These multi-targeted mechanisms, combining inflammatory suppression with antioxidant enhancement, position neferine as a promising natural therapeutic agent for inflammatory diseases.

title: Garlic-Derived Exosome-Like Nanovesicles: A Promising Natural Nanotherapy for Periodontitis via PHGDH/PI3K/AKT-Mediated Metabolic and Inflammatory Regulation
summary: Garlic-derived exosome-like nanovesicles (GaELNs) represent a promising natural nanotherapy for periodontitis, a chronic inflammatory disease caused by bacterial infection and characterized by alveolar bone loss and tissue destruction. GaELNs exert their therapeutic effects primarily through activation of the PHGDH/PI3K/AKT signaling pathway, which upregulates antioxidant responses via Nrf2 activation and tissue repair mechanisms through mTOR while simultaneously inhibiting the pro-inflammatory NF-κB pathway. In mouse periodontitis models, local GaELNs administration significantly reduced gingival inflammation and alveolar bone resorption, while in vitro studies demonstrated enhanced proliferation and migration of human gingival fibroblasts along with increased VEGF expression. Mechanistically, GaELNs alleviate cellular inflammation, oxidative stress, and mitochondrial dysfunction by suppressing excessive glycolytic metabolism and reactive oxygen species accumulation that would otherwise drive uncontrolled osteoclast activity and periodontal tissue destruction. The broad therapeutic potential of GaELNs extends beyond periodontitis to treating colitis, liver dysfunction, osteoarthritis, and adipose tissue inflammation, positioning natural polyphenol-based nanovesicles as viable alternatives to conventional therapeutics for inflammatory diseases.

title: Promising impacts of Achillea spp., beyond A medicinal plant, against toxins, toxicities, and injuries: In vivo and in vitro mechanisms
summary:
# Summary

Achillea species exhibit broad therapeutic potential through multiple complementary mechanisms relevant to tissue injury and inflammatory conditions. The genus demonstrates potent antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activities, with Achillea millefolium, A. wilhelmsii, A. fragrantissima, and other species suppressing oxidative stress, attenuating inflammatory responses, and reducing oxidative DNA damage while lessening apoptosis. Key bioactive compounds including quercetin and apigenin mediate these effects partly through COX-2 inhibition and nuclear factor modulation. Achillea millefolium specifically shows broad-spectrum cytoprotection against diverse toxins including alloxan, chemotherapy agents (bleomycin, cisplatin), heavy metals (carbon tetrachloride), and endotoxins (lipopolysaccharide), while also improving metabolic markers by reducing serum glucose, triglycerides, and cholesterol levels. At the tissue level, Achillea preparations enhance collagen proliferation while paradoxically reducing excessive myofibroblast proliferation, supporting both tissue repair and fibrosis prevention mechanisms relevant to conditions characterized by aberrant inflammation and fibrotic remodeling.

title: Effect of omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acid on endometriosis
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic, estrogen-dependent condition characterized by misplaced endometrial tissue that causes pain, infertility, and dysmenorrhea through excessive prostaglandin production and inflammatory responses. Omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids (ω-3 PUFAs), including EPA and DHA, reduce inflammatory markers such as TNF-alpha, IL-6, and IL-1 by inhibiting NF-κB pathway activation and regulating prostaglandin synthesis, thereby addressing the inflammatory pathology underlying endometriosis. However, clinical evidence from multiple randomized controlled trials shows that ω-3 PUFA supplementation produces no statistically significant improvements in endometriosis-related pain, pelvic pain, sexual dysfunction, or quality of life measures, despite demonstrable reductions in circulating cytokine levels. The ineffectiveness in symptom relief may relate to women with endometriosis potentially lacking adequate PUFAs in their uterine microenvironment, suggesting that systemic supplementation may not sufficiently restore local tissue levels. While ω-3 PUFAs show promise in mechanistic studies and may prevent endometriosis development through dietary intake, the data do not support their use as a therapeutic intervention for established disease symptoms. Complementary approaches, such as olive oil containing oleocanthal, warrant investigation as adjunctive anti-inflammatory strategies for managing endometriosis pain.

title: Biochemical impact of ALAEm supplementation in late gestation on the reproductive performance of sows
summary:
# Summary

Dietary supplementation with ALAEm, a polyphenol-rich extract containing Angelica sinensis, Astragalus mongholicus, and Eucommia ulmoides, significantly improves sow reproductive performance by enhancing placental function during late gestation. ALAEm strengthens the placental barrier through increased expression of tight junction proteins including ZO-1 and Claudin-1, while simultaneously promoting angiogenesis via upregulation of VEGFA and PlGF and growth factors EGF and IGF-1. The supplement exhibits potent antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects, reducing reactive oxygen species and increasing expression of SOD, CAT, and HO-1, while suppressing pro-inflammatory IL-1β and STAT3 signaling. ALAEm also reduces placental apoptosis by downregulating Caspase-3 and Bax, and enhances nutrient transport through increased GLUT1 and SNAT2 expression. These molecular changes translate into improved reproductive outcomes, including higher numbers of piglets born alive and healthy, increased litter weights, and elevated immune markers including IgA and red blood cell counts in treated sows.

title: A mechanism of global gene expression regulation is disrupted by multiple disease states and drug treatments
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals a fundamental mechanism of global gene expression regulation based on nucleobase and amino acid availability that is disrupted across multiple disease states and by numerous pharmaceutical treatments. The researchers demonstrate that mRNA expression levels are directly influenced by the availability of specific nucleobases (purines A/G and pyrimidines C/U) and amino acid supply, with mRNAs containing extreme nucleobase compositions—particularly those enriched in A+U or C+G—being most sensitive to these substrate constraints. Multiple disease conditions including Alzheimer's disease, schizophrenia, autism spectrum disorder, and Lesch-Nyhan syndrome exhibit characteristic distortions in global expression profiles associated with altered nucleobase ratios, suggesting impaired purine metabolism as a common pathological mechanism. Notably, at least 115 of 597 chemical entities tested distort global expression profiles through this mechanism, including commonly prescribed medications such as antibiotics, proton pump inhibitors, and various chemotherapy agents. The study further identifies tissue-specific vulnerabilities, with tissues showing extreme nucleobase compositions—such as fetal tissues, ovary, spleen, and embryonic stem cells—appearing more susceptible to transcriptional reprogramming through substrate availability adjustment. These findings propose a unifying explanation for how metabolic disruptions and drug treatments can cause widespread transcriptional changes independent of traditional gene regulatory pathways.

title: Proanthocyanidins delaying the premature ovarian insufficiency through regulatory sirt1-p53-p21 signaling pathway in female germline stem cells
summary:
# Summary

Proanthocyanidins (PACs), natural polyphenolic compounds with anti-aging and anti-inflammatory properties, delay premature ovarian insufficiency (POI) by promoting female germline stem cell (FGSC) proliferation and restoring ovarian function through activation of the Sirt1-p53-p21 signaling pathway. Treatment with PACs increases the number of primordial and primary follicles while reducing cystic follicles, and elevates circulating levels of estradiol and anti-mullerian hormone in a dose-dependent manner. At the molecular level, PACs upregulate stem cell markers MVH and OCT4 while downregulating the pro-apoptotic factors p53 and p21, effects that depend critically on Sirt1 activation as demonstrated by inhibitor studies. Sirt1 engagement improves oocyte and granulosa cell function while suppressing mitochondrial dysfunction, oxidative stress, and apoptosis—multiple mechanisms contributing to preserved fertility and prevention of POI-associated complications including hormonal imbalances and secondary diseases.

title: Advances in magnetic field approaches for non-invasive targeting neuromodulation
summary:
# Summary

This article advances the understanding of non-invasive magnetic field-based neuromodulation techniques as alternatives to invasive deep brain stimulation for treating neurological disorders. Magnetic fields modulate neuronal ion channels and neuronal activity through multiple mechanisms, including electromagnetic induction of electrochemical currents and direct interactions with ions and proteins, with frequency-dependent effects—extremely low-frequency fields inhibit neuronal activity and reduce seizure frequency, while high-frequency fields enhance activity to improve depression symptoms. Magnetothermal stimulation, combining magnetic fields with functionalized nanoparticles for localized neural tissue heating, and transcranial magnetic stimulation represent promising non-invasive approaches for treating epilepsy, movement disorders, neurodegenerative diseases, chronic pain, depression, and anxiety. The underlying neurobiophysics reveals that ion channel regulation is fundamental to these effects, as potassium and calcium channels control neuronal excitability, membrane potentials, and synaptic transmission through ion flow regulation. Beyond neuromodulation, magnetic fields broadly modulate cellular processes including survival, proliferation, apoptosis, differentiation, gene expression, and protein synthesis, positioning magnetic field technology as a versatile therapeutic platform for multiple neurological conditions with reduced surgical risk compared to traditional invasive approaches.

title: Unveiling vitamin C: A new hope in the treatment of diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (Review)
summary:
# Summary

This review examines vitamin C as a potential therapeutic agent for diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL), a hematological malignancy where pharmacological doses induce apoptosis through multiple mechanisms. Vitamin C inhibits tumor cell proliferation and increases reactive oxygen species (ROS) levels, leading to DNA damage and ATP depletion in cancer cells while simultaneously enhancing TET2 function, which is critical since TET2 mutations occur in 10-25% of myeloid malignancies. The compound promotes apoptosis partly by inhibiting anti-apoptotic BCL2 protein expression and activating pro-apoptotic Bax, while upregulating wild-type p53 and inhibiting HIF-1α to suppress survival pathways. Beyond direct anti-tumor effects, vitamin C enhances immune function by stimulating lymphocyte development, promoting phagocytosis, and shifting cytokine profiles toward anti-inflammatory responses by reducing TNF-α and IFN-γ while increasing IL-10. While the current standard R-CHOP regimen has improved 5-year overall survival to 60-70%, this review positions high-dose vitamin C as a complementary or adjuvant strategy to overcome treatment resistance mechanisms in DLBCL, particularly in cases with unfavorable prognostic markers like Myc/BCL2/BCL6 gene rearrangements.

title: Combination of RNA-sequencing data analysis, network pharmacology, molecular docking techniques to investigate the mechanism of Prunella vulgaris L. in the treatment of non-small cell lung cancer
summary:
# Summary

Prunella vulgaris L. (PVL), a medicinal herb from the Labiaceae family, demonstrates therapeutic potential against non-small cell lung cancer through multiple polyphenolic compounds including morin, luteolin, delphinidin, kaempferol, and quercetin. These flavonoids inhibit NSCLC cell proliferation and viability while promoting apoptosis and inducing cell cycle arrest through binding to and downregulating core oncogenic targets including AKT1, MMP9, ESR1, XDH, and CYP1B1. Network pharmacology and molecular docking analyses identified that PVL's anti-cancer mechanisms involve dysregulated genes associated with cell adhesion, extracellular matrix organization, inflammatory response, angiogenesis, and HIF-1 signaling. Notably, morin was experimentally validated as a key bioactive component capable of reducing mRNA expression of multiple core targets in a dose-dependent manner. The polypharmacological profile of PVL suggests its multi-component, multi-target therapeutic approach offers a promising strategy for NSCLC treatment, consistent with prior observations of PVL cytotoxicity against gastric, breast, and thyroid cancer cells.

title: Resveratrol’s Pro-Apoptotic Effects in Cancer Are Mediated Through the Interaction and Oligomerization of the Mitochondrial VDAC1
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals that resveratrol, a polyphenolic compound found in red wine and other dietary sources, exerts pro-apoptotic effects in cancer cells primarily through direct interaction with the mitochondrial protein VDAC1 (voltage-dependent anion channel 1). Resveratrol increases VDAC1 expression and promotes its oligomerization on the outer mitochondrial membrane, leading to elevated intracellular calcium levels and enhanced reactive oxygen species (ROS) production. The compound simultaneously triggers apoptosis by downregulating anti-apoptotic proteins (Bcl-2, Bcl-xL, survivin, XIAP) while upregulating pro-apoptotic factors (Bax, Bak, PUMA, Noxa, Bim), and detaches hexokinase I from VDAC1, further sensitizing cells to death. Notably, a resveratrol analog (TSG) that binds VDAC1 but does not induce its overexpression failed to trigger apoptosis, demonstrating that VDAC1 upregulation is critical for the compound's anti-cancer activity. These findings position resveratrol as a promising therapeutic agent for cancer treatment with potential advantages over conventional approaches due to its additional antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties.

title: Unlocking the Pharmacological Potential of Myricetin Against Various Pathogenesis
summary:
# Summary

Myricetin, a flavonoid polyphenol, exerts broad pharmacological effects through multiple complementary mechanisms relevant to endometriosis and related inflammatory conditions. The compound powerfully reduces inflammation and oxidative stress by suppressing key signaling pathways including NF-κB activation, STAT1 phosphorylation, and TLR2/4-MyD88 expression, while simultaneously downregulating pro-inflammatory mediators such as TNF-α, IL-6, COX-2, and iNOS. Mechanistically, myricetin enhances antioxidant defenses by elevating Nrf2 expression and increasing glutathione, catalase, and superoxide dismutase activity, while reducing reactive oxygen species and lipid peroxidation products. Beyond inflammation, myricetin demonstrates therapeutic potential against metabolic disorders (obesity, diabetes mellitus), tissue degeneration (arthritis, osteoporosis), liver disease, and cancer through ferroptosis activation and tumor growth inhibition. The compound also maintains tissue architecture and exhibits antimicrobial properties by preventing biofilm formation, suggesting broad applicability in treating pathologies characterized by excessive inflammation and oxidative damage, including endometriosis.

title: In Vivo Insights into the Role of Astragaloside IV in Preventing and Treating Civilization Diseases: A Comprehensive Review
summary: Astragaloside IV (ASIV), a triterpenoid saponin derived from Astragalus species, demonstrates potent anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, antitumor, and neuroprotective properties with therapeutic applications across multiple disease categories. The compound exerts its anti-cancer effects by promoting apoptosis and autophagy while inhibiting tumor growth and metastasis across breast, lung, colon, cervical, and liver cancers, and enhances the efficacy of chemotherapeutic agents like cisplatin and gefitinib. ASIV modulates inflammation through multiple mechanisms including suppression of the TLR4/NF-κB and JAK/STAT signaling pathways, reduction of pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6, IL-1β, IL-18), and regulation of macrophage polarization from pro-inflammatory M1 to anti-inflammatory M2 phenotypes. In endometriosis models, ASIV suppresses disease-induced inflammatory lesions by attenuating TLR4/NF-κB signaling and reducing key inflammatory mediators including TNF-α, Ccl-2, IL-1β, and IL-6. The compound's active metabolite, cycloastragenol, activates telomerase to lengthen telomeres while providing additional neuroprotective, cardioprotective, and hepatoprotective effects. Despite its therapeutic potential, ASIV exhibits limited oral bioavailability in vivo, though its structural analog astragalosidic acid demonstrates superior bioavailability and intestinal absorption.

title: Investigation of the Neurotoxicity Mechanisms of Ni2+ in Rat Neocortical Neurons Through Transcriptome Analysis
summary:
# Summary

This transcriptome study reveals that nickel ion (Ni2+) toxicity in rat neocortical neurons operates through two concentration-dependent mechanisms. At low concentrations, Ni2+ rapidly blocks T-type calcium channels and NMDA receptors, suppressing neuronal network excitability and affecting axon initial segment (AIS) structure without inducing cell death. At higher concentrations (≥200 μM), Ni2+ activates the HIF-1α pathway, upregulating glycolytic genes (Hk2 and Ldha) while simultaneously downregulating critical structural proteins (Cd9 and Nfasc), leading to axonal myelin loss and cytotoxicity. The elevated intracellular calcium from HIF-1α activation further triggers calpain-mediated cleavage of Ankyrin-G, destabilizing the AIS and reducing action potential amplitude. Notably, glycolysis upregulation paradoxically reduces ATP production by bypassing mitochondrial energy pathways, exacerbating cellular energy depletion. These findings suggest that nickel-based implants can accumulate in surrounding tissues and cause neurological deficits through dual neurotoxic and cytotoxic pathways, with AIS properties being more sensitive indicators of Ni2+ toxicity than cell viability measures.

title: Systematic Review of the Antitumor Activities and Mechanisms of Scorpion Venom on Human Breast Cancer Cells Lines (In Vitro Study)
summary: This systematic review examines scorpion venom and its bioactive protein components as potential anticancer agents against human breast cancer cell lines through multiple molecular mechanisms. Crude scorpion venom demonstrates broad-spectrum cytotoxicity across cancer cell lines including MCF-7 and MDA-MB-231, primarily by inducing apoptosis via the intrinsic pathway through upregulation of pro-apoptotic proteins (Bax and Caspase-3) and downregulation of anti-apoptotic Bcl-2, while simultaneously inducing DNA fragmentation and cell cycle arrest. Specific bioactive proteins exhibit selective anticancer effects: chlorotoxin inhibits cell migration and invasion by targeting the ERα/VASP signaling pathway and reducing matrix metalloproteinase-2 expression, while neopladines trigger extrinsic apoptosis through FasL upregulation in HER2-positive cells. Beyond breast cancer, scorpion venom components show therapeutic potential against glioma, colorectal cancer, and leukemia. The venom modulates oxidative stress markers and suppresses pro-tumorigenic signaling by downregulating IL-6 and STAT3, establishing scorpion venom constituents as promising multitarget anticancer agents warranting further preclinical and clinical investigation.

title: Sulindac exhibits anti-proliferative and anti-invasive effects and enhances the sensitivity to paclitaxel in ovarian cancer
summary:
# Summary

Sulindac, a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug, demonstrates potent anti-cancer activity against ovarian cancer through inhibition of Cox-2 and NF-κB signaling pathways, leading to reduced cell proliferation, enhanced apoptosis, and decreased invasive capacity in ovarian cancer cell lines. The drug induces cellular stress by generating reactive oxygen species and upregulating endoplasmic reticulum stress markers (BiP, ATF-4, PDI), while simultaneously causing G1 phase cell cycle arrest and reducing mitochondrial membrane potential. In vivo studies confirm these findings, with sulindac treatment producing a 73.7% reduction in tumor volume and significantly decreasing inflammatory cytokine levels including IL-10, interferon-γ, and multiple chemokines. Notably, sulindac synergizes with paclitaxel to overcome chemoresistance, with N-acetylcysteine partially reversing these effects, suggesting oxidative stress is central to the drug's mechanism. These findings support sulindac's potential as an anti-inflammatory therapeutic agent targeting the chronic inflammation-driven carcinogenesis pathway in ovarian cancer.

title: Magnesium sulfate in oxidative stress-associated pathologies: clinical, cellular, and molecular perspectives
summary:
# Summary

Magnesium sulfate (MgSO₄) serves as a multi-targeted therapeutic agent for oxidative stress-associated pathologies, particularly preeclampsia and eclampsia, where it functions as the gold standard treatment by preventing seizures and mitigating oxidative damage. The compound exerts its effects through multiple interconnected mechanisms, including blocking NMDA receptors to reduce neuronal excitability, inhibiting L-type calcium channels to prevent excessive calcium influx, and suppressing the NF-κB inflammatory pathway while stimulating nitric oxide production for vasodilation. At the cellular level, MgSO₄ reduces lipid peroxidation, restores calcium-ATPase activity critical for maintaining ion homeostasis, and protects cellular membranes including syncytiotrophoblast plasma membranes from oxidative injury. Beyond obstetrics, MgSO₄ demonstrates broader clinical utility as a bronchodilator in severe asthma, a cardiac arrhythmia treatment, and a tocolytic agent in preterm birth management, with evidence from experimental models showing that treatment reduces eclampsia risk by approximately fifty percent and restores vascular function. These findings underscore magnesium's essential role as a natural calcium antagonist and antioxidant with profound implications for treating conditions characterized by vascular dysfunction, excessive calcium signaling, and oxidative stress.

title: Effect of Aegle marmelos Extract-Phospholipid Complexes in Dextran Sulfate Sodium-Induced Ulcerative Colitis in Rats
summary:
# Summary

This study evaluated Aegle marmelos fruit extract formulated as a phospholipid complex (AMEP) for treating dextran sulfate sodium-induced ulcerative colitis in rats. The A. marmelos fruit contains multiple bioactive polyphenols including gallic acid, quercetin, ellagic acid, and the marker compound marmelosin, which possess antioxidant and immunomodulatory properties. AMEP treatment significantly reduced hallmark inflammatory and oxidative markers in colitic rats, including nitric oxide, malondialdehyde, TNF-α, and IL-6 levels, while enhancing antioxidant superoxide dismutase activity and preventing disease-associated weight loss and colon shortening. The phospholipid formulation substantially improved the oral bioavailability of poorly absorbed polyphenols, achieving therapeutic efficacy comparable to the standard drug mesalamine, with marmelosin likely exerting its anti-inflammatory effects through inhibition of the PI3K/Akt/NF-κB pathway and regulation of the Nrf2/ARE/HO-1 antioxidant response pathway.

title: The Therapeutic Potential of EGCG and Pro-EGCG in Mitigating Ovarian Hyperstimulation Syndrome: Unraveling the Modulatory Mechanism through the VEGF Pathway
summary:
# Summary

Vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) is central to ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome (OHSS) pathogenesis, where it promotes angiogenesis and vascular permeability in ovarian tissues. This study demonstrates that epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG) and its derivative Pro-EGCG effectively treat OHSS by suppressing VEGF expression through dual mechanisms: EGCG directly interacts with the TGF-β type II receptor to inhibit the TGF-β/SMAD signaling pathway, and simultaneously activates the 67-kDa laminin receptor to trigger PKA-CREB pathway signaling, both converging on VEGF downregulation. Notably, both compounds also reduce VEGFR-2 expression and inhibit granulosa cell proliferation while inducing apoptosis, resulting in decreased vascular permeability and amelioration of OHSS symptoms in experimental models. Pro-EGCG showed superior anti-proliferative effects compared to EGCG, suggesting potential advantages for clinical development. These findings represent the first demonstration of polyphenol-based therapeutics for OHSS and provide mechanistic insight into how natural compounds can modulate angiogenic pathways relevant to reproductive pathologies.

title: Cervicovaginal microbiome alters transcriptomic and epigenomic signatures across cervicovaginal epithelial barriers
summary:
# Summary

Lactobacillus crispatus dominates healthy cervicovaginal microbiomes and exerts protective effects through metabolic and epigenomic mechanisms, whereas pathogenic bacteria like Gardnerella vaginalis trigger pro-inflammatory responses and epithelial damage. Lactic acid produced by Lactobacillus species maintains vaginal acidity and exhibits antimicrobial properties while enhancing epithelial barrier integrity, whereas G. vaginalis culture supernatants robustly upregulate pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-8, IL-6, TNFα) and chemokines (CCL20) along with antimicrobial peptides, ultimately increasing epithelial cell death. Importantly, L. crispatus modulates cervicovaginal epithelial cell transcription and chromatin accessibility through epigenomic modifications, reducing expression of inflammatory mediators while upregulating protective factors, whereas G. vaginalis induces dysregulated innate immune responses. Gene-disease enrichment analysis revealed that transcription factors altered by microbiota composition associate with endometriosis, infertility, and neoplasms, suggesting microbiome-driven epigenetic changes influence reproductive health outcomes. These findings demonstrate that optimal Lactobacillus-dominated microbiomes protect reproductive health through both antimicrobial and immunomodulatory mechanisms, while lactobacilli-deficient microbiomes linked to infertility, preterm birth, and sexually transmitted infections create a dysregulated inflammatory state through altered epithelial transcriptomics and epigenomics.

title: Zinc valproic acid complex promotes osteoblast differentiation and exhibits anti-osteoporotic potential
summary:
# Summary

Zinc valproic acid (Z-VA) promotes osteoblast differentiation and exhibits anti-osteoporotic effects through multiple molecular mechanisms. At a concentration of 25 µM, Z-VA upregulates key osteogenic transcription factors including Runx2 and enhances alkaline phosphatase activity, type I collagen expression, and calcium deposition in both cellular and animal models. In zebrafish and rat osteoporosis models, Z-VA increased bone mineral content and density while simultaneously suppressing osteoclastic activity by reducing tartrate-resistant acid phosphatase and inhibiting hydroxyproline release. The compound's anabolic effects involve upregulation of bone matrix proteins (osteocalcin, osteonectin, collagen2α, osteopontin) and activation of mitogen-activated protein kinase signaling, while its anti-resorptive properties include downregulation of matrix metalloproteinase 3. These findings suggest Z-VA represents a promising therapeutic candidate for preventing and treating osteoporosis through coordinated stimulation of bone formation and inhibition of bone resorption.

title: A case report of acute interstitial nephritis caused by cotton phenol
summary:
# Summary

This case report documents a patient who developed granulomatous acute interstitial nephritis (AIN) following occupational exposure to cotton phenol, a polyphenolic compound extracted from cotton plant tissues and used extensively in antifertility and antitumor drug research. Cotton phenol causes renal toxicity through multiple mechanisms, including inhibition of mitochondrial electron transport chain enzymes, direct damage to mitochondrial membranes, and reduction of mitochondrial membrane potential, ultimately leading to mitochondrial dysfunction and impaired cellular energy metabolism. The patient presented with characteristic AIN symptoms including oliguria, hematuria, nausea, and vomiting, with diagnosis confirmed by renal biopsy; importantly, the patient achieved rapid restoration of renal function following treatment with glucocorticoids combined with removal of the causative exposure and systemic supportive therapy. Beyond its nephrotoxic effects, cotton phenol has demonstrated activity against various cancers and gynecological conditions including endometriosis, while also suppressing immune function through reduction of leukocytes and lymphocytes. This case highlights the occupational health risks associated with cotton phenol exposure and underscores the importance of identifying environmental toxins in the differential diagnosis of AIN.

title: Neferine Ameliorates Slow-Transmitting Constipation by Inducing PINK1/Parkin-Mediated Mitophagy in Protective Enteric Glial Cells
summary:
# Summary

Neferine, a polyphenolic compound, ameliorates slow transit constipation by activating PINK1/Parkin-mediated mitophagy in enteric glial cells, which are critical components of the enteric nervous system essential for maintaining normal gastrointestinal motility. Treatment with neferine significantly improves constipation symptoms by increasing intestinal propulsion rate, fecal excretion, and fecal water content while reducing mucosal epithelial cell loss, inflammatory infiltration, and fibrous proliferation in a dose-dependent manner comparable to the clinical standard mosapride. At the molecular level, neferine reduces oxidative stress markers (ROS and MDA) while enhancing antioxidant defenses (SOD) and promoting neurotrophic factor expression (GDNF and NGF) through direct binding to PINK1 at phenylalanine 151, thereby triggering selective mitochondrial autophagy and preventing enteric glial cell death. Genetic silencing of PINK1 or inhibition of mitophagy reverses neferine's protective effects on glial cell viability and neurotrophic signaling, confirming that the PINK1/Parkin pathway is essential for the compound's therapeutic mechanism. These findings establish neferine as a promising natural therapeutic agent for constipation that functions by preserving enteric glial cell populations through mitochondrial quality control mechanisms.

title: Protective Effects of Octyl Gallate Against Deoxynivalenol-Induced
summary:
# Summary: Protective Effects of Octyl Gallate Against Deoxynivalenol-Induced Endometriosis

Deoxynivalenol (DON), a mycotoxin commonly found in cereals, induces severe intestinal inflammation and endometriosis-like pathology by upregulating pro-inflammatory cytokines including TNF-α, IL-6, IL-1β, and chemokines while downregulating tight junction proteins ZO-1 and claudin-1. Octyl gallate (OG), a polyphenolic food additive, exerts protective effects through multiple mechanisms including direct binding to the NLRP3 inflammasome LRR domain and inhibition of the NF-κB signaling pathway. Co-exposure to DON and OG (DON-OG) significantly reversed DON-induced pathology by downregulating pro-inflammatory mediators, restoring tight junction protein expression, and upregulating the anti-inflammatory cytokine IL-10. Mechanistically, OG ameliorates DON toxicity by modulating the mTOR signaling pathway and restoring arginine biosynthesis, as demonstrated through proteomic and metabolomic analyses. These findings suggest octyl gallate represents a promising natural compound for mitigating DON-induced intestinal inflammation and endometriosis through coordinated suppression of inflammatory signaling and restoration of intestinal barrier integrity.

title: Treatment of Rheumatoid Arthritis Based on the Inherent Bioactivity of Black Phosphorus Nanosheets
summary:
# Summary

Black phosphorus nanosheets (BPNSs) demonstrate therapeutic potential for rheumatoid arthritis through multiple complementary mechanisms. BPNSs function as potent antioxidants by scavenging reactive oxygen species including hydroxyl radicals, hydrogen peroxide, and superoxide anions, while their degradation products (phosphate radicals) increase intracellular ATP levels and activate the mTOR pathway, thereby suppressing autophagy at the AMPK-mTOR axis. In rheumatoid arthritis synovial fibroblasts, BPNSs treatment reduces apoptosis through elevated BCL-2/BAX ratios while simultaneously promoting macrophage transition from pro-inflammatory M1 to anti-inflammatory M2 phenotypes and downregulating senescence-associated secretory phenotype genes. Additionally, BPNSs suppress inflammation by reducing TNF-α and IL-1β expression while upregulating anti-inflammatory markers like IL-10 and ARG1, and notably exhibit minimal toxicity to normal fibroblasts even at high concentrations—advantages over conventional RA drugs such as methotrexate, chloroquine, and hydroxychloroquine that significantly decrease fibroblast viability. In vivo studies confirm that BPNSs administration alleviates key pathological features of rheumatoid arthritis in mice through coordinated suppression of pro-inflammatory, pro-apoptotic, and pro-autophagic gene programs alongside upregulation of oxidative phosphorylation pathways.

title: Endometriosis-like lesions induced by phthalates: new phytotherapic applications to complement traditional cares: a PNRR 2023 project
summary: Endometriosis is an estrogen-dependent, inflammatory disease characterized by pelvic pain, infertility, and systemic metabolic dysfunction affecting the liver and adipose tissue. The disease is associated with environmental exposure to phthalates, particularly bis(2-ethylhexyl) phthalate (DEHP), which acts as an endocrine disruptor and promotes inflammation and oxidative stress. Current pharmaceutical treatments including NSAIDs, oral contraceptives, and progestins suppress estrogen levels and tissue proliferation, but complementary phytotherapeutic approaches using polyphenolic compounds offer promising alternatives. Specific plant-derived compounds such as hydroxytyrosol from olive extract, oleuropein, naringenin, hesperidin, and β-sitosterol demonstrate multi-targeted anti-endometriotic effects by reducing inflammatory mediators (IL-1β, TNF-α, IL-6), suppressing angiogenesis and endometrial cell proliferation, inducing apoptosis, and restoring tissue architecture. Hydroxytyrosol and oleuropein are particularly notable for decreasing cyst diameter and fibrosis while selectively modulating estrogen receptor activity and improving pregnancy rates. These phytotherapeutic compounds represent valuable adjunctive treatments that address the oxidative stress, inflammation, and pathological angiogenesis underlying endometriosis while potentially counteracting phthalate-induced endometrial damage.

title: Engeletin Targets Mitochondrial Dysfunction to Attenuate Oxidative Stress and Experimental Colitis in Intestinal Epithelial Cells Through AMPK/SIRT1/PGC-1α Signaling
summary: Engeletin, a dihydroflavonoid from the traditional Chinese medicinal herb Smilax glabra, ameliorates dextran sulfate sodium-induced colitis in mice by targeting mitochondrial dysfunction through activation of the AMPK/SIRT1/PGC-1α signaling axis. This pathway restores mitochondrial biogenesis and energy metabolism, increasing ATP production, mtDNA levels, and electron transport chain complex I and IV activity while simultaneously reducing oxidative stress markers and reactive oxygen species. Engeletin suppresses inflammatory signaling by inhibiting NF-κB activation and p65 nuclear translocation while activating the Nrf2 antioxidant pathway, resulting in increased expression of antioxidant enzymes including SOD, catalase, and glutathione. The compound protects intestinal epithelial cell integrity by upregulating tight junction proteins ZO-1 and claudin-1, while preventing apoptosis through modulation of pro- and anti-apoptotic proteins (Bax/Bcl-2 and c-caspase3). Additionally, engeletin suppresses the production of key pro-inflammatory cytokines including TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6, and IL-17A. Pharmacological inhibition of PGC-1α abolished engeletin's mitochondrial protective and anti-apoptotic effects, confirming that mitochondrial biogenesis is essential for the compound's therapeutic benefits in experimental colitis.

title: Natural Compounds in Cancer Therapy: Revealing the Role of Flavonoids in Renal Cell Carcinoma Treatment
summary:
# Summary

This review systematically examines flavonoids, a class of natural polyphenolic compounds, as therapeutic agents for renal cell carcinoma (RCC), analyzing 26 natural flavonoids across six subclasses and their mechanisms of action. Flavonoids exert anticancer effects through multiple pathways, including inhibition of tumor cell proliferation, induction of apoptosis, autophagy, and ferroptosis, while simultaneously regulating critical signaling cascades such as JAK/STAT3 and PI3K/Akt/mTOR. Specific flavonoids demonstrate distinct therapeutic potential: luteolin downregulates p-Akt and p-STAT3 while upregulating heme oxygenase-1 to promote ferroptosis, scutellarin suppresses cell invasion by reducing matrix metalloproteinases and activates the tumor suppressor PTEN, and nobiletin blocks multiple pathways including NF-κB and Wnt/β-catenin while enhancing sensitivity to CDK4/6 inhibitors. Additional flavonoids such as apigenin, diosmetin, hispidulin, jaceosidin, and eupafolin induce cell cycle arrest or enhance apoptotic pathways through distinct molecular mechanisms involving mitochondrial disruption and protein stabilization. These findings position flavonoids as promising natural compounds that may complement or enhance conventional RCC therapies including tyrosine kinase inhibitors and immunotherapies.

title: Recent Trends in the Application of Cellulose-Based Hemostatic and Wound Healing Dressings
summary:
# Summary

This review examines cellulose-based hemostatic and wound healing dressings, which leverage natural polysaccharides' inherent absorbency and biocompatibility to actively promote clotting and tissue regeneration beyond traditional passive absorption. Advanced cellulose formulations, including carboxymethylcellulose derivatives and bacterial cellulose, enhance hemostatic efficacy by serving as nucleation sites for platelet aggregation and accelerating the coagulation cascade through mechanisms mimicking natural hemostasis. Composite systems combining cellulose with synergistic materials such as chitosan, alginate, kaolin, and zeolites further improve clot formation while providing antimicrobial protection and moisture retention for optimal wound healing. Bioengineered cellulose dressings incorporating nanoparticles, growth factor gradients, and antimicrobial agents like silver nanoparticles create intelligent wound microenvironments that stimulate fibroblast activation, collagen deposition, and angiogenesis during the proliferation phase. Hydrogel-based cellulose formulations, particularly polyvinyl alcohol-cellulose and collagen-enriched variants, demonstrate superior performance in accelerating wound closure and tissue regeneration. These innovations represent a paradigm shift from passive to active wound management, where engineered cellulose platforms actively orchestrate hemostasis, immune regulation, and tissue reconstruction.

title: The antitumor effects and apoptotic mechanism of 20(S)-Protopanaxadiol in acute myeloid leukemia
summary:
# Summary

20(S)-Protopanaxadiol (20(S)-PPD), a ginsenoside from Panax ginseng, demonstrates potent antitumor activity against acute myeloid leukemia (AML) by inhibiting cell proliferation and inducing apoptosis through multiple interconnected mechanisms. The compound simultaneously inhibits the pro-survival PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway while activating the pro-apoptotic PERK/ATF4/CHOP endoplasmic reticulum stress pathway, thereby triggering intrinsic apoptosis dependent on Bax and Bak. Notably, 20(S)-PPD reduces c-Myc expression by destabilizing c-Myc mRNA rather than affecting protein stability, and c-Myc downregulation mediates the compound's pro-apoptotic effects in AML cells. The combination of 20(S)-PPD with ABT-199, a Bcl-2 inhibitor, exhibits synergistic anti-tumor activity in both treatment-sensitive and cytarabine-resistant AML cells. Importantly, 20(S)-PPD selectively induces apoptosis in AML cells while sparing normal peripheral blood mononuclear cells, and its efficacy has been confirmed in primary patient samples, supporting its therapeutic potential for AML treatment.

title: The neurobiology of plant-based therapeutics in women's reproductive health: mechanisms, efficacy, and clinical translation
summary:
# Summary

This article examines how plant-derived bioactive compounds, particularly polyphenols and phytoestrogens, modulate the neuroendocrine-reproductive axis to address gynecological disorders including endometriosis, polycystic ovary syndrome, and premenstrual syndrome. Key mechanisms involve plant compounds regulating critical signaling pathways: resveratrol and curcumin modulate the PI3K/Akt/mTOR pathway to reduce aberrant cell proliferation, while traditional Chinese medicine compounds like saikosaponins and tanshinone regulate neurotransmitter balance and inhibit NF-κB-mediated inflammation that disrupts hypothalamic GnRH secretion. Plant flavonoids also influence kisspeptin expression, enhancing the upstream regulation of gonadotropin-releasing hormone pulsatility through the kisspeptin-GPR54 system. Additionally, plant ingredients such as ginkgolide B and ginsenoside Rg1 enhance BDNF expression to support neuroplasticity and emotional regulation, directly addressing the neurological comorbidities—cognitive decline, anxiety, depression, and central sensitization—that frequently accompany reproductive disorders. The article integrates evidence that endometriosis-associated central sensitization and chronic pelvic pain arise from neuroinflammatory cascades involving IL-1β, IL-6, and TNF-α, which plant polyphenols help ameliorate, positioning botanical therapeutics as multi-target agents bridging reproductive and neurological health.

title: Rhein Alleviates Cisplatin-Induced Acute Kidney Injury via Downregulation of NOX4-COX2/PGFS Signaling Pathway
summary: This study demonstrates that rhein, an anthraquinone compound from traditional Chinese medicine, protects against cisplatin-induced acute kidney injury by inhibiting the NOX4-NFκB-COX2/PGFS signaling pathway. Cisplatin, a widely used chemotherapeutic agent, causes nephrotoxicity through mechanisms involving oxidative stress, apoptosis, inflammation, and abnormal autophagy, with approximately 30% of treated patients developing renal dysfunction. In both cellular and animal models, rhein reduced oxidative stress and reactive oxygen species production while suppressing tubular apoptosis and inflammatory responses, as evidenced by decreased plasma creatinine and reduced expression of kidney injury markers KIM-1 and NGAL. Molecular docking studies confirmed rhein's robust binding interactions with NOX4, COX2, and PGFS, establishing direct mechanistic targets within the identified pathway. Notably, rhein preserved cisplatin's tumor-suppressive effects against Lewis lung carcinoma and A549 cells, indicating that nephroprotection does not compromise anticancer efficacy. These findings suggest rhein represents a promising natural compound to mitigate chemotherapy-associated kidney injury while maintaining therapeutic antitumor activity.

title: Inhibitory effect of Fisetin against the aggregation process of SOD1 E100K mutant: computer-based drug design as a potential therapeutic for ALS disease
summary:
# Summary

SOD1 mutations, particularly the E100K variant frequently observed in familial ALS, promote protein aggregation and neurodegeneration through alterations in electrostatic charge, hydrogen bonding, and structural stability of the SOD1 β-barrel domain. The E100K substitution reduces the protein's net negative charge and destabilizes loop IV, increasing aggregation propensity that correlates with disease severity. This study employed computational drug design to identify fisetin, a polyphenolic flavonoid, as a potential therapeutic agent that inhibits SOD1 aggregation by enhancing structural stability and reducing β-sheet formation. Fisetin joins other flavonoids such as baicalein, quercetin, and kaempferol that share anti-aggregation properties toward SOD1, offering a natural compound-based approach to complement existing ALS treatments like riluzole and edaravone. The findings suggest that targeting the aggregation pathways of mutant SOD1 through flavonoid inhibitors represents a viable therapeutic strategy for familial ALS.

title: Potential Therapeutic Efficacy of Ferulic Acid and Its Derivatives in the Management of Cancers: A Comprehensive Analysis With Mechanistic Insight
summary:
# Summary

Ferulic acid (FA), a naturally occurring phenolic compound found abundantly in plant-based foods including grains, fruits, and the Ferula foetida plant, demonstrates promising anticancer efficacy through multiple interconnected molecular mechanisms. FA exerts its antiproliferative effects on cancer cells primarily by inducing oxidative stress, triggering cell cycle arrest through upregulation of p53 and downregulation of cyclin-dependent kinases (CDK2, CDK4, CDK6), and promoting apoptosis via activation of caspase cascades and modulation of anti-apoptotic proteins (downregulating Bcl-2 and Mcl-1 while upregulating Bax). Additionally, FA suppresses cancer cell invasion and migration while regulating critical signaling pathways including PI3K/AKT, p38/MAPK/ERK, AKT/mTOR, NF-κB, and JAK2/STAT6, demonstrating dose-dependent inhibition of phosphorylation events central to cancer progression. Beyond its direct anticancer activity, FA displays antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antibacterial properties, and notably reduces adverse effects of chemotherapy and radiotherapy while being bioavailable through gastrointestinal absorption and liver metabolism, making it a promising natural therapeutic candidate for cancer management.

title: Gallic acid suppresses esophageal squamous cell carcinoma progression and enhances cisplatin chemosensitivity through IL-6/STAT3/Notch pathway
summary: Gallic acid, a natural plant polyphenol, suppresses esophageal squamous cell carcinoma (ESCC) progression by inhibiting the IL-6/STAT3/Notch signaling pathway. The study demonstrates that interleukin-6 activates this cascade through STAT3 phosphorylation, which subsequently activates Notch signaling to promote cancer cell proliferation, invasion, and migration. Gallic acid reduces phosphorylated STAT3 and downstream Notch1 and HES1 protein levels, thereby reversing IL-6-induced increases in ESCC cell viability. Additionally, gallic acid enhances sensitivity to cisplatin chemotherapy by promoting oxidative stress and apoptosis in cancer cells, with STAT3 and Notch inhibitors further amplifying these effects. These findings establish gallic acid as a promising therapeutic agent that synergizes with platinum-based chemotherapy by disrupting a key pro-tumoral signaling axis.

title: Anti-Inflammatory Activity of Thymol and Thymol-Rich Essential Oils: Mechanisms, Applications, and Recent Findings
summary:
# Summary

Thymol, a monoterpenoid compound found abundantly in aromatic plants such as Thymus vulgaris and Origanum vulgare, exhibits potent anti-inflammatory activity through multiple molecular mechanisms. The compound reduces pro-inflammatory cytokine production and inhibits key signaling pathways including NF-κB, MAPK, and JAK/STAT, as well as downstream inflammatory mediators such as COX-2, nitric oxide, and prostaglandins. Beyond its anti-inflammatory effects, thymol demonstrates antimicrobial and antioxidant properties, though bioavailability limitations challenge its clinical application. Thymol-rich essential oils show superior inhibitory effects against lipoxygenase and COX-2 compared to their linalool-rich counterparts, while carvacrol, an isomeric compound found in related plants, exhibits even greater COX-2 inhibition capacity. These findings support the therapeutic potential of thymol and thymol-rich natural compounds for addressing chronic inflammatory conditions through suppression of pro-inflammatory signaling cascades.

title: In Silico Evaluation of Bioactive
summary:
# Summary

This in silico study evaluated bioactive polyphenols as potential inhibitors of NDM-1 metallo-β-lactamase, an enzyme conferring broad-spectrum antibiotic resistance in pathogenic bacteria like Escherichia coli and Klebsiella pneumoniae that cause traveler's diarrhea and nosocomial infections. Three natural polyphenolic compounds—homogentisic acid, caffeic acid, and protocatechuic acid—were identified as lead molecules that bind potently to NDM-1's catalytic active site through hydrogen bonding with key residues (GLN 123 and ASN 220) and metal coordination with zinc ions essential for enzyme activity. These interactions effectively inhibit NDM-1's ability to hydrolyze and inactivate critical β-lactam antibiotics including penicillins, cephalosporins, and carbapenems. The study also identified additional natural compounds such as chlorogenic acid and hesperidin as NDM-1 binders, while establishing Cucumis anguria leaf extract as a potent antibacterial agent against resistant pathogens. These findings suggest that plant-derived polyphenols represent a promising complementary strategy to overcome antibiotic resistance, potentially supporting conventional treatments such as fluoroquinolones, rifaximin, and azithromycin for resistant infections.

title: Investigating the Role and Underlying Mechanisms of 18β-Glycyrrhetinic Acid in the Therapy of Ulcerative Colitis Through Modulation of the PPAR-γ/NF-κB Signaling Pathway
summary:
# Summary

18β-Glycyrrhetinic acid (18β-GA), a triterpenoid saponin derived from Glycyrrhizae uralensis, effectively treats ulcerative colitis by activating the PPAR-γ/NF-κB signaling pathway. In a dextran sodium sulfate-induced colitis model, 18β-GA treatment reduced disease severity markers including weight loss, disease activity index, and colonic damage while promoting mucosal repair and restoring intestinal barrier integrity. The compound's therapeutic mechanism involves dual action: activation of PPAR-γ suppresses NF-κB signaling to reduce inflammatory responses, while simultaneously promoting favorable intestinal microbiota composition and inhibiting pathogenic bacteria proliferation. 18β-GA demonstrated comparable efficacy to the standard treatment 5-ASA and exhibited anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and immunomodulatory properties with good safety profile. These findings suggest that 18β-GA represents a promising natural compound for ulcerative colitis therapy through restoration of gut homeostasis and suppression of hyperactive inflammatory pathways.

title: Network pharmacology and UPLC/MS/MS metabolic profiling unveil the anti-inflammatory potential of Trifolium alexandrinum
summary: This study reveals that Trifolium alexandrinum exhibits potent anti-inflammatory properties through multiple bioactive compounds, with adenosine and tryptophan emerging as the top-scored constituents in network pharmacology analysis. The butanol fraction of the extract significantly suppresses pro-inflammatory cytokines including TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-1β, likely by interrupting the LPS-TLR4 signaling cascade that normally drives inflammatory cytokine production and oxidative stress. Adenosine functions as a strong anti-inflammatory agent by regulating immune cell activation and dampening the arachidonic acid metabolism pathway, a pivotal inflammatory route, while tryptophan similarly reduces inflammatory markers with efficacy comparable to the synthetic drug piroxicam. Beyond inflammation, T. alexandrinum demonstrates antioxidant, antimicrobial, hepatoprotective, and estrogenic activities, with its flavonoid components hesperetin and quercetin additionally improving metabolic parameters including insulin levels, serum glucose, and lipid metabolism. The identification of 57 bioactive compounds—including isoflavones, flavones, and purine nucleosides—provides a chemical basis for the plant's traditional use in treating inflammatory and metabolic conditions, positioning it as a promising natural therapeutic candidate.

title: Homeopathy for Heteropathy: FSS and Its Components for the Treatment of Alzheimer’s Disease and Endometriosis
summary: Foshou San (FSS), a traditional Chinese medicine formula composed of Angelica sinensis and Ligusticum chuanxiong, demonstrates significant therapeutic efficacy for both Alzheimer's disease and endometriosis through distinct but complementary mechanisms. For Alzheimer's disease, FSS and its polyphenolic components—including ferulic acid, ligustrazine, and phthalides—ameliorate cognitive impairment by inhibiting amyloid-beta aggregation, preventing neurodegeneration, and reducing neuroinflammation and oxidative stress through pathways involving Nrf2-mediated antioxidant upregulation. For endometriosis, FSS exerts therapeutic effects by reducing pathological angiogenesis and suppressing ectopic lesion invasion and metastasis, with ferulic acid and ligustrazine playing key roles in inhibiting matrix metalloproteinase activity. The formula's two primary herbal components synergistically promote blood circulation and remove blood stasis, while FSS modulates the gut-liver-brain axis in Alzheimer's models, suggesting additional neuroprotective benefits through microbiota-mediated mechanisms. Multiple bioactive constituents including senkyunolide, chlorogenic acid, caffeic acid, and Angelica sinensis polysaccharides contribute additive anti-inflammatory and cytoprotective effects relevant to both disease conditions, establishing FSS as a multi-target therapeutic agent for these disparate pathologies.

title: Oyster shells water extract ameliorates propylthiouracil-induced goitre in rats via PI3K/AKT/Bcl-2 pathway
summary:
# Summary

Oyster shell water extract (WEE) effectively treats propylthiouracil-induced goitre in rats by restoring thyroid hormone levels and inhibiting the PI3K/AKT/Bcl-2 signaling pathway. The extract, rich in organic acids, amino acids, phenolic compounds, and trace elements including zinc and selenium, significantly increases serum T3, T4, FT3, and FT4 levels while reducing elevated TSH and improving thyroid tissue morphology. High-dose WEE suppresses pathological signaling by inhibiting phosphorylation of PI3K and AKT proteins and downregulating Bcl-2 expression at both protein and mRNA levels, thereby blocking the cascade that drives thyroid tissue hyperplasia and goitre development. Multiple bioactive components of WEE, including organic acids such as 9-decenoic acid and methylmyristic acid, directly bind to PI3K and other pathway targets, providing a mechanistic basis for the therapeutic effect. These findings establish oyster shell extract as a functional food with anti-inflammatory and anti-fibrotic properties that could be developed further for thyroid disorder management.

title: Investigating the correlation between tea intake and glioblastoma: A Mendelian randomization study
summary: This Mendelian randomization study investigated whether tea intake causally influences glioblastoma risk by analyzing genetic variants associated with tea consumption. While observational studies suggest tea consumption (particularly 3-4 cups daily) correlates with reduced glioma risk, the MR analysis using multiple statistical methods found no causal relationship between tea intake and glioblastoma risk. The mechanistic basis for tea's potential protective effects involves polyphenolic compounds, particularly catechins like epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG) from green tea and polyphenols from black tea, which exert antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activities that reduce oxidative stress and DNA damage. These compounds also inhibit cancer cell proliferation and angiogenesis while promoting apoptosis through suppression of antiapoptotic proteins and downregulation of pro-inflammatory cytokines and growth factors. The discrepancy between observed associations and lack of causal evidence suggests that tea intake's epidemiological correlation with reduced glioblastoma risk may reflect reverse causation, confounding factors, or non-causal associations rather than a direct protective effect.

title: Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3): efficacy, safety, and implications in public health
summary:
# Summary

Vitamin D3 (cholecalciferol) functions as a fat-soluble secosteroid with pleiotropic effects mediated primarily through vitamin D receptor signaling. After synthesis in skin or dietary ingestion, cholecalciferol is converted to calcitriol, which activates VDR-mediated pathways to regulate calcium-phosphorus metabolism, bone health, and immune homeostasis. The active form downregulates pro-inflammatory cytokines TNF-α and IL-6 by inhibiting NF-κB and MAPK signaling, while simultaneously enhancing antimicrobial peptides and regulatory T cell differentiation. High-dose vitamin D3 supplementation improves bone mineral density, enhances insulin sensitivity through AMPK activation and mTOR suppression, and reduces systemic inflammation, collectively decreasing fall risk and improving overall quality of life. However, chronic excessive intake poses significant safety concerns, causing hypercalcemia that leads to nausea, muscle weakness, renal dysfunction, nephrolithiasis, and vascular calcification, necessitating careful dose management. The CYP24A1 enzyme provides safety by metabolizing excess vitamin D metabolites, though developmental vitamin D deficiency can induce persistent epigenetic dysregulation with long-term health implications.

title: Palmitoylethanolamide: A Multifunctional Molecule for Neuroprotection, Chronic Pain, and Immune Modulation
summary:
# Summary

Palmitoylethanolamide (PEA) is a multifunctional lipid mediator that exerts therapeutic effects primarily through activation of peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor-alpha (PPAR-α) and modulation of the endocannabinoid system via cannabinoid receptors CB1 and CB2, as well as interactions with TRPV1, GPR55, and GPR119. The ultramicronized formulation of PEA (umPEA) demonstrates superior bioavailability through enhanced gastrointestinal absorption and improved blood-brain barrier penetration, enabling more effective systemic and central nervous system delivery. UmPEA reduces neuroinflammation and protects neuronal cells from oxidative damage by stabilizing mast cells, modulating glial cell activation, and suppressing pro-inflammatory cytokines including TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-6. Additionally, PEA exhibits potent immunomodulatory effects in peripheral tissues, inhibiting mast cell degranulation and reducing the release of inflammatory mediators such as histamine, prostaglandin D2, and leukotrienes, thereby alleviating allergic and inflammatory responses in skin and respiratory tissues. These multi-target mechanisms position PEA as a promising candidate for managing chronic pain, neuroinflammatory conditions, allergic disorders, and immune-mediated pathologies.

title: LPS Regulates Endometrial Immune Homeostasis and Receptivity Through the TLR4/ERK Pathway in Sheep
journal: Animals : an Open Access Journal from MDPI
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that lipopolysaccharide (LPS) from gram-negative bacteria impairs endometrial receptivity and embryo implantation in sheep through activation of the TLR4/ERK signaling pathway. LPS binding to TLR4 initiates both MyD88 and TRIF pathways, ultimately activating downstream NF-κB and MAPK/ERK cascades that promote a pro-inflammatory Th1 shift while suppressing anti-inflammatory Th2 responses, characterized by elevated TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6, and IL-8 alongside reduced IL-4 and IL-10. This immune dysregulation directly disrupts critical implantation factors including ITGB3, ITGB5, VEGF, and LIF, preventing proper trophectoderm adhesion and embryo attachment. Notably, pterostilbene, a natural polyphenol compound, effectively attenuates LPS-induced endometrial damage by downregulating the ERK signaling pathway and restoring expression of implantation-related genes. These findings establish the TLR4/ERK axis as a key mechanism linking bacterial infection to reproductive failure and identify pterostilbene as a potential therapeutic agent for protecting endometrial function in the context of infectious-associated infertility.

title: Targeting Cellular Senescence to Enhance Human Endometrial Stromal Cell Decidualization and Inhibit Their Migration
summary:
# Summary

Cellular senescence in endometrial stromal cells impairs decidualization and promotes endometriosis pathogenesis, making senescent cell clearance a promising therapeutic target. Natural flavonoid senolytics—quercetin, fisetin, and luteolin—along with the senomorphic compound kaempferol and the tyrosine kinase inhibitor dasatinib, effectively eliminated senescent cells and enhanced decidualization in both healthy and endometriosis-derived stromal cells. These compounds suppress AKT and ERK1/2 phosphorylation while upregulating p53 expression, and simultaneously inhibit endometrial stromal cell migration. In rodent models of endometriosis, quercetin treatment significantly reduced lesion size and inflammatory markers while correcting the impaired decidualization characteristic of endometriosis patients. These findings establish senolytic and senomorphic compounds as a novel therapeutic strategy to restore proper decidualization, reduce senescent cell-mediated inflammation, and potentially reverse endometriosis-associated infertility and lesion formation.

title: Alpha-Lipoic Acid in Diabetic Peripheral Neuropathy: Addressing the Challenges and Complexities Surrounding a 70-Year-Old Compound
summary: Alpha-lipoic acid (ALA), an endogenous mitochondrial compound, demonstrates potent therapeutic effects against diabetic peripheral neuropathy through multiple complementary mechanisms. As a universal antioxidant alongside its reduced metabolite dihydrolipoic acid, ALA scavenges free radicals, chelates metals, and regenerates other antioxidants (glutathione, vitamins C and E) while enhancing endogenous antioxidant enzymes and activating the Nrf2 transcription factor. Beyond antioxidant activity, ALA improves glucose metabolism by activating insulin receptor signaling pathways, enhancing glucose uptake, and reducing insulin resistance, thereby mitigating hyperglycemia-induced neuronal damage and advanced glycation end-products. Experimentally, ALA improves nerve blood flow, vascular relaxation, and motor nerve conduction velocity in diabetic models while reducing oxidative stress markers and normalizing glutathione redox status. Clinically, numerous randomized controlled trials support ALA's efficacy in ameliorating neuropathic pain symptoms and preventing diabetic complications, though challenges remain regarding optimal dosing, bioavailability, and standardization of the R-enantiomer in manufacturing. This 70-year-old compound continues to show promise as a multitargeted therapeutic intervention addressing the pathophysiological complexity of diabetes-related complications.

title: Diet and Endometriosis: An Umbrella Review
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis, a chronic inflammatory condition affecting 10-15% of reproductive-age women and associated with infertility and severe pain, can be modulated through dietary interventions. Vegetables and dairy products, particularly cheese and high-fat dairy containing calcium and vitamin D, show protective effects against endometriosis risk, likely by downregulating pro-inflammatory cytokines like TNF-α and interleukin-6 while upregulating growth-inhibitory factors such as transforming growth factor β. Conversely, butter consumption and high caffeine intake (>300 mg/day) increase endometriosis risk, with trans fatty acids promoting inflammation through elevated interleukin-6 and tumor necrosis factor-alpha. Plant-based diets rich in polyphenols, antioxidants, resveratrol, and vitamin C mitigate oxidative stress and inflammation, the central drivers of endometriosis pathogenesis, while Mediterranean diet patterns demonstrate clinical improvement. Notably, probiotics from yogurt and dietary fiber may benefit intestinal microbiota and reduce pelvic pain symptoms, suggesting that comprehensive dietary modification incorporating anti-inflammatory whole foods while limiting processed fats and caffeine represents a promising non-pharmacological strategy for endometriosis management.

title: Molecular Insights into the Nociceptive Modulation by Palmitoylethanolamide and Equisetum arvense Extract: An In Vitro Study Across the Blood–Brain Barrier
summary:
# Summary

This in vitro study demonstrates that palmitoylethanolamide (PEA) and Equisetum arvense extract work synergistically to modulate nociceptive pathways through multiple complementary mechanisms. PEA crosses the blood-brain barrier and exerts analgesic and anti-inflammatory effects primarily by activating peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor alpha (PPARα), which suppresses pro-inflammatory cytokines including TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-1β, while also inhibiting mast cell degranulation and modulating microglial activation. Equisetum arvense contributes antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects through its flavonoid and polyphenolic content, notably suppressing COX-2 expression and inflammatory cytokines. The combination of both compounds demonstrated superior efficacy, increasing cell viability 1.8-fold and reducing oxidative stress while decreasing FAAH and NAAA enzymatic activity—enzymes that normally degrade PEA and related endocannabinoids—thereby elevating endogenous pain-modulatory molecules (AEA and 2-AG) and upregulating CB2 receptor expression. These findings suggest that PEA and Equisetum arvense together represent a promising natural therapeutic approach for managing nociceptive pain through integrated modulation of endocannabinoid signaling, PPAR pathways, and neuroinflammatory responses.

title: 1,25-Dihydroxy vitamin D3 inhibits LPS-mediated inflammatory responses in endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is characterized by chronic inflammation and immune imbalance driven partly by lipopolysaccharide (LPS), which promotes endometrial cell proliferation and migration through NF-κB pathway activation. The active form of vitamin D, 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D3 (1,25(OH)2D3), suppresses these LPS-mediated inflammatory responses by activating vitamin D receptors (VDR) and stabilizing IκBα, thereby blocking NF-κB nuclear translocation and downstream signaling. In cultured endometrial and ectopic stromal cells, 1,25(OH)2D3 potently inhibited LPS-induced proliferation and migration while promoting apoptosis, and significantly reduced production of pro-inflammatory cytokines including TNF-α and IL-6. In a rat endometriosis model, 1,25(OH)2D3 directly inhibited lesion growth and suppressed NF-κB pathway activation. Clinical evidence shows endometriosis patients have lower vitamin D levels, and increasing circulating 25-hydroxyvitamin D can reduce endometriosis risk by 24%, highlighting 1,25(OH)2D3 as a promising VDR-dependent therapeutic target for managing endometriosis through restoration of endometrial homeostasis and immune balance.

title: Phytochemical Screening, Protoscolicidal Activity and Mechanisms of Action of Taraxacum Officinale Extract against Hydatid Cyst Protoscoleces
summary:
# Summary

This study investigated Taraxacum officinale (dandelion) ethanolic extract as a potential treatment for cystic echinococcosis, a parasitic disease caused by Echinococcus granulosus. Phytochemical screening identified multiple bioactive compounds in the extract including flavonoids, saponins, anthraquinones, sterols, and terpenoids. The extract demonstrated potent protoscolicidal activity against hydatid cyst protoscoleces, with complete eradication achieved at a concentration of 800 mg/ml. The mechanism of action involved activation of caspase-3 and induction of apoptosis in the parasitic protoscoleces, as measured by caspase-3 activity assays. These findings suggest that Taraxacum officinale extract may offer a natural alternative to conventional surgical agents like hypertonic saline solution, cetrimide-C, and silver nitrate for managing complications during echinococcosis treatment.

title: Mogrosides Protect Against Diabetic Kidney Injury via Inhibiting Macrophage Activation in a Mouse Model
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that mogrosides, natural polyphenolic compounds, protect against diabetic kidney injury by suppressing macrophage activation through inhibition of interconnected inflammatory and oxidative stress pathways. High glucose levels activate macrophages and elevate reactive oxygen species (ROS), which trigger NF-κB signaling and NLRP3 inflammasome activation, leading to increased production of pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, TNF-α) and kidney damage.
