Beyond its primary anti-inflammatory role, chrysin demonstrates therapeutic potential across diverse conditions including asthma, atopic dermatitis, osteoporosis, cardiovascular disease, and neurological disorders such as Alzheimer's disease and Parkinson's disease, while also exerting anxiolytic and antidepressant effects through GABA receptor binding and modulation of serotonin and corticosterone levels. A significant limitation is chrysin's poor oral bioavailability due to rapid phase II metabolism by UDP-glucuronosyltransferases and swift elimination from the body. The compound's biosynthesis in plants originates from phenylalanine through a enzymatic pathway involving L-phenylalanine ammonia-lyase and cinnamic acid intermediates. Future therapeutic applications of chrysin will likely depend on developing formulations that overcome bioavailability constraints while leveraging its broad-spectrum anti-inflammatory and neuroprotective mechanisms.

title: Effects of Diethylstilbestrol on Uterus Structure and Immunological Function in Mice During Early Pregnancy
summary: Diethylstilbestrol (DES), a synthetic estrogen, impairs early pregnancy in mice through multifaceted disruption of reproductive and immune homeostasis. DES causes dose-dependent reductions in ovarian and uterine weight, embryo number, and uterine gland density while paradoxically increasing maternal body weight and spleen weight, indicating systemic metabolic disruption. The endocrine disruption manifests as elevated serum 17β-estradiol and reduced progesterone, accompanied by architectural uterine damage including epithelial vacuolar degeneration and nuclear pyknosis. DES dysregulates immune tolerance by shifting the Th1/Th2 balance toward proinflammatory responses, increasing IL-2 while suppressing IL-4, and inhibiting lymphocyte proliferation in both uterine and splenic tissues. Additionally, DES impairs antioxidant defenses across reproductive organs, reducing superoxide dismutase, glutathione peroxidase, and total antioxidant capacity while increasing lipid peroxidation markers, and upregulates pro-apoptotic and inflammatory mediators including Bax and inducible nitric oxide synthase. These integrated endocrine, immunological, and oxidative stress mechanisms collectively impair endometrial receptivity and ultimately lead to implantation failure and pregnancy loss.

title: Mycotoxin-Caused Intestinal Toxicity: Underlying Molecular Mechanisms and Further Directions
summary:
# Summary

This review examines how major mycotoxins—including deoxynivalenol, zearalenone, fumonisins, aflatoxin B1, and T-2/HT-2 toxins—damage intestinal health through multiple converging mechanisms. All tested mycotoxins cause intestinal epithelium damage and barrier dysfunction by downregulating tight junction proteins (claudins, occludin, ZO-1) and triggering inflammatory responses and oxidative stress characterized by elevated reactive oxygen species and reduced antioxidant enzyme activity (SOD, CAT, GPX). Beyond general toxicity, zearalenone acts as an endocrine disruptor by binding to and activating estrogen receptors and upregulating the Wnt/β-catenin pathway, while aflatoxin B1 induces pyroptosis through NLRP3/AMPK activation and inhibits the aryl hydrocarbon receptor pathway. At the molecular level, mycotoxin exposure disrupts key signaling pathways controlling intestinal homeostasis, including downregulation of β-catenin and its target proteins (cyclin D1, Lgr5) by deoxynivalenol and inhibition of the Nrf2 antioxidant pathway by fumonisins. These findings collectively demonstrate that mycotoxin-induced intestinal toxicity involves coordinated failures in barrier integrity, immune regulation, oxidative defense, and cellular signaling that collectively compromise gut function.

title: Astaxanthin Modulates Inflammation in Type 2 Diabetes via Regulation of microRNAs, Lysophosphatidylcholine, and α-Hydroxybutyrate
summary:
# Summary

Astaxanthin supplementation at 10 mg daily effectively modulates inflammation in type 2 diabetes through simultaneous reduction of pro-inflammatory microRNAs (miR-21, miR-34a, and miR-155) and metabolic lipid mediators (lysophosphatidylcholine and α-hydroxybutyrate). These microRNAs are implicated in diabetes pathogenesis and diabetic complications including retinopathy and nephropathy, while lysophosphatidylcholine and α-hydroxybutyrate promote insulin resistance and impaired glucose regulation by activating inflammatory signaling cascades through protein kinase C and mitogen-activated protein kinase pathways. Over 12 weeks of treatment, astaxanthin significantly improved glycemic control (reducing fasting plasma glucose and HbA1c), lipid profiles, insulin sensitivity (HOMA-IR), and renal function markers. The mechanism involves modulation of key oxidative stress and inflammatory signaling pathways including NF-κB, Nrf2, and PI3K/Akt, enabling astaxanthin to simultaneously address multiple inflammatory drivers of type 2 diabetes progression and its microvascular complications.

title: Pharmacological properties and therapeutic potential of berberine: a comprehensive review
summary: Berberine is a bioactive alkaloid extracted from traditional medicinal plants including *Coptis chinensis*, *Phellodendron amurense*, and *Zanthoxylum monophyllum*, which demonstrates remarkable therapeutic efficacy across multiple disease states. The compound exerts anti-inflammatory effects by reducing production of pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, IL-1β, TNF-α) and suppressing microglia and astrocyte activation in the brain, while simultaneously enhancing antioxidant capacity through increased expression of protective enzymes like SOD and GSH-PX. Berberine modulates key signaling pathways including cAMP/PKA/CREB, PI3K/AKT, SIRT1/p38 MAPK, and SIRT6-AMPK-FOXO3a, which collectively promote neuroprotection, improve endothelial function, and enhance autophagic and mitochondrial clearance mechanisms. The compound shows clinical promise in treating neurological disorders (Parkinson's disease, cognitive impairment), cardiovascular conditions (atherosclerosis, hypertension, heart failure), metabolic diseases (type 2 diabetes, hyperlipidemia), and infectious conditions, partly through its capacity to modulate the gut microbiota by increasing butyrate-producing bacteria and regulating the gut-brain axis. These pleiotropic mechanisms underscore berberine's therapeutic potential as a natural compound addressing inflammation, oxidative stress, and cellular dysfunction across multiple organ systems.

title: Genetic Insight Into Dissecting the FODMAP Diet Frame and Liver Cancer: The Mediation Role of Efferocytosis and Trogocytosis
summary: This study integrates dietary analysis with genetic and mechanistic investigation to identify protective factors against liver cancer. Using Mendelian randomization, the research identified four dietary components—cheese, fresh fruit, cereal, and dried fruit—that significantly reduce liver cancer risk, with cheese showing the strongest protective effect. The protective mechanisms operate partly through modulation of efferocytosis and trogocytosis, two programmed cell-death processes involving key proteins including TGFB3, EPOR, ELANE, and C3, which regulate antigen presentation and macrophage polarization in the tumor microenvironment. Cheese intake specifically mediates its protective effects by modulating these efferocytosis-related proteins and by altering bile acid composition and colonic fermentation through its calcium and bioactive peptide content. Fresh fruits contribute protection through their abundant polyphenols, flavonoids, and vitamins with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. The findings establish a mechanistic link between dietary components and liver cancer susceptibility through nutrient-mediated alterations in cellular immunity and gut microbiota function.

title: Resveratrol as a naturally occurring inflammasome modulator: Implications for health and disease
journal: Iranian Journal of Basic Medical Sciences
summary:
# Summary

Inflammasomes are multiprotein complexes that recognize pathogen-associated and damage-associated molecular patterns to control the activation of pro-inflammatory cytokines including IL-1β, IL-18, and TNF-α, which are essential for host defense but can contribute to chronic inflammation, autoimmune disorders, metabolic diseases, and neurodegenerative complications when dysregulated. Resveratrol, a naturally occurring polyphenol found in grapes, berries, and peanuts, functions as a potent inflammasome modulator by suppressing NLRP3 inflammasome activation and subsequently reducing the production of multiple pro-inflammatory cytokines. The compound exerts these anti-inflammatory effects through modulation of key signaling pathways including NF-κB inhibition, Nrf2 activation, and Sirt1 modulation, while simultaneously reducing oxidative stress and regulating mitochondrial function. Beyond inflammasome regulation, resveratrol exhibits multiple protective properties including hepatoprotective, neuroprotective, cardioprotective, and anti-cancer effects that contribute to improved health outcomes across diverse organ systems. These collective findings highlight resveratrol's broad therapeutic potential in alleviating inflammation-driven tissue damage and improving cellular resilience against various diseases, making it a promising natural therapeutic candidate for inflammatory and immune-related conditions.

title: Cicer arietinum extract as antitumor and protective agent against Ehrlich Solid Carcinoma-bearing mice
summary:
# Summary

Cicer arietinum (chickpea) extract demonstrates potent antitumor activity against Ehrlich solid carcinoma in mice through multiple mechanistic pathways. Treatment with the extract at doses of 500–750 mg/kg significantly reduced tumor size and weight while inhibiting cancer cell viability, colony formation, and cell adhesion, partly through suppression of the EGF-induced p44/42 MAPK signaling pathway. The extract exerts its protective effects primarily by alleviating oxidative stress, increasing antioxidant enzyme activities including superoxide dismutase (SOD) and glutathione peroxidase (GPx), reducing malondialdehyde (MDA) and nitric oxide (NO) levels, and upregulating Gpx4 expression. Additionally, Cicer arietinum extract promotes cancer cell death by downregulating the anti-apoptotic protein survivin while simultaneously increasing expression of the pro-apoptotic caspase-3 and pyroptotic gasdermin-D, demonstrating dual cell death pathways. The extract's bioactive compounds, including isoflavones (genistein, daidzein, biochanin A, and formononetin), along with peptides, lectins, and protease inhibitors, collectively contribute to its comprehensive antitumor and chemopreventive efficacy.

title: From a traditional medicine monomer to a modern neurotherapeutic scaffold: a review of SAR-Driven tetramethylpyrazine derivatives for cerebrovascular and cognitive health
summary: Tetramethylpyrazine (TMP), a bioactive alkaloid from traditional Chinese medicine, exhibits potent antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and anti-apoptotic properties centered on its electron-rich pyrazine core pharmacophore, which directly scavenges reactive oxygen species and inhibits the TLR4/NF-κB signaling cascade to suppress pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6). Structurally, the four symmetrically positioned methyl groups enhance lipophilicity and blood-brain barrier permeability, enabling TMP to accumulate in mitochondria and engage multiple neuroprotective pathways including Nrf2/HO-1 and Sirt1/NF-κB activation, while suppressing apoptotic cascades and NLRP3 inflammasome-mediated pyroptosis. Strategic structural optimization has yielded several derivatives with enhanced clinical translational potential—T-006, TN-2, and T-VA demonstrate superior neuroprotective efficacy, while Tetramethylpyrazine-2′-O-sodium ferulate (TSF) combines improved aqueous solubility with enhanced anti-inflammatory potency and platelet aggregation inhibition. Collectively, TMP and its derivatives address calcium dysregulation and chronic neuroinflammation in ischemic stroke, cognitive impairment, and neurodegenerative conditions through preservation of blood-brain barrier integrity via tight junction proteins (occludin, claudin-5) and mitigation of pathological hallmarks including amyloid-beta aggregation and tau hyperphosphorylation.

title: Nanotechnology strategies for endometrium health: Are we on the right track?
summary:
# Summary

Endometrial pathologies, including endometriosis, adenomyosis, and endometritis, arise from hormonal imbalance, persistent inflammation, oxidative stress, and dysregulated immune responses, causing significant morbidity through chronic pelvic pain, infertility, and abnormal bleeding. Nanotechnology offers promising therapeutic strategies to address these conditions by enhancing drug solubility, stability, and bioavailability while enabling targeted delivery of therapeutic agents. Mesoporous silica nanoparticles and cerium oxide nanoparticles (nanoceria) demonstrate particular efficacy by reducing oxidative stress, suppressing innate immune markers (NOD2 and RAGE), downregulating phosphorylated STAT3, and inhibiting endometrial lesion proliferation and angiogenesis. Advanced nanoplatforms—including polymeric, metal-based, lipid-based, and hybrid carriers—facilitate delivery of conventional therapeutics, immunomodulatory agents, and gene therapy tools such as CRISPR-Cas9 and siRNA, while functionalized nanoparticles enhance diagnostic accuracy through improved imaging. These nanotechnology approaches represent a next-generation strategy for treating endometrial diseases by addressing underlying pathogenic mechanisms while improving therapeutic efficacy and patient outcomes.

title: Gut microbiota dysbiosis affects intestinal sensitivity through epithelium-to-neuron signaling: novel insights from a colon organoid-based model to improve visceral pain therapy
summary: This study demonstrates that dysbiotic microbiota impairs intestinal epithelial function and activates nociceptive pathways through a novel epithelium-to-neuron signaling mechanism. Using a colon organoid model, fecal supernatants from dextran sodium sulfate-treated mice (FSDSS) induced significant structural damage and metabolic dysfunction characterized by reduced short-chain fatty acids including butyrate, acetate, and propionate. FSDSS-treated organoids released soluble factors that increased dorsal root ganglion neuron excitability, elevating markers of neuronal activation (c-Fos) and pain-related neuropeptides (calcitonin gene-related peptide), which amplify peripheral neurogenic inflammation and visceral pain signaling. Transcriptomic analysis revealed that FSDSS dysbiosis downregulates genes supporting mitochondrial function and energy metabolism while upregulating proteolytic enzymes, inflammatory cytokines (IL-1α), neurotrophic factors (BDNF), and growth factors that sensitize sensory neurons. These findings establish dysbiosis-driven epithelial injury as a critical mechanism linking altered microbiota composition to visceral hypersensitivity, suggesting that restoring short-chain fatty acid production through polyphenol-derived compounds or microbial modulation could represent a therapeutic strategy for dysbiosis-associated pain conditions including endometriosis.

title: Phytoconstituents as emerging therapeutics for breast cancer: Mechanistic insights and clinical implications
summary:
# Summary

Phytoconstituents from natural sources exhibit potent anticancer activity against breast cancer through multiple complementary mechanisms. These bioactive compounds induce apoptosis, inhibit cell proliferation, suppress metastasis, and block angiogenesis by targeting critical survival signaling pathways including NF-κB, PI3K/AKT/mTOR, JAK/STAT, ERK/MAPK, and Wnt/β-catenin. Specific phytochemicals such as curcumin, EGCG, resveratrol, berberine, apigenin, and sulforaphane activate both intrinsic and extrinsic apoptotic pathways through distinct mechanisms—curcumin upregulates p53 and modulates Bax/Bcl-2 ratios, EGCG elevates reactive oxygen species and mitochondrial dysfunction, resveratrol triggers caspase activation via cytochrome c release, and berberine/apigenin/sulforaphane activate death receptors and caspase-8. Additionally, phytochemicals promote cell cycle arrest via cyclin and CDK regulation, induce autophagy through FoxO1 and LC3-II targeting, and suppress metastasis by inhibiting epithelial-mesenchymal transition, matrix metalloproteinases, and urokinase plasminogen activator. These findings establish phytoconstituents as promising therapeutic candidates for breast cancer management with mechanisms complementary to conventional therapies.

title: Monascus purpureus Red Yeast Rice: A Review of the in vitro and in vivo Pharmacological Activities, Studies in Humans, and Case Reports
summary:
# Summary

Red yeast rice, produced by fermentation of rice with *Monascus purpureus*, contains multiple bioactive compounds including monacolins, flavonoids, azaphilones (monascin and ankaflavin), and sterols that exert diverse pharmacological effects. The primary mechanism involves monacolin K, which is structurally identical to lovastatin and inhibits HMG-CoA reductase, thereby reducing cholesterol biosynthesis and lowering blood lipid levels including total cholesterol, LDL-cholesterol, and triglycerides while improving HDL-cholesterol. Beyond lipid management, red yeast rice exhibits anti-inflammatory activity through inhibition of mast cell degranulation and TNF-α secretion, reduces reactive oxygen species production through Nrf2-dependent pathways, and improves bone mineral density by decreasing bone turnover markers. Additional bioactive constituents demonstrate antifungal and antibacterial properties, while specific extracts like Xuezhikang inhibit vulnerable plaque progression and upregulate PPARα signaling, suggesting cardioprotective benefits that extend beyond simple cholesterol reduction.

title: Potential Therapeutic Actions of Flavonoids Present in Propolis That Modulate Vascular Endothelial Growth Factor Signaling to Regulate Angiogenesis
summary:
# Summary

Propolis, a natural product rich in over 100 flavonoids including chrysin, quercetin, kaempferol, and luteolin, exerts therapeutic anti-angiogenic effects primarily through modulation of vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) signaling pathways. These flavonoids inhibit angiogenesis by suppressing VEGF expression and downstream signaling cascades involving HIF-1α, STAT3, and VEGFR2-mediated activation of PI3K and PLCγ-PKC pathways. Specific propolis flavonoids demonstrate clinical potential in treating angiogenesis-dependent pathologies: chrysin and quercetin downregulate VEGF and HIF-1α to alleviate diabetic retinopathy and age-related macular degeneration, while luteolin suppresses Notch-1 signaling to reduce breast cancer progression. Additionally, propolis constituents including chrysin exert antioxidative and anti-inflammatory activities through modulation of NF-κB expression and reactive oxygen species production. These multifaceted mechanisms position propolis-derived flavonoids as promising natural therapeutic agents for diseases characterized by pathological angiogenesis, including cancer, diabetic retinopathy, and rheumatoid arthritis.

title: Dapagliflozin Reverses LPS‐Induced Depressive–Like Behavior in Mice via Modulation of Glutamate and NF‐κB
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that dapagliflozin, an SGLT2 inhibitor primarily used for type 2 diabetes treatment, reverses lipopolysaccharide (LPS)-induced depressive-like behaviors in mice through modulation of glutamate and inflammatory pathways. LPS administration induces depression-like symptoms accompanied by upregulation of glutamate transporters GLT-1 and SLC7A11, as well as elevation of the pro-inflammatory transcription factor NF-κB in the prefrontal cortex. These molecular changes contribute to glutamate dysregulation and neuroinflammation, both recognized pathogenic mechanisms in depression characterized by elevated inflammatory cytokines including IL-6, IL-1β, TNF-α, and IL-8. Dapagliflozin treatment successfully reversed LPS-induced behavioral deficits by downregulating GLT-1, SLC7A11, and NF-κB mRNA expression while reducing phosphorylated NF-κB levels and NLRP3 inflammasome activation. The findings suggest that dapagliflozin's neuroprotective effects in depression may extend beyond glucose metabolism, operating instead through inhibition of neuroinflammatory cascades and restoration of glutamate homeostasis, similar to mechanisms engaged by conventional antidepressants like ketamine, fluoxetine, and amitriptyline.

title: Sustainable Extraction of the Tetrahydropalmatine
summary:
# Summary

Tetrahydropalmatine (THP), the major bioactive alkaloid from Stephania rotunda Lour., exhibits potent antiaddiction, antitumor, and neuroprotective properties through multiple molecular mechanisms. The study demonstrated that lactic acid achieves the highest extraction yield of THP (19.60 ± 0.36 mg/g) among tested solvents, with XAD-8 resin recovering 92.02 ± 0.60% of extracted THP, establishing a sustainable extraction methodology. Mechanistically, THP reduces endometriosis and dysmenorrhea pain by decreasing oxidative stress and suppressing proinflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-6) while inhibiting microglial activation and reducing proton currents in pain-sensing ion channels. The alkaloid demonstrates broad pharmacological activity, enhancing chemotherapy sensitivity in ovarian and breast cancers, promoting vascular relaxation, and supporting muscle regeneration and liver repair. These findings highlight THP's therapeutic potential as a multifunctional natural compound with particular relevance to pain management in endometriosis and dysmenorrhea through anti-inflammatory and antioxidative pathways.

title: Docosahexaenoic Acid (DHA) Decreases IL-6 and Prostaglandin-Endoperoxide Synthase 2 mRNA Expression and IL-6 Protein Release, While Increasing Resolvin D1 and CXCL8 mRNA Expression and Protein Release in BovineEndometrial Cells
journal: Animals : an Open Access Journal from MDPI
summary:
# Summary

Docosahexaenoic acid (DHA), an omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acid found in marine sources, exerts potent anti-inflammatory effects on bovine endometrial cells through both direct suppression of pro-inflammatory mediators and activation of pro-resolving pathways. DHA significantly reduces lipopolysaccharide (LPS)-induced expression of interleukin-6 (IL-6) and prostaglandin-endoperoxide synthase 2 (PTGS2), while simultaneously suppressing the phosphorylation of ERK1/2 and Akt signaling proteins downstream of toll-like receptor 4 activation. Simultaneously, DHA stimulates production of resolvin D1, a specialized pro-resolving mediator generated through 15-lipoxygenase-mediated conversion of DHA, which further reduces chemokine CXCL8 production and enhances macrophage phagocytosis via ALX/FPR2 and GP32 receptor activation. These findings suggest DHA's therapeutic potential for managing uterine inflammatory diseases such as postpartum metritis and endometritis in dairy cattle, particularly given the increased susceptibility during negative energy balance when elevated non-esterified fatty acids drive pathological inflammation. The dual anti-inflammatory and pro-resolving mechanisms of DHA, mediated partly through FFA4 receptor signaling on endometrial cells, provide a molecular basis for omega-3 supplementation as a nutritional strategy to improve reproductive health in postpartum cows.

title: Sakuranetin, A Laxative Component from Peach Leaves and Its Intervention in Metabolism
summary: Sakuranetin, a polyphenolic compound isolated from peach leaves, functions as a laxative agent through multiple mechanisms distinct from traditional prodrug laxatives like sennoside A. The compound promotes intestinal transit and enhances peristaltic activity in a dose-dependent manner by primarily binding to the gnat1 protein, which subsequently suppresses rho-mediated inflammatory signaling. At the metabolic level, sakuranetin upregulates serine and threonine biosynthesis pathways while increasing D-serine production, leading to enhanced anti-inflammatory effects, mucin synthesis, and intestinal barrier stabilization without requiring bacterial transformation. Additionally, sakuranetin downregulates genes involved in glycolysis (pim2 and map6d1), preventing excessive energy metabolism and lipid deposition. The compound's multifaceted approach—combining direct motility enhancement with metabolic reprogramming that supports intestinal epithelial health through glycine-serine-threonine metabolism and suppression of NF-κB signaling—demonstrates sakuranetin as a promising natural therapeutic candidate for constipation and potentially inflammatory bowel conditions.

title: Unlocking Polyphenol Efficacy: The Role of Gut Microbiota in Modulating Bioavailability and Health Effects
summary:
# Summary

Polyphenols, a diverse class of plant-derived compounds including flavonoids and stilbenes, exhibit potent antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial properties with potential therapeutic applications in preventing cardiovascular disease, diabetes, obesity, and cancer. The bioavailability and efficacy of polyphenols depend critically on gut microbiota-mediated metabolism: while only a fraction is absorbed in the small intestine through enzymatic hydrolysis, unabsorbed polyphenols reach the colon where resident microbes convert them into metabolites that demonstrate superior biological activity and systemic circulation compared to parent compounds. These microbial-derived metabolites modulate the gut microbiota composition, enhance intestinal barrier integrity through short-chain fatty acid production, regulate immune responses via NF-κB inhibition, and influence metabolic pathways through the gut-liver-brain axis. Specific polyphenol subclasses show distinct absorption and metabolic patterns: flavonols and flavones are primarily absorbed in the small intestine before colonic metabolism, while flavanones undergo initial gastric hydrolysis followed by microbial processing in the colon. The food matrix composition and individual variation in microbiota function substantially influence polyphenol bioavailability and health outcomes, underscoring that therapeutic benefits extend beyond direct antioxidant activity to encompass microbiota-dependent metabolic, immunomodulatory, and anti-aging effects.

title: Polystyrene nanoplastics induce oxidative stress in Aurelia coerulea polyps, microglia, and mice
summary:
# Summary

Polystyrene nanoplastics induce widespread oxidative stress and neuroinflammation across multiple biological systems through activation of the MAPK signaling pathway. Exposure to PS-NPs reduces antioxidant capacity (decreased T-AOC and catalase activity), increases reactive oxygen species and malondialdehyde levels, and triggers microglial activation characterized by pro-inflammatory M1 polarization with elevated CD32, CD11b, IL-1β, and TNF-α expression. In animal models, PS-NPs accumulated in various tissues and crossed the blood-brain barrier, causing microglial activation in the hippocampus, microglial-mediated cell apoptosis, and neurobehavioral dysfunction including anxiety-like behaviors and cognitive impairment. The study demonstrates that nanoplastics' small size enables systemic bioaccumulation in organs including liver and adipose tissue, with documented human exposure through multiple routes including feces, lung tissue, and placental tissues, establishing PS-NPs as widespread environmental toxins capable of inducing neuroinflammatory cascades with potential long-term health consequences.

title: Kuntai Capsules Improve Premature Ovarian Failure by Regulating AMPK-Mediated Autophagy
summary:
# Summary

Kuntai capsule (KTC), a traditional Chinese medicine formulation containing the polyphenols quercetin, kaempferol, and luteolin, effectively treats premature ovarian failure (POF) by regulating AMPK-mediated autophagy and restoring ovarian function. In cyclophosphamide-induced POF rat models, KTC treatment dose-dependently increased ovarian weight, restored estrus cycles, and elevated serum estradiol, anti-müllerian hormone, and superoxide dismutase while reducing follicle-stimulating hormone, reactive oxygen species, and malondialdehyde. At the molecular level, KTC activated the AMPK/mTOR signaling pathway, which suppressed autophagy markers (LC3B-II/I and Beclin1) and reduced ovarian cell apoptosis, senescence, and DNA damage markers (P16 and γH2AX). Additionally, KTC upregulated oocyte growth factors (BMP-15, BMP-7, GDF-9, and VEGFA) and anti-apoptotic Bcl-2 while downregulating pro-apoptotic markers (Bax and Cleaved Caspase-3), thereby promoting primordial and mature follicle development. These findings suggest that KTC may also benefit other reproductive disorders including polycystic ovary syndrome and endometriosis through similar mechanisms of antioxidant and anti-apoptotic signaling.

title: Biomimetic Nanoparticles Loaded With α‐Cyperone Alleviating LPS‐Induced Inflammation in KGN Cells by Activating Nrf2/HO‐1 and Suppressing ROS
journal: Journal of Biochemical and Molecular Toxicology
summary:
# Summary

Oxidative stress, chronic inflammation, and mitochondrial dysfunction are critical contributors to diminished ovarian reserve, with granulosa cell damage resulting from oxidative/antioxidant imbalance and elevated reactive oxygen species accumulation. This study demonstrates that biomimetic nanoparticles loaded with α-cyperone (a natural polyphenol derived from Cyperi Rhizoma traditional Chinese medicine) and functionalized with an FSHR-targeting peptide (PAMF nanoparticles) effectively alleviate lipopolysaccharide-induced inflammation in granulosa cells. The PAMF nanoparticles suppress pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-1β) while simultaneously activating the Nrf2/HO-1 antioxidant pathway and inhibiting NF-κB signaling, ultimately suppressing ROS production and restoring mitochondrial function. The targeted delivery system enhances cellular uptake through FSHR-mediated endocytosis, promoting cell viability and proliferation while suppressing apoptosis in inflamed granulosa cells. These findings suggest that α-cyperone-loaded nanoparticles represent a promising therapeutic strategy for addressing oxidative stress and inflammation-related ovarian dysfunction.

title: Metabolic Reprogramming Through Polyphenol Networks: A Systems Approach to Metabolic Inflammation and Insulin Resistance
summary:
# Summary

Obesity-induced insulin resistance and type 2 diabetes involve a complex metabolic cascade where expanded adipose tissue produces pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6, MCP-1) that activate NF-κB signaling and degrade insulin receptor function, while accumulated free fatty acids trigger lipotoxicity, ceramide accumulation, and mitochondrial dysfunction that collectively generate reactive oxygen species. Multiple cellular stress pathways converge—including chronic inflammation, oxidative stress, endoplasmic reticulum stress, and impaired mitochondrial ATP production—creating a feed-forward cycle that eventually exhausts pancreatic β-cells through glucolipotoxicity and apoptosis. Citrus polyphenols, including hesperidin, naringenin, nobiletin, and tangeretin, counteract these pathological mechanisms by modulating key metabolic sensors (AMPK, NF-κB, Nrf2) and the SIRT1–PGC1α pathway, thereby reducing pro-inflammatory cytokine expression, improving mitochondrial biogenesis and membrane stability, alleviating endoplasmic reticulum stress, and ultimately restoring insulin sensitivity and preventing β-cell failure. This polyphenol-mediated metabolic reprogramming demonstrates a systems-level approach to reversing metabolic inflammation and restoring glucose homeostasis in preclinical models of obesity-related metabolic disease.

title: Lactobacillus crispatus-derived nCEV vesicles promote cutaneous wound healing and inhibit HPV16 infection
summary: Lactobacillus crispatus secretes nanoparticle-like cytoplasmic extracellular vesicles (nCEVs) enriched in D-lactate that demonstrate superior therapeutic potential compared to vesicles from other Lactobacillus species. These nCEVs promote cutaneous wound healing by enhancing cell migration, angiogenesis, and collagen deposition while upregulating tight junction proteins and wound-associated markers like VEGF and CD31. Mechanistically, nCEVs promote macrophage M2 polarization by suppressing pro-inflammatory factors (IL-6, TNF-α) while enhancing anti-inflammatory mediators (IL-10, TGF-β) and reducing reactive oxygen species production. Additionally, nCEVs potently inhibit HPV16 infection in a dose-dependent manner by blocking viral entry into host cells, consistent with the clinical observation that L. crispatus dominance is associated with successful HPV clearance and reduced cervical cancer risk. In contrast, vesicles from L. iners (nIEVs) associated with bacterial vaginosis show opposite effects, impairing barrier integrity and promoting pro-inflammatory responses. These findings establish L. crispatus-derived nCEVs as promising biological agents for both wound healing and viral infection prevention.

title: Effect of curcumin on fatigue and musculoskeletal health in postmenopausal women: a double-blind randomised controlled trial
summary:
# Summary

This double-blind randomized controlled trial demonstrates that curcumin, a polyphenol extracted from turmeric, significantly improves musculoskeletal health and reduces fatigue in postmenopausal women, with an 8-week intervention producing statistically significant reductions in fatigue symptoms and improvements in muscle function. The therapeutic effects of curcumin are underpinned by multiple biological mechanisms, including potent antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties that inhibit reactive oxygen species (superoxide radicals, hydrogen peroxide, and nitric oxide), suppress the NF-κB signaling pathway, and reduce systemic oxidative stress and neuroinflammation. Postmenopausal women experience a systemic inflammatory state resulting from estrogen depletion, which contributes to widespread musculoskeletal dysfunction, muscle pain, and fatigue; curcumin counteracts these effects through both peripheral mechanisms (reducing muscle damage and enhancing mitochondrial biogenesis for cellular energy production) and central mechanisms (modulating monoamine neurotransmitters and promoting neurogenesis). While curcumin demonstrated efficacy for musculoskeletal outcomes and fatigue, it did not significantly improve depressive symptoms in this population. Some participants experienced mild gastrointestinal side effects, including stomach ache and headache, though the overall safety profile supports curcumin as a potential therapeutic agent for managing postmenopausal musculoskeletal and fatigue symptoms.

title: Research on the inhibitory effect of hydrogen gas on ovarian cancer and potential mechanisms
summary:
# Summary

Hydrogen gas demonstrates potent anti-ovarian cancer activity through a multi-targeted mechanism involving oxidative stress and inflammatory pathway suppression. In SKOV3 ovarian cancer cells, hydrogen inhibits proliferation, migration, and invasion while inducing apoptosis and G1/G2 phase arrest in a concentration-dependent manner. The therapeutic effects operate through elevation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and subsequent downregulation of the HIF-1α/NF-κB signaling axis, which normally promotes tumor cell survival, angiogenesis, and metastatic vasculogenic mimicry. In vivo studies confirm that hydrogen treatment significantly reduces tumor growth and angiogenesis while decreasing Ki-67 proliferation markers, with synergistic enhancement observed when combined with carboplatin chemotherapy. Beyond direct anti-tumor effects, hydrogen improves cancer-related symptoms including fatigue, insomnia, anorexia, and pain. These findings suggest hydrogen gas offers a novel therapeutic approach for ovarian cancer, particularly addressing platinum resistance that limits current treatment options.

title: New Perspectives on the Use of Resveratrol in the Treatment of Metabolic and Estrogen-Dependent Conditions Through Hormonal Modulation and Anti-Inflammatory Effects
summary:
# Summary

Resveratrol demonstrates therapeutic potential for endometriosis and related metabolic and estrogen-dependent conditions through dual mechanisms of hormonal modulation and anti-inflammatory activity. The polyphenol interacts with estrogen receptors and exerts phytoestrogenic activity while simultaneously modulating anti-inflammatory factors, decreasing insulin resistance, enhancing lipolysis, and mitigating oxidative stress. Resveratrol primarily operates through sirtuin activation, which regulates metabolic processes across multiple tissues and triggers downstream pathways including PGC-1α, AMPK, and PPARγ to enhance mitochondrial biogenesis and glucose metabolism. Activated sirtuins inhibit pro-inflammatory mediators (IL-1, IL-6, TNF-α, NF-κB, COX-1/2), stimulate antioxidant enzymes (superoxide dismutase, catalase, glutathione peroxidase), and upregulate eNOS to improve endothelial function. These mechanisms address the underlying pathology in endometriosis, where estrogen receptor hyperactivity and chronic inflammation drive disease progression, offering a natural compound approach to hormonal regulation without systemic estrogen elevation.

title: Potential of Natural Products in Hangeshashinto Water Extract on the Direct Suppression of Stomatitis Induced by Intra-/Extracellular Advanced Glycation End-Products
summary: This review examines how Hangeshashinto, a traditional Kampo medicine, suppresses stomatitis through inhibition of advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and their inflammatory signaling. Stomatitis is induced by both intracellular AGEs (from blood glucose) and extracellular AGEs (from dietary sources), which activate RAGE and toll-like receptor 4 on oral epithelial cells, triggering inflammatory responses. Multiple natural products in Hangeshashinto—including glycyrrhizin, baicalin, 6-shogaol, quercetin, and epigallocatechin-3-gallate—directly inhibit AGE formation and suppress the AGEs-RAGE/TLR4 signaling axis while reducing inflammatory mediators like prostaglandin E2, IL-6, and IL-8. The Hangeshashinto extract also inhibits cyclooxygenase-2 and promotes oral keratinocyte migration through CXCL12-CXCR4 signaling, facilitating epithelial tissue repair. These polyphenolic compounds demonstrate therapeutic potential as direct protective agents against AGE-induced oral inflammation associated with lifestyle-related diseases including diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and hypertension.

title: Epigallocatechin-Gallate (EGCG): An Essential Molecule for Human Health and Well-Being
summary:
# Summary

Epigallocatechin-gallate (EGCG), the predominant polyphenolic catechin found in green tea (10–15% by concentration), exhibits potent antioxidant and anti-cancer properties through multiple molecular mechanisms including inhibition of p38 and JNK phosphorylation, blockade of NF-κB translocation, and suppression of tumor-related angiogenesis. EGCG demonstrates particular relevance to endometriosis and other hormone-sensitive conditions through its anti-angiogenic activity, suggesting therapeutic potential in this context. The compound modulates cellular energy metabolism by activating AMPK, promoting fat oxidation while suppressing lipogenic genes (ACC1, FAS, SCD1, PPARγ) and stimulating lipolytic genes like HSL, resulting in measurable clinical benefits including 4.6% body weight reduction and improved insulin sensitivity. EGCG's bioavailability is optimized under acidic pH conditions but significantly compromised by heat exposure above 80°C, with the compound being rapidly absorbed in the digestive system, metabolized primarily in the liver and colon, and eliminated via bile and urine. Beyond metabolic effects, EGCG prolongs norepinephrine activity by inhibiting COMT, thereby enhancing thermogenesis and energy expenditure in brown adipose tissue. These multifaceted mechanisms position EGCG as a promising therapeutic agent for managing cancer, metabolic disorders, and endometriosis-related pathology.

title: Curcumin Alleviates HMGB1-Mediated Inflammation Through the Signaling Pathway of TLR2-NF-κB in Bovine Ovarian Granulosa Cells
summary: Curcumin attenuates high-mobility group box 1 (HMGB1)-induced inflammation in bovine ovarian granulosa cells by suppressing the toll-like receptor 2 (TLR2)-nuclear factor kappa B (NF-κB) signaling pathway. HMGB1 released extracellularly initiates pro-inflammatory cascades through interaction with TLR2, TLR4, and RAGE, triggering upregulation of TLR family members and downstream ovulation-related genes including EGFR, VEGF, STAR, TIMP1, and TIMP2, which impairs follicular development when excessive. Curcumin dose-dependently inhibits HMGB1-induced TLR expression (TLR1, TLR2, TLR6), phosphorylation of NF-κB p65, and production of the pro-inflammatory cytokine IL-6, thereby preventing the pathological cascade. At physiologically optimal concentrations (1.25–5 μg/mL), curcumin promotes granulosa cell viability and reduces apoptosis while suppressing oxidative stress and inflammation, supporting folliculogenesis and preventing premature ovarian failure. These findings establish curcumin as a potential therapeutic agent for managing HMGB1-mediated ovarian dysfunction and inflammation-related reproductive disorders including endometriosis and polycystic ovarian syndrome. The polyphenol's mechanism highlights a natural compound approach to restoring the balance between inflammatory and ovulatory signaling in reproductive tissues.

title: Therapeutic Potential of Food-Derived Rutin Phytosome Nanoparticles: Anti-Tumor, Antioxidant, and Anti-Inflammatory Activity in Ehrlich Ascites Carcinoma
summary:
# Summary

Rutin, a bioflavonoid phytocompound, demonstrates potent anti-cancer properties through multiple molecular mechanisms including inhibition of the PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling pathway and suppression of key inflammatory mediators such as NF-κB, COX-2, IL-6, and TNF-α. The compound induces apoptosis by downregulating anti-apoptotic proteins (Bcl-2) while upregulating pro-apoptotic factors (Bax, caspases, and p53) and modulates tumor-suppressive microRNAs to limit cancer cell proliferation. When formulated as rutin-loaded phytosome nanoparticles, which enhance bioavailability and membrane permeability through phospholipid vesicle delivery, the compound significantly improved therapeutic efficacy in an Ehrlich ascites carcinoma mouse model, reducing ascitic fluid volume and tumor cell viability while extending mean survival time from 9 to 14 days. Treatment with rutin phytosomes also restored hematological parameters, normalized metabolic markers, suppressed tumor biomarkers (AFP, CEA, and CA 19-9), and enhanced antioxidant enzyme levels while reducing oxidative stress markers. Histopathological analysis confirmed protective effects on liver and kidney tissues, establishing rutin phytosomes as a promising natural therapeutic agent combining anti-cancer, antioxidant, and anti-inflammatory activities.

title: Intervention of Rutin and Ochnaflavone in the Attenuation of Neuroinflammation and Neuronal Apoptosis in Spinal Cord Injury
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that rutin and ochnaflavone, two polyphenolic flavonoids derived from Chinese medicine sources, provide neuroprotective effects in spinal cord injury through synergistic inhibition of microglial activation and suppression of inflammatory cascades. Both compounds downregulate key pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6) and inhibit phosphorylation of critical signaling intermediates including PI3K, AKT, IKKβ, and NF-κB p65, thereby blocking NF-κB nuclear translocation and subsequent transcription of inflammatory mediators like iNOS and COX-2. Additionally, rutin and ochnaflavone suppress neuronal apoptosis by downregulating pro-apoptotic markers (Bax and cleaved Caspase-3) while upregulating the anti-apoptotic factor Bcl-xL. These findings establish that the dual flavonoids attenuate secondary injury mechanisms in spinal cord trauma through coordinated modulation of both PI3K/AKT and NF-κB signaling pathways, offering a promising therapeutic strategy for managing neuroinflammation and neuronal death following traumatic spinal cord injury.

title: Role of vitamin D in the management of chronic pain after gastrointestinal surgery
summary: Vitamin D plays a multifaceted protective role in managing chronic postsurgical pain through multiple interconnected mechanisms. After synthesis in the skin or dietary intake, vitamin D is converted to its active form, calcitriol, which binds to vitamin D receptors distributed across immune cells, neurons, and gastrointestinal cells to regulate bone metabolism, immune responses, inflammatory pathways, and neuronal health. Vitamin D reduces inflammation by inhibiting proinflammatory cytokines like tumor necrosis factor-α and interleukin-6 while promoting anti-inflammatory interleukin-10, downregulates pain-related genes including TRPV1, enhances nerve regeneration and myelination through nerve growth factor upregulation, and prevents gut dysbiosis to modulate the gut-brain axis. Vitamin D deficiency is associated with increased pain sensitivity, heightened opioid consumption, and impaired tissue repair following gastrointestinal surgery, while supplementation demonstrates clinical efficacy in reducing chronic postsurgical pain and opioid dependence across multiple surgical populations. Additionally, vitamin D supports wound healing by facilitating mesenchymal stem cell differentiation, increasing collagen synthesis, reducing scar formation, and enhancing immune cell function to lower surgical site infection rates. These findings suggest vitamin D optimization represents a valuable preventive and therapeutic strategy for managing chronic pain and improving recovery outcomes following gastrointestinal and other surgical procedures.

title: Ilimaquinone-induced lipophagy diminishes lipid accumulation via AMPK activation
summary: Ilimaquinone (IQ), a marine sponge-derived sesquiterpene compound, reduces lipid accumulation through activation of the AMPK-mTOR signaling pathway and promotion of lipophagy in both C. elegans and mammalian liver cells. IQ activates AMPK while inhibiting mTOR, leading to enhanced autophagy and selective lipophagy of lipid droplets, as evidenced by increased LC3-II conversion and decreased p62 levels. The compound upregulates key lipid-metabolizing enzymes including lysosomal acid lipase (Lipa) and carnitine palmitoyltransferase 1b (Cpt1b), which facilitate the breakdown of triglycerides and cholesterol esters into free fatty acids for β-oxidation. Pharmacological blockade of autophagy with Bafilomycin A1 reversed IQ's lipid-reducing effects, confirming that lipophagy is essential for the compound's mechanism. These findings demonstrate that IQ extends lifespan in a dose-dependent manner by up to 50% through AMPK-mediated lipid catabolism, suggesting therapeutic potential for metabolic diseases associated with lipid accumulation including atherosclerosis and type II diabetes.

title: Citronellol silver nanoconjugates as a therapeutic strategy for glioblastoma through computational and experimental evaluation
summary:
# Summary

This study evaluated citronellol and its silver nanoconjugate (CN@AgNPs) as therapeutic agents against glioblastoma by targeting key cell cycle regulators. Both compounds reduced CDK4 expression and enhanced p53 expression at comparable IC50 values (approximately 20 µg/mL), mechanisms that promote cell cycle arrest and apoptosis since CDK4 normally drives cell cycle progression under p53 regulation. Computational docking analyses confirmed direct binding of citronellol and CN@AgNPs to both p53 and CDK4, supporting their mechanism of action. Beyond their cytotoxic effects, these compounds demonstrated favorable pharmacological properties including blood-brain barrier permeability, high gastrointestinal absorption, oral bioavailability, and notably are not substrates for P-glycoprotein efflux pumps, reducing the risk of multidrug resistance that complicates temozolomide treatment. Additionally, citronellol alleviated chemotherapy-related side effects including nausea, dysgeusia, and immune suppression. The silver nanoconjugate displayed superior stability compared to free citronellol, suggesting that CN@AgNPs may represent a promising therapeutic strategy for glioblastoma by simultaneously targeting dysregulated cell cycle pathways and improving treatment tolerability.

title: Dimethyloxalylglycine improves functional recovery through inhibiting cell apoptosis and enhancing blood-spinal cord barrier repair after spinal cord injury
summary:
# Summary

Dimethyloxalylglycine (DMOG) improves functional recovery following spinal cord injury by activating the HIF-1α signaling pathway through inhibition of prolylhydroxylase. DMOG stabilizes HIF-1α, which in turn suppresses endoplasmic reticulum (ER) stress—a key mechanism driving blood-spinal cord barrier (BSCB) disruption and endothelial cell apoptosis after injury. By maintaining tight junction proteins (ZO-1, occludin, P120, and β-catenin) and reducing apoptotic markers (cleaved-caspase3 and Bax), DMOG preserves BSCB integrity and prevents toxic substance infiltration into the spinal cord. HIF-1α inhibition completely blocks DMOG's protective effects, demonstrating that HIF-1α stabilization is essential for the compound's therapeutic action. These findings identify DMOG as a neuroprotective agent with potential clinical applications for spinal cord injury treatment through HIF-dependent mechanisms.

title: Tetrahydropalmatine has analgesic role in mouse model of bone cancer pain by inactivating the TNF-α/uPA/PAR2/TRPV1 pathway in dorsal root ganglia
summary: This study demonstrates that tetrahydropalmatine (THP), the active ingredient of the traditional Chinese herb yanhusuo, exerts analgesic effects in a mouse model of bone cancer pain by inhibiting the TNF-α/uPA/PAR2/TRPV1 signaling pathway in dorsal root ganglia (DRG) neurons. THP treatment attenuated pain nociception, reduced tibia destruction, and mitigated inflammation, neuronal death, and neuronal excitotoxicity in DRG tissue, while simultaneously enhancing cell viability and suppressing apoptosis in bone cancer pain cell models. The mechanism relies critically on pathway inactivation, as exogenous TNF-α or capsaicin administration abolished both the analgesic effects and pathway inhibition induced by THP, confirming the TNF-α/uPA/PAR2/TRPV1 axis as essential to the compound's action. Additionally, combination treatment with pomalidomide showed additive synergistic effects, further reducing pain nociception and DRG damage compared to monotherapy. These findings suggest THP may represent a promising therapeutic agent for managing bone cancer pain through its dual neuroprotective and anti-tumor properties, while avoiding the neuronal toxicity associated with conventional cancer treatments.

title: Advances in understanding the functions and regulatory factors of secondary metabolites in
summary:
# Summary

This article examines the bioactive properties and regulatory mechanisms of secondary metabolites, particularly polyphenols from *Reynoutria japonica*, with significant implications for inflammatory and metabolic diseases. Resveratrol and its glucoside derivative polydatin exhibit potent anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activities, with polydatin specifically inhibiting key inflammasome components (NLRP3, ASC, Caspase-1) and pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α, IL-1β) while suppressing NF-κB signaling. Beyond inflammation, these compounds enhance fatty acid oxidation and cholesterol efflux, while *Reynoutria japonica* extracts comprehensively reduce lipid profiles and improve metabolic outcomes including glucose tolerance and liver function markers. The article addresses resveratrol's bioavailability limitations and proposes solutions through gut microbiota conversion to enhanced metabolites (dihydroresveratrol and piceatannol) and nanocarrier delivery systems (liposomal encapsulation and plant-derived nanoparticles). Finally, the synthesis of these bioactive compounds is detailed through the polyketide pathway, involving acetyl-CoA and malonyl-CoA substrates with regulatory enzymes including cytochrome P450 monooxygenases, O-methyltransferases, and UDP-glycosyltransferases controlling hydroxylation, methylation, and glycosylation modifications.

title: 5,7-dimethoxyflavone inhibits hepatocellular carcinoma progression via increasing intestinal Akkermansia muciniphila and hepatic CD8+ T cell infiltration
summary:
# Summary

5,7-dimethoxyflavone (DMF), a flavonoid from Kaempferia parviflora, inhibits hepatocellular carcinoma progression through dual mechanisms involving the gut microbiota and immune system. DMF upregulates the beneficial bacterium Akkermansia muciniphila, which repairs intestinal barrier integrity and reduces hepatocellular carcinoma development by enhancing antioxidant capacity through glutathione regulation. Simultaneously, DMF directly inhibits the NF-κB/CCL2 signaling pathway in tumor cells by binding to NF-κB and blocking p65 phosphorylation, thereby reducing CCL2-mediated recruitment of immunosuppressive cells and allowing increased CD8+ T cell infiltration into tumors. These enhanced CD8+ T cells upregulate interferon-gamma expression, amplifying anti-tumor immunity. In mouse models, DMF treatment substantially reduced tumor number, tumor size, and liver-to-body ratio while improving liver function, with antibiotic treatment partially reversing these benefits, confirming the critical role of gut microbiota in DMF's therapeutic effects.

title: Uncovering the Mechanism of Quercetin in the Treatment of Premature Ovarian Failure: A Multi‐Faceted Approach Integrating Network Pharmacology, Bioinformatics Analysis and Experimental Validation
summary:
# Summary

Quercetin, a polyphenolic antioxidant, effectively treats premature ovarian failure (POF) by activating the PI3K/AKT signaling pathway in granulosa cells. The mechanism involves quercetin binding to and upregulating phosphorylation of PI3K and AKT proteins, which suppresses the pro-apoptotic p53 protein and downstream caspases (Caspase3 and Caspase9), thereby inhibiting oxidative stress-induced granulosa cell apoptosis. This protective effect was validated in vitro using hydrogen peroxide-induced apoptosis models in KGN cells, where quercetin pretreatment significantly reduced cell death markers and restored PI3K/AKT signaling. Additionally, quercetin reduces inflammatory cytokine release (IL-1α and IL-6) and improves insulin sensitivity, contributing to enhanced ovarian function and follicular development. These multi-faceted anti-apoptotic, antioxidant, and anti-inflammatory actions address the pathological mechanisms underlying POF, including diminished ovarian reserve, infertility, and menstrual irregularities.

title: Di-(2-ethylhexyl) phthalate induces endometriosis by modulating IGF-1 m6A methylation via the intestinal Odoribacter–butyric acid axis in female rats
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals a novel mechanistic pathway linking the environmental pollutant DEHP to endometriosis development through disruption of the gut microbiota–butyrate axis. DEHP exposure reduces populations of the butyrate-producing bacterium *Odoribacter*, leading to decreased intestinal butyrate levels, which subsequently enhances METTL3-mediated m6A methylation of IGF-1 mRNA. The elevated m6A-modified IGF-1 promotes epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition (EMT) through the PI3K/Akt/mTOR/Snail signaling pathway, culminating in endometriosis development and increased serum levels of inflammatory cytokines and endometriosis biomarkers. Additionally, DEHP exposure weakens intestinal tight junctions and increases expression of EMT-related transcription factors, further facilitating ectopic endometrial lesion formation. Notably, sodium butyrate supplementation reverses DEHP-induced intestinal barrier damage, suppresses EMT, and downregulates the IGF-1 pathway, suggesting butyrate as a potential therapeutic intervention. This research identifies m6A methylation as a critical post-transcriptional mechanism in environmental toxicant-induced endometriosis and highlights the therapeutic potential of butyrate-based treatment strategies.

title: Choline Intake and Pelvic Inflammatory Disease Risk: A Cross-Sectional Study Based on NHANES 2013–2018
summary: This cross-sectional study demonstrates that higher dietary choline intake is associated with significantly reduced pelvic inflammatory disease (PID) risk, with women in the highest choline intake quintile showing 45% lower PID risk compared to the lowest quintile. Choline exerts its protective effects through multiple anti-inflammatory mechanisms: its metabolites acetylcholine and betaine modulate macrophage activation and cytokine secretion, activate the nAChR/ERK pathway to promote IL-10 secretion, and inhibit pro-inflammatory cytokines including TNF-α and IL-6. Additionally, betaine strengthens intestinal barrier integrity by enhancing tight junction proteins and promotes beneficial gut microbiota abundance, while choline deficiency conversely leads to intestinal barrier disruption and dysbiosis that could predispose to infection. The relationship between choline and PID risk is nonlinear and modulated by hypertension status, with a threshold effect at 0.41 g/day overall and 0.29 g/day for normotensive women, suggesting that hypertensive individuals gain more pronounced PID protection at lower choline intakes. These findings suggest that achieving adequate choline intake through dietary sources such as eggs, red meat, milk, and cheese may help reduce PID risk by promoting both local immune regulation and systemic anti-inflammatory homeostasis.

title: Liangfang Wenjing decoction regulates endoplasmic reticulum stress-mediated apoptosis to alleviate uterine microvascular injury in cold-stressed rats
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that Liangfang Wenjing Decoction (LFWJD), a traditional herbal formulation, protects against cold stress-induced uterine microvascular injury through inhibition of endoplasmic reticulum stress (ERS)-mediated apoptosis. Cold exposure impairs uterine blood circulation by disrupting the balance between the vasoconstrictor endothelin-1 (ET-1) and vasodilator nitric oxide (NO), ultimately compromising endometrial blood supply and causing dysmenorrhea. LFWJD treatment restored normal estrous cycles, improved uterine histopathology, and enhanced blood perfusion by rebalancing ET-1 and NO levels while repairing endothelial cell function. The decoction reduced apoptosis in uterine tissue and vascular endothelial cells by downregulating key ERS pathway components including GRP78, CHOP, XBP1s, phosphorylated IRE1α, ASK1, and JNK, while upregulating the anti-apoptotic protein Bcl-2. The primary bioactive metabolites identified in LFWJD include catechin, paeoniflorin, ferulic acid, liquiritin, and senkyunolide H, which collectively contribute to its therapeutic effects on cold-related gynecological disorders including dysmenorrhea and endometriosis.

title: Dual-functional molecule 3c targeting CYP24A1-mediated 25-hydroxyvitamin D metabolism and microbial biofilms overcomes mixed vaginal infections
summary:
# Summary

Compound 3c is a dual-functional molecule combining a 1,2,4-triazole ring with Schiff base chemistry that addresses mixed vaginal infections through two complementary mechanisms. First, compound 3c inhibits CYP24A1, the enzyme responsible for degrading active vitamin D (1,25(OH)2D3), thereby maintaining elevated vitamin D concentrations that activate the vitamin D receptor and enhance antimicrobial immunity through induction of LL-37 and tight junction proteins. Second, compound 3c directly demonstrates broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity against bacterial vaginosis and vulvovaginal candidiasis pathogens, including fluconazole-resistant *Candida albicans*, through membrane disruption and biofilm inhibition. The compound exhibits excellent safety profiles with minimal cytotoxicity and hemolysis while preventing resistance development after serial passages. By synergistically combining immunomodulation via vitamin D pathway enhancement with direct antimicrobial and anti-biofilm activity, compound 3c overcomes limitations of standard sequential metronidazole and fluconazole therapy for mixed vaginal infections.

title: Protective effects of alpinumisoflavone against streptozotocin-induced gestational diabetes mellitus in female rats: mechanistic insights and therapeutic potential
summary:
# Summary

Alpinumisoflavone, a natural polyphenolic compound, demonstrates significant protective effects against streptozotocin-induced gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) in female rats through multifaceted mechanisms. The compound reduces blood glucose and HbA1c levels while improving plasma insulin, thereby ameliorating the metabolic dysfunction characteristic of GDM. Mechanistically, alpinumisoflavone exerts its therapeutic effects by suppressing inflammatory and oxidative stress pathways, including downregulation of Toll-like receptor 4, NF-κB, NLRP3, and MyD88, alongside suppression of COX-2 and restoration of antioxidant enzyme levels. Additionally, alpinumisoflavone modulates lipid metabolism and adipokine signaling by downregulating fatty acid synthesis genes (SREBP-1, SCD1, FAS, and ACC) and suppressing adhesion molecules (ICAM-1 and VCAM-1), while restoring critical metabolic markers including resistin, adiponectin, and triglyceride levels. These findings suggest that alpinumisoflavone holds therapeutic potential for managing GDM by simultaneously addressing glucose homeostasis, inflammation, and oxidative stress, thereby improving fetal outcomes and reducing pregnancy complications.

title: SAT-134 Gene Expression in Response to Retinoids and Estrogen Suggests the Diversity of Cellular Environment and Cell Characteristics in Endometriosis Lesions
summary: Endometriosis is an estrogen-dependent disease in which estrogen promotes lesion growth, and hormonal therapies targeting estrogen reduction are currently used to manage symptoms. This study demonstrates that retinoids, particularly all-trans retinoic acid (ATRA) and the RARγ-specific compound CD437, induce antiproliferative activity by upregulating ATRA-responsive genes and suppressing cell proliferation in endometriotic stromal cells. However, 17β-estradiol (E2) antagonizes the therapeutic effects of ATRA by blocking its antiproliferative activity and downregulating ATRA-responsive genes, including those controlling cell proliferation. The RARγ receptor appears critical for retinoid-mediated growth inhibition, as blocking RARγ with the antagonist MM11253 abolished ATRA's antiproliferative effects. These findings reveal cellular heterogeneity in endometriotic lesions and suggest that combining retinoid therapy with estrogen-reduction strategies may improve treatment efficacy by overcoming estrogen-mediated resistance to retinoid signaling.

title: LC/ESI-MS/MS phytochemical profiling and apoptotic effect of Haloxylon scoparium leaf extract on hepatocellular carcinoma
summary:
# Summary

Haloxylon scoparium leaf extract demonstrates potent anticancer activity against hepatocellular carcinoma through multi-targeted mechanisms involving polyphenolic compounds identified via LC/ESI-MS/MS analysis. The extract, rich in quercetin and isorhamnetin glycosides, organic acids, and flavonoids, induced apoptosis in HepG2 cells (IC50: 282.4 µg/ml) by suppressing oncogenic markers (TNF-α, Bcl-2, AFP) while simultaneously enhancing pro-apoptotic factors (BAX, caspase-3, caspase-8) in diethylnitrosamine-induced mouse models. Molecular docking revealed that quercetin and isorhamnetin triglycosides bind strongly to critical apoptotic regulators—quercetin triglycoside showing particularly robust interactions with BCL-2, BAX, caspase-8, and TNF-α. Treatment with the extract significantly improved hepatic architecture, normalized liver function markers (AST, ALP, TBILR), prevented body weight loss, and reduced liver weight in diseased mice. These findings support H. scoparium's traditional medicinal uses and establish its extract as a promising natural compound for hepatocellular carcinoma intervention.

title: The role of reactive oxygen species in the pathogenesis and treatment of endometrial cancer
summary:
# Summary

This article examines the dual role of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in endometrial cancer pathogenesis and treatment, revealing a complex mechanism where ROS simultaneously promote cancer progression while offering therapeutic opportunities. At physiological levels, ROS regulate essential cellular processes including proliferation, differentiation, and immune responses through activation of transcription factors such as NF-κB, AP-1, and HIF-1α; however, pathologically elevated ROS cause DNA damage, genomic instability, and ferroptosis-independent cancer cell survival by modulating critical signaling pathways including PI3K/AKT/mTOR, MAPK, and p53. Endometrial cancer tissues exhibit characteristic oxidative stress signatures with elevated malondialdehyde and ceramides, reduced antioxidant capacity, and suppressed antitumor immunity through enhanced myeloid-derived suppressor cell function. The article identifies multiple natural compounds and polyphenols—including hinokitiol, ellipticine, oleanolic acid 3-acetate, and amentoflavone—as ROS-targeting therapeutic agents, while also highlighting emerging immunotherapies (pembrolizumab, dostarlimab) and HER2-targeted therapy (trastuzumab) for advanced disease. Additionally, methoxyeugenol demonstrates potent anti-proliferative activity in endometrial cancer cells by increasing intracellular ROS and upregulating p53, suggesting that therapeutic ROS induction represents a promising treatment strategy complementary to conventional approaches.

title: Mechanism of Jiedu Prescription in Hepatocellular Carcinoma: Integrating Network Pharmacology and Experimental Validation
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that the Jiedu Prescription (JDP), a traditional Chinese medicine formulation, effectively treats hepatocellular carcinoma through immunomodulation and signaling pathway inhibition. Network pharmacology analysis identified quercetin, luteolin, and apigenin as the key polyphenolic compounds in JDP that bind to and inhibit STAT3, a critical regulator of immune suppression in tumors. JDP downregulates the IL-6/STAT3 signaling pathway, which normally promotes T cell exhaustion, thereby increasing CD8+ T cell counts and reducing their apoptosis in the tumor microenvironment. In clinical trials, the full formulation YFJP significantly improved overall and progression-free survival in hepatocellular carcinoma patients while enhancing anti-tumor immunity. These findings validate the therapeutic potential of natural polyphenols in cancer immunotherapy by restoring CD8+ T cell function and shifting the tumor microenvironment toward a more immunostimulatory state.

title: Development and Characterization of Liposome-Based Dermocosmetic Formulations with Red Grape Pomace and Polygonum cuspidatum Extracts
summary:
# Summary

This study developed and characterized liposome-based dermocosmetic formulations incorporating extracts from red grape pomace and Polygonum cuspidatum, both rich sources of polyphenolic compounds. Chemical characterization revealed that both plant extracts contain high levels of catechin, resveratrol, kaempferol, myricetin, vanillic acid, and caffeic acid, with catechin being the most abundant polyphenol in grape pomace at 687.87 mg/kg. Encapsulation of these extracts in liposomes significantly enhanced the stability and bioavailability of the phenolic compounds by providing a protective barrier against oxidative stress, UV radiation, and temperature fluctuations while enabling controlled and sustained release of active ingredients. The liposomal formulations improved dermal penetration of antioxidants through the stratum corneum via the nanoscale size and amphiphilic properties of the liposome carriers. These polyphenol-enriched liposomal formulations leverage the traditional medicinal uses of P. cuspidatum for treating inflammatory conditions, infections, and metabolic disorders to create advanced cosmetic products with enhanced antioxidant and skin-protective properties.

title: Efficacy and Safety of Topical 5% Cannabidiol Plus Myrcene for the Treatment of Vestibulodynia: A Multi-Centric Randomized Controlled Trial
summary:
# Summary

This randomized controlled trial demonstrates that topical 5% cannabidiol (CBD) plus myrcene effectively reduces symptoms of vestibulodynia, a chronic vulvar pain condition characterized by burning sensations, dyspareunia, hyperalgesia, and allodynia. The therapeutic effects operate through multiple complementary mechanisms: CBD desensitizes TRPV1 channels located on peripheral C-fiber nociceptors and mast cells, while also downregulating pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, IL-17) and upregulating anti-inflammatory IL-10. CBD exhibits low affinity for CB1 receptors in the central nervous system, avoiding psychotropic effects, while preferentially activating CB2 receptors on peripheral nerve terminals and immune cells to modulate pain transmission and neuroinflammation. Myrcene acts synergistically by inhibiting peripheral sensitization and providing additional analgesic properties. The combination treatment significantly improved dyspareunia and pain scores compared to placebo, addressing the underlying pathophysiology of vestibulodynia, which involves heightened vulvar vestibule innervation, neuroinflammatory cell infiltration, and pelvic floor muscle overactivity.

title: Modulation of Nuclear Factor Kappa B Signaling and microRNA Profiles by Adalimumab in LPS-Stimulated Keratinocytes
summary: Adalimumab, a TNF-α-inhibiting monoclonal antibody, potently suppresses lipopolysaccharide (LPS)-induced NF-κB signaling in keratinocytes by downregulating key pathway components including IKBKB, IRAK1, TRAF2, and MAP3K7. LPS activates the NF-κB cascade through TLR4 engagement, leading to upregulation of pro-inflammatory mediators (IL-6, IL-8, CCL20) and anti-apoptotic genes (BIRC2, BIRC3, XIAP), which adalimumab reverses in a time-dependent manner. Adalimumab treatment simultaneously upregulates anti-inflammatory microRNAs (miR-1297, miR-30a, miR-95-5p, miR-125b) that target inflammatory effectors and downregulates pro-survival miRNAs (miR-4329, miR-20b-3p), creating a coordinated suppression of inflammatory signaling. This dual modulation of both NF-κB pathway genes and regulatory microRNA profiles demonstrates adalimumab's multi-layered anti-inflammatory mechanism in keratinocytes, relevant to inflammatory skin conditions where NF-κB dysregulation drives pathology.

title: How Swimming Modulates Inflammatory Pathways in Pain, Neurodegenerative, and Metabolic Disorders
summary: Swimming serves as a comprehensive anti-inflammatory intervention that modulates multiple signaling pathways implicated in neuroinflammation, metabolic dysfunction, and cognitive decline. As a non-weight-bearing exercise, swimming activates key regulatory proteins including SIRT1, which subsequently enhances BDNF and PGC-1α while suppressing NF-κB and pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6), collectively reducing systemic inflammation and oxidative stress. Swimming also upregulates protective signaling cascades through PI3K/Akt and CREB activation, alongside enhanced expression of anti-inflammatory molecules like IL-10 and endothelial nitric oxide synthase (eNOS). Clinically, regular swimming reduces symptoms across multiple disease states including cardiovascular disease, diabetes, obesity, and depression, while improving lipid profiles and reducing body fat percentage by up to 2.98%. At the molecular level, swimming exercise attenuates neurodegeneration-associated markers such as α7nAChR and NLRP1 expression while enhancing synaptic plasticity proteins (Bcl-2, PSD95, synaptophysin, NeuN). These findings establish swimming as a multitarget exercise modality capable of simultaneously addressing inflammatory, metabolic, and neurodegenerative pathways through coordinated activation of cellular defense mechanisms.

title: Study on the therapeutic effect of Viola yedoensis and Leonurus japonicus on bovine endometritis
summary: A traditional Chinese medicine formula (TCMF) composed of Viola yedoensis and Leonurus japonicus demonstrated significant therapeutic effects against bovine endometritis by suppressing key pro-inflammatory cytokines and signaling pathways. The treatment substantially reduced mRNA and protein levels of IL-1β, IL-6, IL-8, and IL-18—central mediators of the innate immune response that orchestrate inflammation and pathogen defense—compared to lipopolysaccharide (LPS)-induced inflammation models. TCMF also downregulated TNF-α, caspase-3, and PTGS2, all components of the TNF signaling pathway implicated in inflammatory cell death and tissue damage. In vivo, the herbal formula reduced clinical endometritis symptoms including uterine discharge scoring, bacterial load, and uterine weight while promoting healthy cell growth at specific concentrations. These findings suggest that polyphenolic compounds from natural plant sources can effectively mitigate endometrial inflammation through coordinated suppression of multiple pro-inflammatory cascades, offering a potential therapeutic alternative to conventional antibiotics like oxytetracycline for managing endometritis in cattle.

title: Potential role of different animal models for the evaluation of bioactive compounds
summary:
# Summary

Animal models are essential tools in biomedical research, enabling the evaluation of bioactive compounds through assessment of efficacy, safety, and ADMET properties, as well as elucidation of disease mechanisms and pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic relationships. Diverse model organisms including Drosophila, zebrafish, mice, rats, and guinea pigs are utilized across pharmacology, toxicology, cancer biology, and neuroscience. Bioactive compounds derived from plants, spices, and marine microorganisms possess antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activities that prevent oxidative stress-induced metabolic diseases, cardiovascular disease, cancer, and brain dysfunction. Specific polyphenols such as resveratrol, quercetin, and naringenin exhibit therapeutic potential against cancer and cardiovascular damage, while capsaicin from chili peppers treats diabetes, obesity, and dyslipidemia. Plant-derived natural products, including traditional medicinal plants and phytochemicals, represent essential sources for drug discovery and development, with animal model studies demonstrating that bioactive compounds can effectively modulate key molecular pathways and control disease progression.

title: Alpinumisoflavone ameliorates experimental acute reflux esophagitis in rats via regulation of inflammatory pathway
summary:
# Summary

Alpinumisoflavone, a natural polyphenolic compound derived from *Alpinia galanga*, effectively ameliorates acute reflux esophagitis in rats by suppressing multiple inflammatory and oxidative pathways. The compound significantly inhibits key inflammatory mediators including TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6, COX-2, and PGE2, while also reducing the activation of the NF-κB signaling cascade that drives the inflammatory response. Additionally, alpinumisoflavone enhances the endogenous antioxidant defense system by increasing superoxide dismutase, catalase, glutathione, and glutathione peroxidase levels, thereby reducing oxidative stress markers such as nitric oxide, hydrogen peroxide, and free iron. These combined anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects result in reduced esophageal lesion scores, improved pH levels, and restored esophageal tissue integrity. The protective mechanisms identified suggest that alpinumisoflavone may represent a promising natural therapeutic approach for managing reflux esophagitis by addressing both the inflammatory and oxidative components of disease pathogenesis.

title: Polychlorinated Biphenyls Alter Estrogen Receptor β-mediated Epigenetic Regulation, Promoting Endometriosis
summary: This study demonstrates that polychlorinated biphenyl 126 (PCB126), an endocrine-disrupting chemical, promotes endometriosis progression through estrogen receptor β (ESR2)-mediated epigenetic reprogramming. PCB126 exposure upregulates the AXL/growth arrest-specific 6 (GAS6) signaling axis, which enhances ESR2 activity and subsequently increases expression of the epigenetic modifier DNMT3A. This AXL/ESR2/DNMT3A cascade drives ectopic lesion growth by elevating cellular proliferation, inducing aberrant epithelial structures, and promoting inflammatory cytokine production through altered DNA methylation patterns. Additionally, PCB126 modulates the SRC-1 isoform/MMP-9/ESR2 axis, which further inhibits apoptosis and promotes endometriotic lesion development. Functional validation using AXL inhibitors and DNMT3A knockout mice confirmed that blocking either component of this pathway suppresses lesion growth and immune dysregulation. These findings establish a mechanistic link between environmental PCB126 exposure and endometriosis pathogenesis via epigenetic and immune reprogramming, highlighting how endocrine-disrupting chemicals promote hormone-sensitive diseases.

title: Cannabidiol sensitizes triple-negative breast cancer cells to NK cell-mediated killing via EGFR inhibition and FAS upregulation
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that cannabidiol (CBD) sensitizes triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC) cells to natural killer cell-mediated killing through dual mechanisms of EGFR inhibition and FAS upregulation. EGFR, commonly overexpressed in TNBC and associated with poor prognosis, normally drives cancer progression through activation of downstream signaling proteins including KRAS, PI3K, and AKT. CBD treatment inhibits this EGFR pathway by downregulating these oncogenic effectors while simultaneously upregulating FAS, a death receptor that NK cells activate through FAS ligand binding to trigger apoptosis. When combined with EGF stimulation, CBD-treated TNBC cells showed enhanced vulnerability to NK-92 cell cytotoxicity and reduced proliferation compared to EGF treatment alone. These findings suggest CBD represents a promising phytochemical-based therapeutic approach for TNBC by simultaneously suppressing tumor-promoting signaling and enhancing immune-mediated cancer cell death.

title: Saussurea lappa enhances folliculogenesis by suppressing F2-isoprostane, TNF-α, and apoptosis in endometriosis mice
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis disrupts folliculogenesis and causes infertility by elevating pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6), reactive oxygen species, and lipid peroxidation (measured as F2-isoprostanes), which collectively accelerate granulosa cell apoptosis through both extrinsic TNF-α/TNFR-1-mediated and intrinsic ROS/mitochondrial pathways.
