Treatment with Saussurea lappa root extract, which contains bioactive sesquiterpene lactones including costunolide, dehydrocostus lactone, and santamarin, significantly reduced F2-isoprostane levels, TNF-α, and apoptosis in follicles while increasing follicle count in an endometriosis mouse model. These sesquiterpene lactones exert multi-targeted anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects that suppress the upstream inflammatory and oxidative signals driving follicular cell death. The findings suggest that Saussurea lappa may restore oocyte quality and fertility in endometriosis by interrupting the pathological cascade linking endometrial inflammation to ovarian dysfunction.

title: Black Garlic: Evolution
journal: Journal of Agricultural and Food Chemistry
summary: Black garlic is produced through the Maillard reaction applied to fresh garlic under controlled temperature and humidity conditions, which converts unstable pungent compounds like allicin into highly bioactive, stable organosulfur compounds such as S-allyl-l-cysteine. During this thermal aging process, the garlic undergoes significant compositional changes including elevated levels of reducing sugars, phenolic compounds, melanoidins, minerals, and organic acids, while simultaneously experiencing increases in antioxidant enzyme activity including superoxide dismutase, glutathione peroxidase, and catalase. Black garlic exhibits potent anti-inflammatory activity mediated through its bioactive lipid component 2-linoleoylglycerol, which suppresses key inflammatory mediators including nitric oxide, prostaglandin E2, interleukin-6, and tumor necrosis factor-alpha by inhibiting signaling pathways such as mitogen-activated protein kinase. Beyond its antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects, black garlic demonstrates anticancer activity through suppression of cell proliferation and promotion of apoptosis in cancer cell lines via phosphoinositide 3-kinase/protein kinase B pathway inhibition. Preliminary evidence suggests black garlic extracts can suppress cell cycle progression and reduce interleukin-6 secretion in TNF-α-activated human endometrial cells, indicating potential therapeutic utility for inflammation-related conditions including endometriosis.

title: Theobroma cacao seed extracts attenuate dyslipidemia and oxidative stress in L-NAME induced hypertension in Wistar rats
summary: Theobroma cacao seed extracts, particularly the ethanol formulation, attenuate L-NAME-induced hypertension and dyslipidemia in Wistar rats through complementary antioxidant and lipid-regulating mechanisms. Both ethanol and chloroform extracts contain abundant polyphenols including quercetin, kaempferol, chlorogenic acid, and theobromine, which reduce oxidative stress by scavenging nitric oxide, inhibiting protein denaturation, and protecting red blood cells from heat-induced hemolysis. Co-treatment with cacao extracts significantly lowered atherogenic lipid markers including total cholesterol, LDL, triglycerides, and VLDL while simultaneously elevating cardioprotective HDL cholesterol and reducing lipid peroxidation markers such as malondialdehyde. The extracts restored endogenous antioxidant enzyme activity by increasing superoxide dismutase, glutathione peroxidase, and catalase, alongside enhancing nitric oxide bioavailability in L-NAME-treated animals. These findings support the therapeutic potential of cacao polyphenols for managing both cardiovascular dysfunction and oxidative stress-related pathologies through flavonoid-mediated vasodilation and lipid metabolism regulation.

title: Chitosan nanoparticles: Green synthesis, biological activities, and sustainable frontiers in targeted drug delivery and cancer nanomedicine – A comprehensive review
summary: Chitosan, a naturally derived biopolymer obtained through deacetylation of chitin found in crustacean exoskeletons and fungal cell walls, has emerged as a versatile material for biomedical applications. Chitosan nanoparticles synthesized via ionic gelation or polyelectrolyte complexation exhibit multiple therapeutic properties, including low toxicity to mammalian cells, cationic nature, and mucoadhesive qualities that make them suitable for mucosal delivery routes including nasal, oral, and ocular applications. The antimicrobial activity of chitosan is enhanced through chemical modifications, such as N-cinnamyl substitution and quaternary ammonium derivatization, which effectively inhibit pathogenic bacteria including Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Escherichia coli, and Staphylococcus aureus. Chitosan-based polymeric carriers effectively deliver diverse therapeutic agents including anticancer drugs, peptides, growth factors, and anti-inflammatory medications, with applications in cancer therapy, tissue engineering, bone regeneration, and wound healing. Chemical stability and nanoparticle formation are optimized through crosslinking with multivalent ions such as sodium tri-polyphosphate, citrate, and sulfate, while monovalent salts enhance colloidal stability. These properties position chitosan nanoparticles as promising platforms for targeted nanomedicine and regenerative medicine applications.

title: Effects of a traditionally prepared Glycyrrhiza glabra root-based beverage on female reproductive function in rats
summary: A traditionally prepared Glycyrrhiza glabra (licorice) root-based beverage demonstrates significant modulatory effects on female reproductive function in rats through both hormonal and uterine mechanisms. The beverage upregulates circulating levels of luteinizing hormone (LH) and follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) while simultaneously increasing primary and antral follicles, suggesting enhanced ovarian folliculogenesis. Key bioactive phytoestrogenic compounds in licorice—including glabrene, isoliquiritigenin, and glabridin—bind to human estrogen receptors and exert estrogen-like activity, while inhibiting steroidogenic enzymes (3β-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase and 17β-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase) and upregulating aromatase activity. The beverage markedly reduces both spontaneous and oxytocin-induced uterine contractions through mechanisms involving calcium channels, cyclooxygenase, and nitric oxide pathways, effects mediated by isoliquiritigenin's spasmolytic activity. These combined actions—enhanced gonadotropin signaling coupled with uterine relaxation—suggest therapeutic potential for endometriosis, polycystic ovary syndrome, and dysmenorrhea, though safety considerations regarding glycyrrhizin-associated mineralocorticoid effects warrant attention in clinical applications.

title: Anti-ovarian cancer effects of forsythiaside A: insights from in vitro and in vivo studies
summary: Forsythiaside A (FSA), a polyphenolic natural compound, demonstrates potent anti-ovarian cancer activity through multiple mechanistic pathways in both in vitro and in vivo models. In ovarian cancer cell lines (SK-OV-3 and OVCAR3), FSA dose- and time-dependently reduced cell proliferation, induced G1 phase cell cycle arrest, and inhibited cell migration and invasion. The compound promotes apoptotic and necrotic cell death by upregulating pro-apoptotic proteins (BAX, CASP3, BID, and CYCS) while downregulating the anti-apoptotic protein BCL2, thereby shifting the balance toward programmed cell death. In vivo xenograft studies confirmed FSA's tumor-suppressive effects, and transcriptomic analysis revealed its modulation of cell cycle control and apoptosis pathways alongside broader signaling mechanisms including PPAR-γ/RXR-α, TLR4/MAPK/NF-κB, and immune checkpoint inhibition via KLRB1-CLEC2D. Beyond oncology, FSA exhibits multi-system protective effects through anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antiviral properties, positioning it as a promising candidate for further therapeutic development in ovarian cancer and fibrotic disorders.

title: Butyrate Enhances Antimicrobial Defence in Chicken Macrophages Through Reactive Oxygen Species Generation and Autophagy Activation
summary:
# Summary

Sodium butyrate, a short-chain fatty acid produced by gut microbiota, enhances the antimicrobial capacity of chicken macrophages against major bacterial pathogens including *Escherichia coli*, *Salmonella Typhimurium*, *Pseudomonas aeruginosa*, and *Staphylococcus aureus*. This antimicrobial enhancement is mechanistically dependent on two interconnected pathways: butyrate-induced reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation and autophagy activation through mTOR pathway inhibition. Pharmacological blocking of either ROS production or autophagy using N-acetyl-L-cysteine or chloroquine, respectively, significantly reduced butyrate's protective effects, demonstrating that both processes are essential for bacterial clearance. Furthermore, mTOR inhibition with rapamycin synergized with butyrate priming to boost antimicrobial activity, while mTOR activation with L-leucine counteracted these benefits, confirming the regulatory role of mTOR signaling. These findings suggest that butyrate's pleiotropic effects—mediated through G-protein-coupled receptor activation (GPR109A, GPR41, GPR43) and resulting in anti-inflammatory responses—position it as a promising immunomodulatory agent for enhancing macrophage-mediated defense against bacterial infections in poultry and potentially other biological systems.

title: HMGB-1 Increases Proinflammatory Reaction via TLR4 in Human Granulosa Cells of Endometriosis
summary: Oxidative stress is a central driver of endometriosis pathogenesis that triggers the release of high-mobility group box 1 (HMGB-1), a damage-associated molecular pattern molecule acting as a proinflammatory cytokine in granulosa cells. Hydrogen peroxide-induced oxidative stress upregulates both HMGB-1 and its receptor TLR4, leading to HMGB-1-TLR4 interaction that activates the NF-κB signaling pathway and amplifies proinflammatory cytokine production including IL-1β and IL-6. This oxidative-inflammatory cascade simultaneously impairs granulosa cell function by promoting apoptosis through increased Bax and cleaved caspase-3 expression while decreasing estradiol secretion, thereby compromising oocyte competence and steroidogenesis. The HMGB-1-TLR4-NF-κB axis creates a positive feedback loop where increased HMGB-1 release further exacerbates oxidative stress, perpetuating local inflammation within the follicular microenvironment. These findings identify the HMGB-1-TLR4 signaling pathway as a critical mechanism linking oxidative stress to both inflammatory responses and reduced fertility in endometriosis patients, suggesting that targeting this pathway may offer therapeutic potential to restore granulosa cell function and reproductive outcomes.

title: The mechanisms of specialized pro-resolving mediators in pain relief: neuro-immune and neuroglial regulations
summary:
# Article Summary: Specialized Pro-Resolving Mediators in Pain Relief

Specialized pro-resolving mediators (SPMs)—bioactive lipids derived from omega-3 and omega-6 fatty acids—represent a novel class of endogenous analgesic compounds that alleviate chronic pain through distinct neuro-immune and neuroglial mechanisms. Specific SPMs including Resolvin D1 (RvD1), Resolvin E1 (RvE1), Maresin 1 (MaR1), and Protectin D1 (PD1) demonstrate potent pain-reducing effects across multiple pain models, including neuropathic, inflammatory, visceral, and post-operative pain, while functioning through G-protein-coupled receptors (ALX/FPR2, ChemR23, GPR32, GPR18, GPR37L1, LGR6) to suppress inflammatory cytokines and modulate transient receptor potential channels. Notably, RvE1 is twice as potent as RvD1 in reducing pain, and resolvins outperform conventional analgesics like gabapentin and morphine, with the critical distinction that SPMs do not alter baseline pain sensitivity in healthy individuals—limiting unwanted side effects. Remarkably effective preclinical results include MaR1's acceleration of pain recovery in vulvodynia models and 3-oxa-PD1n-3 DPA's rapid anti-nociceptive effects in diabetic neuropathic pain within one hour, while prophylactic PD1 administration during surgery completely prevents chronic constriction injury-induced mechanical hyperalgesia for weeks. These findings establish SPMs as promising therapeutic candidates for managing diverse pain conditions through resolution-based mechanisms rather than conventional suppression approaches.

title: Metabolomics reveals the therapeutic efficacy of liposomal resvida in endometrial cancer through regulating autophagy-related gene expression
summary: Oestradiol valerate induces endometrial cancer through activation of the PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling pathway, which suppresses autophagy and promotes cell survival while simultaneously triggering oxidative stress and downregulating the tumor suppressor p53. Liposomal resveratrol, a polyphenol compound, reverses these pathogenic changes by inhibiting mTOR signaling, restoring p53 expression, and downregulating cancer biomarkers including CA-125, effectively restoring autophagy-dependent cell death mechanisms. The metabolomic analysis reveals that oestradiol valerate dysregulates lipid and amino acid metabolism through mTOR-dependent pathways, increasing hexadecanoic and octadecanoic acids while decreasing protective amino acids like proline and serine. Liposomal resveratrol's therapeutic efficacy operates by suppressing the PI3K/AKT/mTOR axis, reducing oxidative stress biomarkers, and upregulating critical autophagy regulators including LKB1, ULK1/2, ATG4, ATG7, and ATG10 through p53 reactivation. These findings demonstrate that liposomal delivery of resveratrol provides superior therapeutic benefit compared to conventional resveratrol or immunotherapy alone by modulating both autophagy gene expression and metabolic dysregulation associated with endometrial cancer development.

title: Selenium nanoparticles mitigate Cyclophosphamide-Induced premature ovarian failures in mice by activating PI3K/AKT signaling pathway and inhibiting ferroptosis
summary: Selenium nanoparticles (SeNPs) effectively mitigate cyclophosphamide-induced premature ovarian failure in mice through activation of the PI3K/AKT signaling pathway and inhibition of ferroptosis. SeNPs treatment restored critical reproductive hormones, increasing estradiol and anti-Müllerian hormone while reducing follicle-stimulating hormone and luteinizing hormone levels, alongside restoring ovarian weight and increasing follicles across all developmental stages while reducing atretic follicles. At the molecular level, SeNPs attenuated cyclophosphamide-induced DNA damage, apoptosis, and oxidative stress in granulosa cells by restoring antioxidant enzyme activities and reversing the suppression of steroidogenesis-related genes downregulated by the chemotherapeutic agent. In cultured granulosa cells exposed to cyclophosphamide's active metabolite 4-HC, SeNPs enhanced cell viability and proliferation, normalized cell cycle progression, and restored PI3K/AKT phosphorylation, thereby counteracting chemotherapy-induced reproductive toxicity. These findings demonstrate that SeNPs harness selenoprotein-mediated antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and cell survival mechanisms to preserve ovarian function and fertility in the context of chemotherapy exposure.

title: Mechanism of active component β-sitosterol from Myristica fragrans inducing apoptosis in bladder cancer cells via regulating the BCL-2/BAX/caspase-3 pathway
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that β-sitosterol, a major bioactive phytosterol isolated from nutmeg (*Myristica fragrans*), suppresses bladder cancer progression through modulation of the BCL-2/BAX/Caspase-3 apoptotic pathway. β-sitosterol significantly reduced cell viability and proliferation in T24 and 5637 bladder cancer cells in a dose-dependent manner while inhibiting colony formation and cell migration. The compound induces apoptosis by upregulating pro-apoptotic proteins BAX and cleaved Caspase-3 while downregulating anti-apoptotic BCL-2, as confirmed by Western blot analysis. Molecular docking studies revealed strong binding interactions between β-sitosterol and both BCL-2 and Caspase-3, supporting a direct mechanism of action. Network pharmacology analysis identified 284 overlapping targets between nutmeg and bladder cancer, with enriched pathways involving apoptosis, protein phosphorylation, and EGFR signaling, alongside potential indirect modulation of the PI3K-Akt survival pathway. These findings provide novel mechanistic insight into the therapeutic potential of this natural compound as a complementary strategy against bladder cancer.

title: Bioinformatics analysis for Taohe Chengqi Decoction in treating diabetic kidney disease and cutaneous pruritus based on the theory of “same treatment for different diseases”
summary:
# Summary

Taohe Chengqi Decoction (THCQD), a traditional Chinese herbal formula, demonstrates therapeutic efficacy against both diabetic kidney disease and cutaneous pruritus through multi-target mechanisms involving inflammatory and metabolic pathways. The decoction's active ingredient beta-sitosterol exerts protective effects by inhibiting the IKKβ/NF-κB and JNK signaling pathways, reducing pro-inflammatory cytokines TNF-α and IL-6, and suppressing AKT and PI3K expression to enhance insulin sensitivity and glucose uptake. Key therapeutic targets identified through protein-protein interaction analysis include TNF, ALB, IL1B, AKT1, and TP53, with involvement of AGE-RAGE signaling, PI3K-Akt, MAPK, and TNF signaling pathways in disease treatment. The n-butanol extract component specifically inhibits inflammation and epithelial-mesenchymal transition while reducing extracellular matrix deposition, thereby exerting anti-renal fibrosis effects. For cutaneous pruritus, THCQD modulates itch signaling through AKT1-mediated TRPV1 phosphorylation in sensory neurons and suppresses IL-31 cytokine production triggered by IL-4 and IL-33. This integrated bioinformatics analysis supports the traditional concept of "same treatment for different diseases" by demonstrating shared molecular targets and signaling mechanisms underlying THCQD's therapeutic benefits across these two disease conditions.

title: Mechanism of Shenghua decoction in the treatment of primary dysmenorrhea based on network pharmacology and molecular docking analysis technology
summary:
# Summary

Shenghua Decoction (SHD), a traditional Chinese medicine formula, treats primary dysmenorrhea through multiple polyphenolic compounds that target key inflammatory and signaling pathways. The main active ingredients—liquiritigenin, ferulic acid, and senkyunolide I—bind directly to PTGS2 (COX-2), the rate-limiting enzyme for prostaglandin synthesis, thereby reducing the abnormal production of PGF2α and PGE2 that drives uterine hypercontractility and pain. Additionally, liquiritigenin and glycyrrhetinic acid interact with AKT1, a serine/threonine kinase that controls cellular metabolism and proliferation; inhibiting AKT1 reduces dysmenorrhea symptoms by suppressing the PI3K-Akt signaling pathway, which downregulates downstream COX-2 and VEGF expression. SHD also alleviates dysmenorrhea by promoting blood circulation and improving uterine contraction while modulating inflammatory mediators including IL-6 and TNF through involvement in steroid hormone biosynthesis and TNF signaling pathways. These findings demonstrate that SHD's therapeutic efficacy rivals conventional NSAIDs through coordinated suppression of prostaglandin-driven pathology and inflammatory gene expression networks.

title: Effects of omega-3 fatty acids on chronic pain: a systematic review and meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review and meta-analysis demonstrates that omega-3 fatty acids provide moderate, clinically significant reductions in chronic pain intensity across multiple conditions, with benefits particularly pronounced in rheumatoid arthritis, migraines, and mixed chronic pain disorders. The analgesic mechanisms involve both peripheral and central nervous system effects: EPA and DHA downregulate pro-inflammatory mediators including prostaglandin E₂ and leukotriene B₄, while simultaneously promoting synthesis of specialized pro-resolving mediators such as resolvins, protectins, and maresins. Within the nervous system, omega-3 fatty acids suppress microglial activation and attenuate central sensitization and neuroinflammation, likely through modulation of the SIRT1–HMGB1–NF-κB pathway. Analysis of 41 randomized controlled trials revealed that lower doses (≤1.35 g/day) were more effective than higher doses, with pain relief becoming noticeable at one month and substantially improving by six months. Both short-term and long-term supplementation showed efficacy, with consistent benefits observed across geographic regions and supplement formulations including fish oil and mixed formulations.

title: An up-to-date overview of baicalein and its biological and pharmacological activities
summary: Baicalein, a flavone compound abundant in Scutellaria baicalensis and Scutellaria lateriflora plants, is biosynthesized from the amino acid phenylalanine through a sequential enzymatic pathway involving phenylalanine ammonialyase, cinnamate 4-hydroxylase, cinnamate-CoA ligase, chalcone synthase, chalcone isomerase, flavone synthase II, and flavone 6-hydroxylase. This polyphenolic compound exhibits multiple beneficial pharmacological activities including potent antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects that contribute to its therapeutic potential. Baicalein demonstrates significant anti-cancer activity and has gained considerable attention as an antitumor agent for treating malignant tumors, with notable advantages including high efficacy and low toxicity. The compound's diverse biological properties position it as a promising natural therapeutic agent with applications across multiple disease contexts, making it a valuable subject for continued biomedical research and drug development.

title: Curcumin, a bioactive supplement for premenstrual syndrome and dysmenorrhea: A systematic review of randomised clinical trials
journal: European Journal of Obstetrics & Gynecology and Reproductive Biology: X
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review of ten randomized controlled trials demonstrates that curcumin, a polyphenolic compound extracted from turmeric, effectively treats premenstrual syndrome and dysmenorrhea through its anti-inflammatory, immunomodulatory, and antioxidant properties. Six of the ten trials reported significant reductions in PMS and dysmenorrhea symptoms, with one study showing 56.1% improvement in PMS alleviation compared to 36.1% in controls. At the mechanistic level, curcumin treatment decreased key inflammatory markers including high-sensitivity CRP, nitric oxide metabolites, and serum IgE levels while increasing brain-derived neurotrophic factor and vitamin D status, addressing pathophysiological mechanisms implicated in menstrual pain and premenstrual symptoms. These findings position curcumin as a promising natural alternative to conventional therapies such as NSAIDs, hormonal contraceptives, and SSRIs, which carry significant adverse effects including gastrointestinal issues, thromboembolic risk, nausea, and sleep disturbances. The safety profile of curcumin appears favorable, with no significant effects on hematologic parameters, iron metabolism, or bleeding time, though one study noted decreased nocturnal sleep duration warranting further investigation.

title: Therapeutic effect of baicalein as an antiparasitic agent against Toxoplasma gondii in vitro and in vivo
journal: Journal of Zhejiang University. Science. B
summary: Baicalein, a polyphenolic compound, demonstrates potent antiparasitic activity against Toxoplasma gondii both in vitro and in vivo, with an IC50 of 6.457×10⁻⁵ mol/L and a capacity to reduce parasite invasion rate to 33.56%. The compound inhibits both T. gondii invasion and intracellular proliferation while simultaneously suppressing excessive inflammatory responses in infected mice. In vivo studies revealed that baicalein treatment significantly extended survival time in T. gondii-infected mice and substantially reduced parasite burdens in the liver and spleen compared to controls. Beyond toxoplasmosis, baicalein exhibits broad-spectrum antiparasitic activity against other protozoan parasites including Leishmania and Cryptosporidium, positioning it as a promising therapeutic alternative to conventional drugs like pyrimethamine and sulfadiazine. These findings suggest baicalein's dual mechanism of action—direct antiparasitic effects combined with immunomodulatory properties—makes it a valuable candidate for treating toxoplasmosis and potentially other parasitic infections.

title: Protective effects of 20(R) ginsenoside Rg3 on DBP-induced reproductive injury in mice through Nrf2/ARE pathway
summary: Di-n-butyl phthalate (DBP), an environmental endocrine disruptor, causes male reproductive dysfunction through multiple pathways including reduced testosterone synthesis, increased spermatogenic cell apoptosis, and downregulation of the Nrf2/ARE antioxidant signaling pathway. 20(R) ginsenoside Rg3, a natural polyphenol compound, protects against DBP-induced reproductive injury by activating the Nrf2/ARE pathway, which upregulates expression of the antioxidant response genes NQO1 and StAR, the latter being the rate-limiting enzyme in testosterone synthesis. Treatment with ginsenoside Rg3 restored testicular tissue structure, increased sperm density and motility, and normalized hormonal levels (testosterone and FSH) in DBP-exposed mice. The protective mechanism involves both transcriptional upregulation and increased phosphorylation of Nrf2 and its downstream effectors, enhancing cellular antioxidant capacity and reducing oxidative stress-mediated damage. These findings demonstrate that natural compounds targeting the Nrf2/ARE pathway represent a promising therapeutic strategy for counteracting endocrine disruptor-induced male infertility.

title: Phenothiazine-Based
summary:
# Summary

This study develops phenothiazine-based photoacoustic imaging (PAI) contrast agents that leverage the twisted intramolecular charge transfer (TICT) effect to enhance imaging performance. The lead compound DHU-PA-1 suppresses fluorescence through TICT-induced conformational changes, redirecting excited-state energy toward photothermal conversion and photoacoustic signal generation rather than radiative decay, achieving superior photoacoustic signals compared to the parent methylene blue. DHU-PA-1 demonstrates excellent biosafety, thermal stability over multiple heating cycles, and prolonged in vivo retention due to optimized water solubility that facilitates renal clearance. A derivative probe, DHU-PAOCl-1, responds selectively to hypochlorous acid (HOCl)—a reactive oxygen species marking inflammation and disease—undergoing molecular conversion to release DHU-PA-1 upon activation, enabling precise real-time visualization of endogenous HOCl at inflammatory sites with a detection limit of 11.88 nM. These structurally tunable phenothiazine-based agents represent an advance in PAI contrast agent design, addressing the fundamental trade-off between fluorescence and photoacoustic conversion while providing a platform for biomarker-responsive imaging applications.

title: Cervicovaginal microbiome alters transcriptomic and chromatin accessibility signatures across cervicovaginal epithelial barriers
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that the cervicovaginal microbiome composition directly shapes the transcriptomic and epigenomic landscape of cervicovaginal epithelial cells through distinct bacterial metabolite-mediated mechanisms. Lactobacillus-dominated microbiomes, particularly L. crispatus, promote beneficial epigenetic modifications and reduce chromatin accessibility while downregulating pro-inflammatory genes (S100A8, LCN2, CCL20) and maintaining epithelial barrier integrity through lactic acid production. Conversely, lactobacilli-deficient microbiomes enriched with Gardnerella vaginalis trigger pathogenic immune responses by upregulating inflammatory cytokines (IL-8, IL-6, TNFα) and antimicrobial peptides, inducing epithelial cell death and dysregulating innate immunity through NF-κB pathway activation. These microbiome-induced transcriptional changes are associated with downstream reproductive health consequences, including infertility, preterm birth, sexually transmitted infections, and endometriosis. The findings establish that optimizing the cervicovaginal microbiome composition could represent an innovative therapeutic strategy to prevent reproductive disorders through epigenomic and immunological modulation.

title: In Vivo Studies on the Interaction Between Orally Administered Nitrite and Omeprazole: Beyond Proton-Catalyzed S-Nitrosation
summary: This study examines how oral nitrite administration and the proton pump inhibitor omeprazole interact to modulate hepatic oxidative stress and nitrosation pathways. Nitrite exerts antioxidant effects by increasing liver concentrations of nitrite and nitroso species (RxNO), elevating non-protein thiols, and upregulating the antioxidant enzyme microsomal glutathione S-transferase 1 (Mgst1), while simultaneously decreasing superoxide and hydrogen peroxide production. Omeprazole, despite possessing some antioxidant properties, demonstrates pro-oxidant effects by increasing gastric pH, which inhibits the gastric formation of nitroso species from nitrite, and by elevating xanthine oxidoreductase expression in the liver. Co-administration of omeprazole substantially attenuates nitrite's beneficial effects, preventing the elevation of hepatic thiols and nitrosation of hepatic proteins while blocking the blood pressure-lowering effects of oral nitrite and promoting oxidative stress through increased xanthine oxidoreductase activity. Nitrite treatment counteracts omeprazole's pro-oxidant effects through Mgst1 upregulation and enhanced hepatic glutathione production. These findings demonstrate a complex pharmacological interaction where nitrite's gastric conversion to nitrosative species and subsequent hepatic antioxidant signaling are critically dependent on stomach acid levels, with important implications for patients using proton pump inhibitors.

title: Resveratrol Alleviates Effects of LPS on Estrogen Synthesis, Oxidative Stress, Inflammation, and Pyroptosis of Goat Granulosa Cells by Activating the PPARG/NRF2/HO-1 Signaling Pathway
summary:
# Summary

Resveratrol, a natural polyphenol with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, protects goat granulosa cells from lipopolysaccharide (LPS)-induced damage through activation of the PPARG/NRF2/HO-1 signaling pathway. LPS stimulates oxidative stress and inflammation in granulosa cells by increasing reactive oxygen species (ROS) production, malondialdehyde (MDA) content, and pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α), while simultaneously triggering pyroptosis via the NLRP3/Caspase-1/GSDMD pathway and reducing estrogen synthesis. Resveratrol counteracts these effects by inhibiting ROS and MDA production, suppressing inflammatory mediators and pyroptotic markers, and restoring antioxidant enzyme activity through PPARG activation, which subsequently upregulates the NRF2/HO-1 pathway. The PPARG/NRF2/HO-1 axis protects granulosa cells against oxidative stress and inflammation, with pharmacological blockade of PPARG (using GW9662) completely abolishing resveratrol's protective effects. These findings suggest that resveratrol may represent a therapeutic approach to preserve granulosa cell function and reproductive health by targeting the interconnected mechanisms of oxidative stress, inflammation, and pyroptosis.

title: Development of a Collagen–Cerium Oxide Nanohydrogel for Wound Healing: In Vitro and In Vivo Evaluation
summary:
# Summary

Researchers developed collagen-cerium oxide nanoparticle hydrogels designed to enhance wound healing by combining the structural and cellular guidance properties of collagen with the multifunctional benefits of cerium oxide nanoparticles. Collagen serves as the extracellular matrix scaffold that promotes fibroblast and mesenchymal stem cell migration while fostering the appearance of pro-regenerative M2 macrophages and reducing inflammation, whereas cerium oxide nanoparticles provide antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, antibacterial, and pro-angiogenic effects through redox modulation. In vitro studies using human fibroblasts demonstrated that optimized collagen-nanocerium composites enhanced cellular proliferation and metabolic activity while reducing DNA damage and providing substantial protection against oxidative stress (31-49% protection against hydrogen peroxide and 15-23% against superoxide radicals). In vivo evaluation in full-thickness skin wounds of Wistar rats showed the composites significantly accelerated healing, improved tissue regeneration, and increased fibroblast infiltration compared to untreated controls and the reference drug dexpanthenol. The developed hydrogels exhibited a synergistic combination of antioxidant, antimutagenic, anti-inflammatory, and pro-regenerative properties, with confirmed chemical interactions between components and no observable nanoparticle agglomeration.

title: The Potential of Bioactive Plant Phytochemicals, Pro-Resolving Anti-Inflammatory Lipids, and Statins in the Inhibition of Intervertebral Disc Degeneration, Low Back Pain Resolution, Disc Functional Repair, and Promotion of Intervertebral Disc Regeneration
summary:
# Summary

Intervertebral disc degeneration is driven by elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines IL-6 and IL-8, matrix metalloprotease-mediated extracellular matrix breakdown, and apoptosis of disc cells. This article demonstrates that diverse bioactive plant phytochemicals including flavonoids, terpenoids, alkaloids, and polyphenolics effectively inhibit intervertebral disc degeneration through multiple complementary mechanisms. Specific compounds like quercetin, baicalein, naringin, and icariin suppress inflammatory pathways (NF-κB, p38 MAPK, NLRP3 inflammasome) while simultaneously promoting cell survival through SIRT1-autophagy, AMPK/SIRT1, and Nrf2/HO-1 pathway activation. Pro-resolving anti-inflammatory lipids (lipoxin A4, resolvin D1, protectins, maresins) and statins further reduce inflammation and promote tissue repair by upregulating restorative factors like IGF-1, TGF-β, and matrix components such as aggrecan and type II collagen. These natural compounds represent promising therapeutic candidates for treating disc degeneration and low back pain while promoting functional disc regeneration.

title: Mechanistic Insights into the Anti-Inflammatory and Anti-Proliferative Effects of Selected Medicinal Plants in Endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is characterized by chronic inflammation and abnormal endometrial cell proliferation, leading to clinical manifestations including dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, infertility, and chronic pelvic pain, with associations to autoimmune disorders such as rheumatoid arthritis and psoriasis. The disease pathophysiology involves a pathological feedback loop wherein elevated prostaglandin E2 (PGE2) and cyclooxygenase-2 (COX-2) enhance aromatase expression and local estrogen synthesis, which further amplifies PGE2 production while suppressing apoptosis and promoting cellular proliferation and angiogenesis. Medicinal plant polyphenols, particularly curcumin from turmeric and shogaol from ginger, effectively counteract these mechanisms by downregulating COX-2 and aromatase, activating the antioxidant Nrf2 pathway, suppressing pro-inflammatory cytokines including IL-6 and TNF-α, and inducing caspase-dependent apoptosis through modulation of the PI3K/Akt pathway and Bcl-2 family proteins. Additional compounds such as isoliquiritigenin from liquorice and silymarin from milk thistle inhibit inflammatory signaling cascades (HMGB1-TLR4-NF-κB) and reduce pro-inflammatory mediators while enhancing antioxidant capacity. In vivo studies demonstrate that curcumin and ginger treatments reduce endometrial lesion burden and restore fertility, establishing plant-derived polyphenols and flavonoids as promising therapeutic agents for managing endometriosis through multi-target inhibition of inflammation, proliferation, and angiogenesis.

title: Neuroprotective Pathway Modulation by a Novel Coriandrum sativum, N-Acetylcysteine and Glutathione-Based Formulation: Insights from In Vitro 3D Models
summary:
# Summary

This study evaluated IperALA® Forte, a nutraceutical formulation combining Coriandrum sativum extract, N-acetylcysteine, and glutathione, for neuroprotective potential using 3D in vitro models relevant to chronic pain and neuroinflammation. The formulation significantly enhanced cell viability while reducing reactive oxygen species, pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNFα and IL-1β), and promoting analgesic pathways by increasing cannabinoid receptor CB2R and GABA expression. IperALA® Forte also preserved intestinal barrier integrity and blood-brain barrier function, improving tight junction protein expression (claudin-5) and component permeability compared to individual agents alone. Among the individual ingredients, the Coriandrum sativum extract and N-acetylcysteine demonstrated the most robust antioxidant and cytoprotective effects, suggesting their synergistic contribution to the formulation's superior neuroprotective properties. These findings support the potential of polyphenol-rich natural compounds combined with antioxidant molecules as therapeutic agents for managing chronic pain conditions associated with central sensitization and neuroinflammation.

title: Cardiopulmonary and Immune Alterations in the Ts65Dn Mouse Model of Down Syndrome and Modulation by Epigallocatechin-3-Gallate-Enriched Green Tea Extract
summary:
# Summary

This study investigated cardiopulmonary and immune dysfunction in Ts65Dn mice, a model of Down syndrome that manifests cardiac anomalies, craniofacial malformations, and cognitive impairment alongside reduced B-cell lymphocytes and right ventricular hypertrophy. Treatment with epigallocatechin-3-gallate (EGCG)-enriched green tea extract, a polyphenol with known antioxidative and antiatherogenic properties, successfully normalized arterial and right ventricular morphology while improving B-cell lymphocyte numbers through mechanisms involving vascular endothelial growth factor interaction and inhibition of nuclear factor of activated T-cells signaling. However, EGCG administration induced dose-dependent adverse pulmonary effects, including transient lung immaturity, persistent decreases in lung function, and airway hyperreactivity, revealing a critical therapeutic limitation. These findings demonstrate that while green tea polyphenols can ameliorate certain cardiovascular and immune deficits in Down syndrome models, careful optimization of dosing is essential to avoid iatrogenic respiratory complications.

title: Inhibitory Effects of Syringic Acid on Endometrial Cancer Cell Growth and Migration and Its Synergistic Suppression with Doxorubicin
summary: Syringic acid, a naturally occurring polyphenol, demonstrates potent anti-proliferative effects against endometrial cancer RL95-2 cells by inducing apoptosis through activation of intrinsic and extrinsic caspase pathways while simultaneously suppressing anti-apoptotic factors like Bcl-2 and Bcl-XL. The compound arrests cell cycle progression by downregulating cyclin D and upregulating tumor suppressors p21 and p27, and effectively inhibits cell migration by reducing expression of matrix metalloproteinases and β-catenin. Notably, syringic acid exhibits cell-type-dependent effects: in cancer cells it promotes oxidative stress by inhibiting the Nrf2 antioxidant pathway and activating pro-inflammatory NF-κB signaling, whereas in normal human endometrial stromal cells it attenuates lipopolysaccharide-induced inflammation and oxidative stress while enhancing antioxidant enzyme activity. Combination treatment with doxorubicin produces synergistic suppression of endometrial cancer cell proliferation and migration, with both agents converging on similar apoptotic mechanisms. These findings suggest syringic acid represents a promising natural compound for selective targeting of endometrial cancer while potentially protecting normal endometrial cells from inflammatory damage.

title: Alpha-linolenic acid reshapes the gastrointestinal ecology to enhance immune function in dairy cows: insights from microbiome and metabolome profiling
summary:
# Summary

Alpha-linolenic acid (ALA), an essential omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acid, reshapes the gastrointestinal microbiota and enhances immune function through multiple interconnected mechanisms. High dietary ALA increases beneficial bacteria including Eubacterium coprostanoligenes group and Ruminococcus while reducing proinflammatory metabolites such as dodecanoic acid, myristic acid, prostaglandin I2, and leukotriene C4. The immunomodulatory effects operate primarily through competitive inhibition of arachidonic acid metabolism, where ALA competes with arachidonic acid for shared metabolic enzymes (cyclooxygenase and lipoxygenase), thereby reducing the production of pro-inflammatory eicosanoids and promoting conversion to specialized pro-resolving mediators like resolvins. ALA-derived metabolites suppress proinflammatory responses by downregulating TNF-α and IL-6 through NF-κB signaling while simultaneously promoting M2 macrophages via G protein-coupled receptor 40 activation. Notably, in endometriosis models, ALA attenuates endotoxin levels and peritoneal inflammation by restoring gut barrier integrity, suggesting therapeutic potential for inflammatory reproductive disorders beyond ruminant health applications.

title: Curcumin's Therapeutic Potential in Ovarian Cancer: Current Insights and Future Perspectives
summary: Curcumin, a natural polyphenol derived from turmeric (Curcuma longa), demonstrates potent therapeutic potential against ovarian cancer through multiple molecular mechanisms and can be used as an adjuvant to enhance standard chemotherapy regimens including platinum agents, paclitaxel, and bevacizumab. The compound exerts its anticancer effects by inhibiting key proliferation pathways (EGFR, AP-1, PI3K/Akt), disrupting cell cycle progression through downregulation of cyclins D1 and E, suppressing angiogenesis via VEGF inhibition, and triggering apoptosis by modulating pro- and anti-apoptotic proteins (upregulating p53 and Bax; downregulating BCL-2, Bcl-XL, and Survivin). Curcumin also possesses anti-inflammatory properties that inhibit NF-κB, TNF, IL-6, and COX-2, while regulating microRNAs and long non-coding RNAs to overcome drug resistance and reduce metastatic potential through MMP-9 inhibition. A major clinical limitation is curcumin's poor water solubility and bioavailability; however, nanoformulations including PLGA nanoparticles, nano lipid carriers, and folic acid-conjugated nanomicelles significantly enhance cellular uptake and therapeutic efficacy while reducing treatment-associated toxicities. These advances position nanocurcumin as a promising complementary strategy for ovarian cancer therapy, particularly in patients with genetic vulnerabilities such as BRCA1/BRCA2 mutations or those developing chemotherapy resistance.

title: Impacts of Andrographis paniculata supplementation on health and productivity in monogastric farm animals: A comprehensive review
summary:
# Summary

Andrographis paniculata and its bioactive constituents, particularly andrographolide, exert multifaceted protective effects in farm animals through potent anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and immunomodulatory mechanisms. Andrographolide suppresses key inflammatory signaling pathways including NF-κB and JAK-STAT, thereby reducing production of pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6, IL-1β) and inhibiting COX-2, while also promoting macrophage polarization toward anti-inflammatory phenotypes. The compound demonstrates broad-spectrum antiviral activity against influenza, hepatitis C, dengue, HIV, and SARS-CoV-2 through multiple mechanisms including direct viral protein synthesis inhibition and enhanced interferon production. In addition to anti-inflammatory properties, andrographolide displays anticancer potential via apoptosis promotion and angiogenesis inhibition, alongside hepatoprotective effects mediated by enhanced antioxidant enzyme activity. When administered as a dietary supplement, Andrographis paniculata improves intestinal morphology and nutrient absorption, selectively inhibits pathogenic bacteria while promoting beneficial lactic acid bacteria, and significantly enhances humoral and cellular immune responses including antibody titers and immune organ enlargement, ultimately improving productivity parameters such as feed conversion and egg production in monogastric farm animals.

title: Astragalin: A promising herbal compound with broad anticancer potential (Review)
summary:
# Summary

Astragalin, a polyphenolic compound derived from natural sources, demonstrates broad-spectrum anticancer potential through multiple interconnected molecular mechanisms. The compound potently inhibits cancer cell proliferation and induces apoptosis by activating pro-apoptotic caspases (3, 6, 7, and 8) while simultaneously downregulating anti-apoptotic factors like BCL-2 and upregulating pro-apoptotic proteins BAX and BAD. Astragalin exerts its anticancer effects through modulation of numerous signaling pathways, including JAK/STAT, PI3K/AKT/mTOR, NF-κB, ERK-1/2, and p38 MAPK, as well as through direct binding to estrogen receptor α and HER2. Additionally, astragalin suppresses cell cycle progression by inhibiting cyclin-dependent kinases (CDK2 and CDK4) and promoting cell cycle arrest at the G2/M phase, while also reducing glucose metabolism through downregulation of metabolic enzymes. These multifaceted mechanisms, combined with the compound's ability to modulate microRNAs and upregulate tumor suppressor genes, position astragalin as a promising herbal-derived therapeutic candidate for cancer treatment with potentially favorable safety profiles.

title: Macrophage targeted cellular microparticles synergize anti-tuberculosis therapy combining CircRNA-mediated host immune defense and antibiotic killing
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that galactose-modified cellular microparticles co-delivering circTRAPPC6B and rifampicin (Gal-CircRNA@Rif@MPs) synergistically enhance tuberculosis treatment by targeting macrophages and activating both innate immunity and antibiotic killing. The circular RNA circTRAPPC6B promotes M1 macrophage polarization and autophagy in Mycobacterium tuberculosis-infected cells, mechanisms that enhance intracellular bacterial clearance. Gal-CircRNA@Rif@MPs are efficiently internalized into CD14+ macrophages through galactose receptor-mediated, clathrin-dependent, dynamin-dependent, and lipid raft-dependent endocytosis, with subsequent lysosomal release of both cargo components. In infected macrophages, this dual-action system significantly reduced intracellular bacterial burdens, alleviated lung tissue pathology, and mitigated infection-associated inflammation more effectively than single agents. The findings highlight how engineering macrophage-targeted delivery systems that combine immunomodulatory circRNAs with conventional antibiotics can overcome Mtb's immune evasion strategies and drug resistance mechanisms.

title: Spatially selective clearance of inflammation-mediated reactive oxygen species in injured kidney cells
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that platelet-coated tannic acid-derived nanoparticles (TFNAs@PLT) effectively treat both acute and chronic kidney injury by spatially targeting damaged kidney cells and neutralizing harmful reactive oxygen species (ROS). Inflammatory cytokines TNF-α and IL-6 released from injured tissue activate platelets, which deliver TFNAs directly to damaged kidney cells where they exert potent antioxidant effects and quench ROS accumulation. By reducing ROS levels, TFNAs@PLT suppress multiple inflammatory pathways including the NLRP3-mediated Caspase-1/IL-1β axis and the Caspase-3/GSDME pathway, thereby inhibiting both apoptosis and pyroptosis in kidney cells. The treatment restores mitochondrial function, decreases inflammatory markers (TNF-α and IL-6), reduces kidney injury biomarkers (BUN and creatinine), and prevents renal fibrosis through suppression of TNF-α/NF-κB and NLRP3/Caspase-1 signaling. Importantly, the platelet coating protects TFNAs from immune degradation while enhancing their renal targeting capacity, resulting in superior antioxidant efficacy compared to uncoated TFNAs or standard treatments like N-acetylcysteine.

title: Potential Mechanisms of Tetramethylpyrazine in the Treatment of Traumatic Brain Injury Based on Network Pharmacology, Molecular Docking, Molecular Dynamics Simulations, and in vivo Experiments
summary: Tetramethylpyrazine (TMP), a natural alkaloid derived from the traditional Chinese medicine herb Chuanxiong, demonstrates therapeutic potential for traumatic brain injury through multiple interconnected molecular pathways. TMP exerts its neuroprotective effects primarily by suppressing neuroinflammation, reducing oxidative stress, and stabilizing the blood-brain barrier; it accomplishes this by downregulating pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, IL-17A, TNF-α) and binding to key regulatory proteins (MMP2, MMP3, MMP13, GSK3B) involved in IL-17 and relaxin signaling pathways. Additionally, TMP activates the Nrf2 antioxidant pathway while inhibiting NLRP3 inflammasome activation and NF-κB signaling, thereby dampening the cascade of secondary injury mechanisms that include neuroinflammation, oxidative stress, and blood-brain barrier disruption. Mechanistically, TMP downregulates phosphorylated GSK3β, reduces matrix metalloproteinase activity, and upregulates the tight junction protein occludin to preserve barrier integrity. In vivo studies in rat traumatic brain injury models confirmed TMP's efficacy, showing significant improvements in neurological severity scores and functional recovery, validating its multi-target therapeutic approach to mitigating both primary mechanical injury and secondary pathological cascades.

title: Glutamine alleviates immunosuppression in polymicrobial sepsis by augmenting bacterial phagocytosis through sustaining the GFAT-DRP1 dependent mitochondrial calcium dynamics
summary:
# Summary

Glutamine alleviates sepsis-induced immunosuppression by restoring macrophage bacterial phagocytosis through a specialized metabolic pathway centered on mitochondrial calcium dynamics. The mechanism involves glutamine activation of GFAT-dependent protein O-GlcNAcylation, which promotes DRP1 oligomerization and phosphorylation via a CDK1-dependent pathway, ultimately triggering DRP1-mediated mitochondrial fission. This mitochondrial fission process is essential for releasing calcium from mitochondrial stores into the cytoplasm, where elevated calcium levels drive actin polymerization and facilitate bacterial particle uptake. In polymicrobial sepsis models, glutamine supplementation significantly reduced bacterial load, restored TNF-α and IL-6 production, and improved survival rates by restoring phagocytic capacity in immunosuppressed macrophages. Pharmacological inhibition of GFAT or DRP1, but not glutaminase pathways, blocked glutamine's beneficial effects, indicating that the hexosamine biosynthetic pathway rather than glutamine catabolism is critical for immunoresuscitation. These findings reveal how glutamine supports immune recovery in sepsis through a non-canonical metabolic mechanism linking nutrient sensing to subcellular calcium signaling and antimicrobial immunity.

title: Precision Antimicrobial Therapy Against Fusobacterium nucleatum Using Bioengineered Probiotics Expressing Guided Antimicrobial Peptides (gAMPs)
summary:
# Summary

Fusobacterium nucleatum, a bacterium strongly associated with colorectal cancer progression, employs the outer membrane porin FomA as a key virulence factor facilitating biofilm formation and host cell invasion. This study developed a precision therapeutic approach by engineering Lactococcus lactis probiotics to express guided antimicrobial peptides (gAMPs) derived from Ovispirin and Cathelin-related peptide CPSC5, each linked to a Statherin-derived guide peptide that specifically binds FomA. The resulting gAMP-expressing probiotics demonstrated selective toxicity toward F. nucleatum while preserving microbial diversity and the richness of beneficial bacteria, effectively reducing pathogen abundance in simulated human gut microbiome conditions. This targeted approach represents a significant advance in precision antimicrobial therapy, offering potential to mitigate colorectal cancer-associated infection and inflammation while maintaining microbiota integrity, outperforming conventional broad-spectrum antimicrobial strategies.

title: Anti-Inflammatory Properties of Resveratrol
summary:
# Summary

Resveratrol, a polyphenolic compound, exerts potent anti-inflammatory effects through multiple interconnected mechanisms relevant to inflammatory disorders including endometriosis. The compound suppresses production of key pro-inflammatory cytokines including interleukin-1β, interleukin-6, tumor necrosis factor-alpha, and interferon-gamma while simultaneously promoting anti-inflammatory mediators like interleukin-10 and interleukin-4. Mechanistically, resveratrol achieves these effects by inhibiting critical inflammatory signaling pathways including nuclear factor kappa-B, toll-like receptor signaling, and the TLR4/MyD88 axis, while also suppressing cyclooxygenase enzymes and downstream inflammatory mediators such as prostaglandin E2 and inducible nitric oxide synthase. Additionally, resveratrol reduces oxidative stress by increasing antioxidant enzymes including glutathione peroxidase and catalase, and enhances immune function through activation of natural killer cells and cytotoxic T lymphocytes. These multifaceted anti-inflammatory and immunomodulatory properties position resveratrol as a promising natural therapeutic candidate for managing chronic inflammatory conditions where uncontrolled inflammation drives pathogenesis.

title: Effect of Central Injection of Anandamide on LPS-Dependent Suppression of GnRH/LH Secretion in Ewes During the Follicular Phase of the Estrous Cycle
summary: This study demonstrates that anandamide (AEA), an endogenous cannabinoid, partially counteracts lipopolysaccharide (LPS)-induced suppression of the reproductive axis in ewes by modulating central inflammatory signaling. While LPS administration dramatically reduces GnRH and LH secretion through elevated cortisol and proinflammatory IL-1β, central injection of anandamide upregulates GnRH expression in the hypothalamus and reduces proinflammatory IL-1β while simultaneously increasing anti-inflammatory IL-10. However, despite these favorable local effects on GnRH synthesis, anandamide failed to reverse the LPS-induced suppression of circulating LH levels, indicating that central restoration of GnRH is insufficient to overcome systemic inflammatory inhibition of pituitary function. The mechanism involves anandamide binding to CB1 and CB2 receptors localized in the central nervous system and immune tissues, where it promotes anti-inflammatory cytokine production and suppresses proinflammatory signaling. These findings suggest that while anandamide has therapeutic potential in modulating central immune-reproductive interactions, additional interventions targeting pituitary-level suppression may be necessary to fully restore LH secretion during systemic inflammation.

title: Lipid-Enriched Gintonin from Korean Red Ginseng Marc Alleviates Obesity via Oral and Central Administration in Diet-Induced Obese Mice
summary: This study demonstrates that lipid-enriched gintonin extracted from Korean red ginseng marc (KRGM-gintonin) alleviates obesity through multiple metabolic mechanisms in diet-induced obese mice. Both oral and central administration of KRGM-gintonin, which is rich in lysophosphatidic acid and phospholipids, significantly reduced body weight gain, improved physical performance, and increased metabolic rate while enhancing lean mass percentage. The compound reduced hepatic steatosis and plasma lipid levels while promoting thermogenesis by upregulating uncoupling protein-1 and hormone-sensitive lipase in brown adipose tissue. Mechanistically, KRGM-gintonin activates lysophosphatidic acid receptor signaling, which elevates intracellular calcium and subsequently upregulates key thermogenic transcription factors including PRDM16 and PGC1α. Additionally, oral administration demonstrated anti-inflammatory effects by reducing tumor necrosis factor-α expression. These findings suggest that KRGM-gintonin represents a promising natural compound for treating obesity and its associated metabolic complications through enhanced energy expenditure and suppressed adipogenesis.

title: Regulatory Effect and Mechanism of Tanshinone I on Cell Apoptosis in Steroid‐Induced Osteonecrosis of the Femoral Head
journal: The Kaohsiung Journal of Medical Sciences
summary: Tanshinone I (TsI), a diterpenoid compound derived from *Salvia miltiorrhiza*, effectively alleviates steroid-induced osteonecrosis of the femoral head (SIONFH) by suppressing the PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling pathway and reducing osteocyte apoptosis. In SIONFH rat models, TsI treatment significantly improved bone microarchitecture by increasing bone mineral density, trabecular thickness, and bone volume while reducing empty lacunae and restoring osteopontin expression. The compound's therapeutic mechanism involves downregulating phosphorylation of PI3K, AKT, and mTOR, which in turn decreases pro-apoptotic markers (Bax and cleaved-caspase-3) while upregulating the anti-apoptotic protein Bcl-2. Functional studies confirmed that pathway activation through the PI3K/AKT/mTOR activator 740 Y-P reversed TsI's protective effects, demonstrating that pathway inhibition is essential for TsI's therapeutic action. Beyond bone protection, TsI exhibits antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and anti-osteoclastogenic properties, suggesting a multifaceted approach to treating SIONFH. These findings position TsI as a promising natural therapeutic agent for glucocorticoid-induced bone disease.

title: Effects of quercetin and its derivatives in in vivo models of neuroinflammation: A systematic review and meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review and meta-analysis demonstrates that quercetin, a naturally occurring polyphenol found in cherries, apples, and other dietary sources, effectively reduces neuroinflammation through multiple complementary mechanisms. Quercetin suppresses the production of pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6) and reduces microglial and astrocytic activation by inactivating the NF-κB signaling pathway and modulating MAPK and Nrf2/HO-1 pathways. Beyond its anti-inflammatory effects, quercetin exhibits potent antioxidant properties by enhancing expression of superoxide dismutase and catalase while reducing reactive oxygen species and nitric oxide production, thereby protecting neurons from oxidative damage and apoptosis. The meta-analysis confirms that quercetin treatment significantly decreases pro-inflammatory cytokine levels and microgliosis markers across in vivo models, suggesting therapeutic potential for neuroinflammatory diseases including Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, and autism. Quercetin derivatives, particularly alpha-glycosyl isoquercitrin and quercitrin, retain the biological activities of the parent compound while demonstrating improved bioavailability, offering promising alternatives to conventional anti-inflammatory drugs that carry significant adverse effects including gastrointestinal complications, bone loss, and metabolic dysfunction.

title: Pharmacological evaluation of 1-acetyl-β-carboline, a naturally occurring compound with anti-skin cancer potential
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that 1-acetyl-β-carboline (ABC), a naturally occurring compound identified in *Lactobacillus gasseri* culture extract, exhibits potent anti-melanoma activity through multiple mechanisms. ABC dose-dependently reduced melanoma cell viability and colony formation in SK-MEL-5 and SK-MEL-28 cells while maintaining minimal cytotoxicity to normal dermal fibroblasts, with selectivity above 87% viability. The compound induced G2/M phase cell-cycle arrest through upregulation of the cell-cycle regulator p27 and promoted apoptosis via activation of caspase-3 and caspase-7. ABC achieved these anti-cancer effects by inhibiting the MEK/ERK signaling pathway and downregulating cyclooxygenase-2 (COX-2), thereby targeting both proliferation and inflammation-related pathways. In a mouse model, ABC effectively reduced 12-O-tetradecanoylphorbol-13-acetate-induced epidermal hyperplasia in a dose-dependent manner, validating its in vivo anti-inflammatory and chemopreventive potential for skin cancer.

title: Vitamin B6 promotes the activation of primordial follicles through the PI3K/Akt signaling pathway
summary: This study demonstrates that vitamin B6 (pyridoxine) and multiple other B vitamins promote primordial follicle activation through the PI3K/Akt signaling pathway. Pyridoxine treatment significantly increased the number of growing follicles in both mouse models and cultured human ovarian tissue by upregulating phosphorylated Akt (p-Akt) and FOXO3a, with FOXO3a subsequently translocating from the nucleus to the cytoplasm. The PI3K inhibitor LY294002 blocked pyridoxine-induced follicle activation, confirming the essential role of the PI3K/Akt pathway. Additionally, vitamin B6 upregulated markers of oocyte and granulosa cell function including Gdf9, Zp3, DDX4, PCNA, and Ki-67, while promoting granulosa cell proliferation without affecting apoptosis. Among the nine vitamins tested (B1, B2, B3, B5, B6, B9, B12, C, and E), all successfully increased growing follicle numbers, with vitamins B7 and K showing no effect, suggesting a broad role for micronutrients in reproductive function and potential therapeutic applications for fertility enhancement.

title: Fibrinolytic and
summary:
# Summary

This study investigated the fibrinolytic and anti-adhesion properties of Rumex nepalensis, a plant with traditional medicinal uses, identifying cinnamtannin B1 and epicatechin gallate as its primary bioactive compounds. Rumex nepalensis demonstrated superior fibrinolytic activity compared to other plant species tested, with cinnamtannin B1 showing stronger activity than epicatechin gallate in breaking down fibrin—a critical mechanism since fibrin accumulation drives intra-abdominal adhesion formation after surgical injury. Treatment with Rumex nepalensis root extract, its fractions, and isolated cinnamtannin B1 significantly reduced adhesion formation and fibrotic area in experimental models while suppressing key inflammatory mediators including TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-8 through immune cell infiltration inhibition. The study demonstrates that plant-derived polyphenols (flavonoids, tannins, and anthraquinones) exert therapeutic potential by enhancing the plasmin-mediated fibrinolysis pathway—where plasmin cleaves fibrin under activation by tissue and urokinase plasminogen activators—while simultaneously counteracting plasminogen activator inhibitor-1 (PAI-1) that would otherwise suppress fibrinolysis. These findings provide mechanistic evidence supporting the use of Rumex nepalensis compounds as potential preventative agents against postoperative complications including chronic pain, infertility, and intestinal obstruction that arise from abdominal adhesions.

title: Mitigating Risk of Premature Ovarian Insufficiency Using Adjunct Chinese Herbal Medicines: A Nested Patient-Control Study in Female Cancer Patients with Chemotherapy
summary: Premature ovarian insufficiency (POI) is a significant late effect of cancer-related therapies in adolescent and young adult cancer (AYAC) survivors, characterized by irregular menstruation and leading to serious complications including cardiovascular disease, osteoporosis, and cognitive dysfunction. While gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists (GnRHa) are commonly used to protect ovarian function during chemotherapy, they have deleterious effects on bone and cardiovascular health. This nested case-control study demonstrates that adjunctive Chinese herbal medicine (CHM) substantially reduces POI risk, with patients receiving CHM being 84% less likely to develop POI, particularly when initiated within three years of AYAC onset and among patients under 30 years old. Specific herbal agents including Da-Huang, Xiang-Fu, Ge-Gen-Tang, and others were associated with reduced POI risk through multiple mechanisms: CHM modulates apoptosis, enhances antioxidant capacity, regulates immune responses, and exhibits estrogen-like activity. Key active compounds such as aloe-emodin from Da-Huang inhibit apoptotic caspases, while Xiang-Fu's active components modulate the PI3K/Akt and NF-κB pathways that control cellular proliferation and inflammatory responses affecting ovarian function. These findings suggest that plant-derived polyphenolic compounds in CHM offer a safer therapeutic adjunct to conventional cancer treatments by mitigating chemotherapy-induced ovarian dysfunction through anti-inflammatory and cytoprotective mechanisms.

title: Taurine Alleviates Inflammation, Oxidative Stress, Apoptosis, and Uterus Microbiota Dysregulation of Endometritis by Inhibiting PI3K-AKT/MAPK/NF-κB Pathways in Mice
journal: Animals : an Open Access Journal from MDPI
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that taurine effectively alleviates lipopolysaccharide (LPS)-induced endometritis in mice through multi-pathway mechanisms involving inflammation suppression, oxidative stress reduction, and apoptosis inhibition. Taurine achieves these protective effects primarily by inhibiting the PI3K-AKT, MAPK, and NF-κB signaling pathways, which are central mediators of the inflammatory cascade triggered by LPS. The compound reduces pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6), enhances antioxidant enzyme activity (SOD, GSH, T-AOC), upregulates the Nrf2 antioxidant response pathway, and restores tight junction proteins (ZO-1, Claudin-3, Occludin) critical for uterine barrier integrity. Additionally, taurine restores dysregulated uterine microbiota composition, reducing pro-inflammatory bacteria (Muribacter, Rodentibacter) while promoting beneficial commensals, thereby addressing both host and microbial contributors to endometritis pathology. These comprehensive anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects position taurine as a promising therapeutic agent for managing endometritis and potentially related reproductive disorders.

title: Effect of Hyaluronic Acid Content on Functional Properties, Antioxidant Activity, and In Vitro Digestion of Food-Grade Furcellaran Hydrogels and Emulgels
summary:
# Summary

This study investigated furcellaran-hyaluronic acid (HA) composite hydrogels and emulgels as functional biomaterials for bioactive compound delivery. Increasing hyaluronic acid content enhanced elasticity, antioxidant potential, and electrostatic stabilization of the composites while reducing hardness, with HA's natural presence in synovial fluid and connective tissue supporting its biocompatibility. Evening primrose oil, which contains high levels of omega-6 fatty acids including linoleic acid and γ-linolenic acid, was incorporated into the emulgels and demonstrated therapeutic potential for inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis, atopic dermatitis, and endometriosis. The composite gels' swelling properties increased with HA concentration due to its hygroscopic nature, and hydrogen bonding stabilized the polysaccharide network structure. These materials show promise as delivery platforms for both hydrophilic and lipophilic bioactive compounds, with applications in drug delivery and tissue engineering supported by their biodegradability, biocompatibility, and anti-inflammatory properties.

title: Evaluation of Selective Efficacy of Indocyanine Green-Mediated Photodynamic Therapy ICG-PDT in MCF-7 Breast Cancer Cells Compared to Healthy Cells in a 3D Hollow Fiber Bioreactor Model
summary:
# Summary

This study evaluated indocyanine green-mediated photodynamic therapy (ICG-PDT) as a selective cancer treatment using MCF-7 breast cancer cells expressing estrogen receptors, progesterone receptors, and HER2 in a three-dimensional hollow fiber bioreactor model. ICG-PDT reduced MCF-7 cell viability in a dose-dependent manner while sparing healthy breast epithelial cells, operating through activation of reactive oxygen species and singlet oxygen that trigger apoptosis via caspase-3 activation and modulation of anti-apoptotic (Bcl-2 downregulation) and pro-apoptotic (Bax upregulation) pathways. The therapy also induced G1 phase cell cycle arrest and demonstrated synergistic effects when combined with cisplatin or paclitaxel, substantially increasing apoptosis rates. A key limitation was the heterogeneous distribution of ICG within three-dimensional spheroids, with accumulation in peripheral regions but poor penetration to hypoxic central regions where HIF-1α activation suppressed reactive oxygen species formation and enabled treatment resistance. The hollow fiber bioreactor system enabled real-time monitoring of light propagation and demonstrated that ICG-PDT enhanced immunogenicity through necrosis of the outer spheroid layers, supporting its potential as a selective early-stage breast cancer therapy when combined with chemotherapeutic agents.

title: Ethnomedicinal validation of Telfairia occidentalis L. leaf: a dual experimental and computational approach to uterine leiomyoma therapy
summary: Telfairia occidentalis L., a nutrient-rich West African leafy vegetable, demonstrates therapeutic potential for treating uterine leiomyomas through multiple bioactive mechanisms. The plant's aqueous leaf extract exhibits antifibrotic and hormone-modulating properties by reducing testosterone and oestradiol levels while simultaneously decreasing oxidative stress and inhibiting key enzymes including acetylcholinesterase and monoamine oxidase. These polyphenolic compounds—including flavonoids, alkaloids, and fatty acids such as 9-octadecenoic acid and linoleic acid methyl ester—work synergistically to suppress inflammation and oxidative damage implicated in fibroid pathogenesis. In an MSG-induced uterine fibroid model, T. occidentalis treatment restored ovarian architecture, improved uterine integrity, and corrected hormone-related abnormalities associated with infertility and pelvic pain. Computational analyses revealed that specific plant constituents including beta-carotene and lutein interact with STEAP4, suggesting a molecular basis for the extract's therapeutic action. This ethnomedicinal validation supports the traditional use of this vegetable while establishing a mechanistic foundation for developing plant-derived interventions targeting hormonally-mediated reproductive pathology.

title: Can artificial intelligence uncover the bioactive peptides’ benefits for human health and knowledge? A narrative review
summary:
# Summary

This narrative review examines how artificial intelligence, particularly machine learning and deep learning approaches, is transforming the discovery and characterization of bioactive peptides from natural sources. Bioactive peptides demonstrate multiple beneficial activities including antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial properties, with specific examples such as antihypertensive peptides that inhibit angiotensin-converting enzyme extracted from milk proteins and antioxidant peptides derived from soy and marine sources. AI-driven methodologies employ diverse computational architectures—including convolutional neural networks, recurrent neural networks, transformer models, and support vector machines—to predict peptide bioactivity, optimize extraction processes, and assess binding interactions through molecular docking and dynamics simulations. Advanced techniques like AlphaFold enable 3D structure prediction, while deep learning models specifically predict gastrointestinal stability and intestinal permeability of food-derived peptides. The integration of interpretability methods such as SHAP and LIME, combined with nutrigenomics approaches, provides mechanistic insights into how dietary peptides modulate gene expression, substantially accelerating bioactive peptide identification while reducing discovery time and cost compared to traditional experimental screening.

title: Network pharmacology and molecular docking to explore the potential mechanism of Codonopsis pilosula delaying aging
summary:
# Summary

Codonopsis pilosula (CP), a traditional Chinese medicine, exhibits potent antiaging and antioxidant effects through multiple molecular pathways. Network pharmacology analysis identified 21 active components from CP that target 433 potential genes involved in aging, with 14 core targets including AKT1, TNF, SRC, ESR1, EGFR, BCL2, and HSP90AA1. Molecular docking studies demonstrated that key CP constituents luteolin and 7-(β-xylosyl) cephalomannine_qt bind with high affinity to critical aging-related proteins, particularly AKT1, EGFR, and BCL2. CP's antiaging mechanisms involve activation of autophagy and the CLEC7A/inflammasome pathway, suppression of inflammatory responses and oxidative stress through gut-liver axis signaling, and modulation of lipid metabolism and estrogen signaling pathways. Polysaccharide components enhance immune function by promoting TNF and interleukin secretion while supporting brain injury protection, digestion, hematopoiesis, and blood glucose regulation. These integrated effects across multiple signaling cascades—including PI3K-Akt, estrogen, and TNF pathways—collectively explain CP's traditional use in delaying aging and supporting healthy aging processes.

title: Covalent coating strategy for enhancing the biocompatibility and hemocompatibility of blood-contacting medical materials
summary:
# Summary

This article describes covalent coating strategies to enhance the biocompatibility and hemocompatibility of blood-contacting medical materials, particularly vascular grafts and catheters. Plasma treatments (nitrogen, ammonia, and argon) generate bioactive sites on polymer surfaces that enable covalent attachment of bioactive molecules including collagen IV, fibronectin, and vascular endothelial growth factor. Multiple coating approaches have demonstrated superior anti-thrombotic properties: transmembrane protein TM-N3 immobilized on grafts inhibits thrombin generation, laminin-coated expanded polytetrafluoroethylene (ePTFE) grafts accelerate neovascularization and endothelialization, and heparin-conjugated coatings on various polymers (chitosan, POC, polyurethane, polycarbonate urethane) effectively prevent clot formation and platelet adhesion. Additional strategies employ phosphorylcholine groups mimicking cell membranes, anti-CD34 antibodies for endothelial progenitor cell capture, sulfobetaine polymers with antibacterial and anticoagulant properties, and VEGF-modified surfaces that selectively capture endothelial progenitor cells while minimizing platelet adhesion. These surface modifications consistently outperform commercially available devices by providing sustained anticoagulant activity and promoting beneficial cellular interactions while reducing thrombogenicity.

title: Mesenchymal stem cells derived extracellular vesicles ameliorate ovarian aging through inhibiting LGALS3BP/NF-κB induced inflammation
summary:
# Summary

Umbilical cord mesenchymal stem cell-derived extracellular vesicles (UCMSC-EVs) ameliorate ovarian aging by suppressing LGALS3BP-mediated NF-κB inflammation and promoting ovarian function. UCMSC-EVs transport LGALS3BP to injured ovarian granulosa cells, where LGALS3BP modulates the NF-κB signaling pathway and inhibits pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α) while enhancing anti-inflammatory factors. Treatment with UCMSC-EVs restores ovarian morphology, increases ovarian volume and weight, upregulates fertility markers (AMH, FSHR, ER-α, CYP19A1), and reduces cellular apoptosis through downregulation of aging-associated proteins (p53, p16, p21, Bax) while upregulating anti-apoptotic Bcl-2. The absence of LGALS3BP in UCMSC-EVs impairs these therapeutic benefits, confirming LGALS3BP as a critical mediator. These findings establish MSC-derived extracellular vesicles as a regenerative therapy that counteracts age-related ovarian decline through targeted anti-inflammatory signaling.

title: Visual screening and efficacy evaluation of high-performance fluorescent probe DAF-FM in esophagitis cancer transformation
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that the fluorescent probe DAF-FM enables early detection of esophageal cancer transformation by specifically visualizing nitric oxide (NO), a key mediator in the inflammation-dysplasia-cancer pathway. Chronic inflammation produces elevated NO levels, which activate the NF-κB signaling pathway and drive malignant transformation through upregulation of oncogenes (c-Myc, Bcl-2) and inactivation of tumor suppressors (p53), ultimately promoting cell proliferation and immune escape in the tumor microenvironment. DAF-FM demonstrates superior clinical performance compared to traditional endoscopic biopsy, achieving 92.5% sensitivity with real-time detection in 30 minutes versus 48 hours for histopathological examination, while maintaining low cytotoxicity. The probe showed concentration- and time-dependent fluorescence responses that correlated with NO levels and tumor burden (R² = 0.87 in rat models), validating its mechanistic utility. These findings establish DAF-FM as a feasible diagnostic tool for visual screening of esophageal cancer risk and evaluation of therapeutic efficacy in inflammatory bowel disease patients prone to malignant transformation.

title: Development and
summary: This study developed and validated a dual delivery system for trans-resveratrol, a polyphenol with established anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and anticancer properties, to overcome its poor oral bioavailability and chemical instability. The researchers created inclusion complexes of resveratrol with hydroxypropyl-beta-cyclodextrin (HPβCD) and encapsulated them into PEG-coated stealth liposomes, which significantly enhanced both solubility and physicochemical stability while prolonging systemic half-life and avoiding reticuloendothelial uptake. A robust reversed-phase HPLC method was developed and validated to quantitatively analyze trans-resveratrol with excellent linearity (R² = 0.9997), accuracy (98–102% recovery), and precision (RSD ≤ 2%), achieving limits of detection and quantification of 0.96 and 2.91 μg/mL, respectively. The liposomal formulations released resveratrol through a non-Fickian mechanism combining diffusion and matrix erosion, with the F5 formulation achieving optimal sustained release over 24 hours by balancing composition of unsaturated (EPC) and saturated (HSPC) phospholipids. Critically, the HPβCD inclusion complexes and phospholipid matrix provided dual protection against UV-induced isomerization to the biologically inactive cis-form, with no cis-resveratrol detected in the final RHCD stealth liposome formulation even under light exposure.
