This delivery system addresses key bioavailability challenges and represents a promising approach for therapeutic applications of resveratrol in endometriosis and other inflammatory conditions.

title: Mechanistic insights into drying methods: how they govern the structure and bioactivity of resveratrol-HP-β-CD inclusion complexes
summary: Resveratrol, a naturally occurring polyphenol found in grapes and berries, possesses valuable antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and cardioprotective properties but suffers from poor aqueous solubility and low bioavailability that limit its clinical application. The study demonstrates that complexation of resveratrol with hydroxypropyl-β-cyclodextrin (HP-β-CD) significantly overcomes these limitations through hydrogen bonding and hydrophobic interactions, achieving up to 89-fold solubility enhancement. The drying method employed during complex preparation critically governs bioactivity: spray drying produced the highest solubility enhancement and amorphous structures but caused heat-induced degradation of the bioactive trans-isomer; in contrast, freeze-drying preserved the superior free radical scavenging capacity of trans-resveratrol, yielding complexes with the most potent antioxidant activity (IC50 = 14.01 μg/mL). Solvent evaporation emerged as the optimal balance, minimizing thermal degradation while achieving superior resveratrol recovery and reduced moisture content. These findings establish that low-temperature processing methods better preserve resveratrol's bioactive form and antioxidant functionality in inclusion complexes, with a 1:2 resveratrol-to-HP-β-CD molar ratio providing maximal therapeutic benefit.

title: Tellimagrandin II Stimulates Inflammasomes by Causing an Accumulation of 3-Aminopropanal, Which Promotes Apoptosis of Endometriotic Cells While Inhibiting Invasion
summary: Tellimagrandin II, a polyphenol from Paeoniae Radix used in traditional Chinese medicine, effectively treats endometriosis through a multi-targeted mechanism involving inflammasome activation and metabolic reprogramming. The compound binds to and inhibits ALDH7A1 while simultaneously activating SMOX, leading to intracellular accumulation of 3-aminopropanal, which directly activates NLRP3 inflammasomes. Inflammasome activation initiates a cascade of pro-apoptotic signaling through caspase-1, caspase-3, and BAX upregulation, while simultaneously suppressing β-catenin and MMP2 expression to inhibit ectopic endometrial cell invasion and migration. Additionally, tellimagrandin II activates JAK-STAT and NF-κB pathways while inhibiting β-alanine metabolism and ECM-receptor interactions, further constraining pathological cell behavior. These coordinated effects promote apoptosis of ectopic endometrial cells while simultaneously preventing their invasive capacity, offering a mechanistic explanation for the compound's therapeutic efficacy in endometriosis treatment.

title: Engineered M2 macrophage-derived extracellular vesicles reprogram mitochondrial metabolism to alleviate temporomandibular joint cartilage degeneration
summary:
# Summary

Temporomandibular joint osteoarthritis (TMJOA) is driven by mitochondrial dysfunction, which promotes chondrocyte inflammation and extracellular matrix breakdown. This study developed engineered extracellular vesicles derived from M2 macrophages loaded with curcumin (Cur@M2-EVs) that reprogram mitochondrial metabolism by scavenging reactive oxygen species, restoring mitochondrial membrane potential, and shifting chondrocytes from glycolysis toward oxidative phosphorylation and enhanced ATP production. Cur@M2-EVs suppress inflammation primarily through inhibition of the NF-κB signaling pathway and suppression of the Thbs1 gene, while curcumin contributes additional antioxidant and mitophagy-regulating effects. When incorporated into biocompatible PEGDA hydrogel microspheres for controlled release (Cur@M2-EVs@PEGDA), the system demonstrated potent anti-inflammatory and cartilage-protective effects in a rat TMJOA model, while simultaneously improving joint lubrication. This dual-functional therapeutic approach effectively alleviates cartilage degeneration by restoring mitochondrial homeostasis and reducing mechanical joint friction, representing a promising strategy for TMJOA treatment.

title: Nutritional Impact on Breast Cancer in Menopausal and Post-Menopausal Patients Treated with Aromatase Inhibitors
summary:
# Summary

This review examines the nutritional impact on estrogen-receptor-positive breast cancer outcomes in menopausal and postmenopausal patients treated with aromatase inhibitors (AIs), particularly letrozole, anastrozole, and exemestane. Nutrition plays a pivotal role in clinical response to AI therapy, with multiple polyphenol-rich foods demonstrating complementary anticancer mechanisms: licorice, rosemary, juniper, cannabis, and citrus fruits contain bioactive compounds with intrinsic aromatase-inhibiting properties, while honey, ginger, turmeric, pomegranates, and resveratrol exhibit antiproliferative effects. Conversely, certain compounds including soy, cow's milk, and sesame products possess estrogen-like effects and warrant caution during AI treatment. The review also identifies specific phytochemical mechanisms, such as liquiritigenin from licorice upregulating E-cadherin and triggering apoptosis, and xanthohumol increasing reactive oxygen species to enhance anti-tumor effects. Additionally, AIs are noted to treat endometriosis while causing adverse effects including musculoskeletal complications, cognitive dysfunction, and vaginal dryness—side effects that targeted nutritional interventions may help mitigate. The Mediterranean diet is highlighted as a protective dietary pattern, while obesity emerges as a significant modifiable risk factor associated with disease progression, recurrence, and treatment resistance.

title: Validating
summary:
# Summary

This review examines natural compounds as toll-like receptor 4 (TLR4) antagonists for treating inflammatory diseases. TLR4 activation by damage-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs) and lipopolysaccharide (LPS) triggers a cascade of inflammatory signaling through MyD88-dependent and TRIF-dependent pathways, ultimately activating NF-κB and producing pro-inflammatory cytokines including TNF, IL-1β, IL-6, and interferon-β. Excessive TLR4 signaling contributes to multiple acute and chronic inflammatory conditions including sepsis, inflammatory bowel disease, atherosclerosis, diabetes, neurodegenerative diseases, and myocarditis. Natural polyphenolic compounds such as lupeol, cichoric acid, and ferulic acid have demonstrated therapeutic potential by inhibiting TLR4 activation and downstream inflammatory pathways, reducing cytokine production and protecting against LPS-induced tissue damage in various disease models. While clinical TLR4 antagonists like eritoran and TAK-242 remain in phases II-III trials, natural compounds represent a promising source of chemical diversity for developing effective TLR4-targeted therapeutics with improved safety profiles.

title: Nanoceria as Next-Generation Immunotherapeutics: Applications in Chronic Inflammation, Cancer, and Tissue Repair
summary:
# Summary

Cerium oxide nanoparticles (nanoceria) function as next-generation immunotherapeutics by mimicking natural antioxidant enzymes, particularly superoxide dismutase and catalase, through their catalytic Ce3+ and Ce4+ ions that scavenge reactive oxygen species and mitigate oxidative stress. These nanomaterials modulate immune function by polarizing macrophages and regulating cytokine production, thereby reducing chronic inflammation and cellular damage associated with diseases including endometriosis, rheumatoid arthritis, and cancer. Following intravenous administration, nanoceria primarily accumulates in the liver and spleen via clathrin-mediated endocytosis and localizes to mitochondria, lysosomes, and the endoplasmic reticulum, with clearance occurring through hepatobiliary excretion and renal elimination in a size-dependent manner. The antioxidant and enzymatic activities of nanoceria can be enhanced through strategic modifications such as europium doping, which increases oxygen vacancies and Ce3+ levels, or by optimizing particle size and morphology to maximize catalytic surface facets. Notably, nanoceria exhibits minimal toxicity while demonstrating protective anti-inflammatory effects and, in certain models, enhancing vaccine immunogenicity, positioning these nanoparticles as promising therapeutic agents for inflammatory and immune-mediated diseases.

title: Exploring the mechanism of Kemofang in treating idiopathic membranous nephropathy based on LC–MS/MS combined with network pharmacology, molecular docking, and molecular dynamics simulation
summary:
# Summary

This study investigates the therapeutic mechanism of Kemo Formula, a traditional herbal remedy, in treating idiopathic membranous nephropathy (IMN) through integrated computational approaches including LC-MS/MS, network pharmacology, and molecular dynamics simulation. The formula's 15 key active components—particularly baicalein, quercetin, apigenin, scutellarein, and norizalpinin—exert protective effects through multiple interconnected pathways involving the PI3K/AKT, MAPK, and TGF-β1/Smad signaling cascades. These polyphenolic compounds reduce podocyte apoptosis, a primary driver of IMN progression, by suppressing oxidative stress and inflammatory responses through mechanisms such as NF-κB inhibition and ROS reduction, while simultaneously ameliorating lipid dysregulation associated with proteinuria. Molecular docking studies reveal that multiple flavonoids directly bind and stabilize interactions with key therapeutic targets including AKT1, caspase-3, and TNF-α, with binding energies indicating favorable complex stability. The multi-target modulation approach demonstrated by Kemo Formula offers a mechanistic rationale for its efficacy against IMN while addressing multiple pathogenic pathways simultaneously, potentially preventing progression to nephrotic syndrome and end-stage renal disease.

title: The role of metformin in extracellular matrix-based three-dimensional cell culture Models: A mini-review on therapeutic potentials
summary:
# Summary

Metformin, a widely used antidiabetic agent, exhibits pleiotropic effects on extracellular matrix composition and remodeling through activation of the AMPK/mTOR pathway and inhibition of TGF-β signaling. The drug modulates ECM biomechanics and cell-ECM interactions by altering collagen deposition, reducing cross-linking, and regulating multiple miRNAs (including miR-33a, miR-34a, and miR-200) that suppress fibrotic gene programs and epithelial-mesenchymal transition. In three-dimensional ECM-based culture models, metformin-loaded scaffolds promote wound healing and tissue regeneration while simultaneously reducing pro-inflammatory biomarkers and oxidative stress through AMPK-dependent downregulation of NF-κB, IL-8, and ICAM-1. The compound demonstrates particular therapeutic potential in fibrotic and reproductive disorders, including polycystic ovary syndrome, by restoring balanced MMP/TIMP ratios and suppressing aberrant ECM synthesis. These mechanisms highlight metformin's value as a scaffold-incorporated therapeutic agent for regenerative medicine and as an adjunct treatment for conditions characterized by ECM dysfunction and fibrosis.

title: Echinacoside alleviates asthenozoospermia by upregulating Sox5-mediated transcriptional activation of the CatSper gene
summary:
# Summary

Echinacoside, a phenylethanoid glycoside derived from *Cistanche tubulosa*, alleviates asthenozoospermia by upregulating the Sox5 transcription factor, which enhances expression of all four alpha subunits (CatSper1-4) of the cation channel of sperm (CatSper). In rat models and human samples, echinacoside treatment increased both mRNA and protein levels of CatSper subunits and elevated intracellular calcium influx through CatSper-mediated pathways, leading to improved sperm motility, viability, hyperactivation, and acrosome reaction in both healthy donors and patients with idiopathic asthenozoospermia. The Sox5 transcription factor directly binds to the CatSper1 gene promoter to activate its transcription, establishing the mechanistic link between echinacoside-induced Sox5 upregulation and enhanced sperm function. Additionally, echinacoside improves sperm quality through antioxidant mechanisms, including increased glutathione peroxidase activity and suppression of pro-inflammatory cytokines, collectively reversing the impaired sperm functionality characteristic of asthenozoospermia.

title: Metabolite and gut microbiota co-biomarkers in Danggui Shaoyao San: insights into a shared therapeutic approach
summary: Danggui Shaoyao San (DSS), a traditional Chinese medicine formulation, demonstrates therapeutic efficacy across Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus, Alzheimer's Disease, and Polycystic Ovary Syndrome through overlapping metabolite and microbial biomarkers. The herbal preparation's active compounds—ligustilides, paeoniflorin, and ferulic acid—suppress inflammation and oxidative stress by inhibiting the TLR4–NF-κB and TGF-β/Smad pathways while activating AMPK/PINK1 signaling and improving mitochondrial function. DSS treatment normalizes tryptophan and phenylalanine metabolism, reducing their elevated levels associated with insulin resistance and cognitive decline, while simultaneously enriching the beneficial gut bacterial phylum Bacteroidetes, which correlates with improved glycemic control and restored ovulatory function. The kynurenine pathway—a major tryptophan metabolite route dysregulated in all three diseases—represents a shared mechanistic target, with elevated kynurenine-to-tryptophan ratios correlating with insulin resistance, reproductive hormone imbalances, and amyloid-beta accumulation. By modulating amino acid metabolism and gut microbial composition, DSS alleviates diabetic complications, enhances cognitive function, and ameliorates hyperandrogenism and menstrual irregularities in PCOS, establishing metabolite-microbiota interactions as central to its multi-system therapeutic benefits.

title: Therapeutic potential of epigallocatechin gallate in gynecologic cancer, endometriosis, polycystic ovary syndrome: a mechanistic and translational perspective
summary:
# Summary

Epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG), the most abundant catechin in tea, exhibits potent therapeutic potential for gynecological conditions including endometriosis, polycystic ovary syndrome, and reproductive cancers through multiple interconnected mechanisms. EGCG functions as both an antioxidant and anti-inflammatory agent by neutralizing reactive oxygen species, suppressing inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6), and enhancing endogenous antioxidant enzyme activity (SOD, CAT, GPX). The compound modulates multiple critical signaling pathways including PI3K/Akt/mTOR, MAPK/ERK, NF-κB, Wnt/β-catenin, JAK/STAT, and HIF-1α/VEGF, thereby inhibiting cell proliferation, inducing apoptosis, suppressing angiogenesis and metastasis, and regulating cell-cycle progression. EGCG also modulates epigenetic mechanisms and exhibits antimutagenic effects while sensitizing cancer cells to chemotherapy. These multifaceted actions position catechins as promising natural compounds for preventing and treating oxidative stress-related gynecological pathologies, particularly endometriosis where oxidative damage plays a central pathogenic role.

title: Complementary and alternative therapies in the treatment of primary dysmenorrhea
summary:
# Summary

Primary dysmenorrhea results from excessive prostaglandin release, which causes uterine contractions and pain through multiple inflammatory pathways. While conventional NSAIDs inhibit cyclooxygenase enzymes to reduce prostaglandin synthesis, and hormonal contraceptives provide relief in 70–80% of women, complementary and alternative therapies offer additional therapeutic options with fewer adverse effects. Natural compounds including vitamin D, vitamin E, ginger, cinnamon, fennel, and fish oil reduce dysmenorrhea pain through complementary mechanisms—inhibiting prostaglandin biosynthesis, suppressing inflammatory cytokine cascades via NF-κB regulation, and reducing uterine contractility. Dietary interventions emphasizing fruits, vegetables, fish, and dairy products, alongside physical modalities like transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation (TENS), provide evidence-based non-pharmacological approaches to dysmenorrhea management. These polyphenol-rich and micronutrient-based therapies represent viable alternatives or adjuncts to conventional treatment, particularly for women seeking to minimize pharmaceutical side effects such as nausea, depression, and headache.

title: Differential Regulatory Effects of Cannabinoids and Vitamin E Analogs on Cellular Lipid Homeostasis and Inflammation in Human Macrophages
summary:
# Summary

This study examined how cannabinoids and vitamin E analogs differentially regulate lipid homeostasis and inflammation in human macrophages. Tetrahydrocannabinol (THC) promoted pro-inflammatory and lipid-accumulating responses by binding to CB1 receptors, increasing reactive oxygen species production, activating the NFκB inflammatory pathway, and upregulating CD36/FAT fatty acid transporter expression. In contrast, vitamin E analogs—particularly αTAr—and cannabidiol (CBD) suppressed these harmful effects by inhibiting lipid accumulation, reducing inflammatory cytokine production (TNFα, IL-6, IL-1β), and blocking CD36/FAT expression. The study revealed that CB1 activation promotes atherosclerotic pathways through inflammation and oxidative stress, while CB2 activation and vitamin E supplementation exert protective effects. These findings suggest that dysregulation of lipid homeostasis and cannabinoid receptor signaling contribute to chronic macrophage-mediated inflammation, and that vitamin E analogs and selective CB2 agonists represent potential therapeutic strategies to counteract THC-induced lipotoxicity and foam cell formation.

title: Apigenin Alleviates Zearalenone-Induced Oxidative Stress and Apoptosis in Swine Testis Cells Through the Wnt Signaling Pathway
summary: Zearalenone (ZEN), a mycotoxin with estrogenic properties, induces apoptosis, oxidative stress, and cell cycle arrest in swine testis cells through suppression of the Wnt signaling pathway, particularly by downregulating LRP5 and its downstream effector c-Myc, while also reducing expression of proliferation markers including PCNA, CCNB1, and CCNA2. Apigenin, a polyphenolic flavonoid with documented antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, protects testis cells from ZEN-induced damage by upregulating LRP5 and related Wnt pathway components including iASPP and TRAF2, thereby restoring cell proliferation and reversing ZEN-induced G1 phase arrest. At optimal concentrations (0.1–1 μM), apigenin treatment significantly attenuated ZEN-induced decreases in cell viability, reduced lactate dehydrogenase release, and restored expression of cell cycle regulators. The protective mechanism operates through Wnt pathway activation, as evidenced by the fact that specific pathway inhibition (IWR-1-endo) blocks apigenin's cytoprotective effects. These findings suggest that apigenin represents a promising natural therapeutic agent for mitigating reproductive toxicity caused by mycotoxin exposure.

title: Ginseng Polysaccharides Protect Against Endoplasmic Reticulum Stress-Induced Damage via PI3K/Akt Signalling Pathway in Bovine Ovarian Granulosa Cells
summary: Ginseng polysaccharides (GPS) protect bovine ovarian granulosa cells from endoplasmic reticulum (ER) stress-induced damage primarily through activation of the PI3K/Akt signaling pathway. ER stress triggers necroptosis in granulosa cells via the RIPK3/MLKL pathway and the PERK–eIF2α–ATF4 axis, ultimately contributing to follicular atresia and reduced fertility, while GPS alleviates this damage by suppressing ER stress markers (GRP78, PERK, ATF6, IRE1) and inhibiting the unfolded protein response. GPS restores granulosa cell proliferation and estrogen synthesis, both of which are compromised by ER stress-induced suppression of key steroidogenic genes (StAR, CYP11A1, CYP19A1) and cell cycle regulators (CDK2, PCNA). The protective effects of GPS depend critically on PI3K/Akt signaling, as inhibition of this pathway with LY294002 abolishes GPS-mediated recovery of cell proliferation and estrogen production. These findings establish GPS as a promising natural compound for preserving ovarian function and reproductive outcomes by mitigating ER stress-induced cellular dysfunction.

title: The Role of Astaxanthin as an Antioxidant and Anti-Inflammatory Agent in Human Health: A Systematic Review
summary:
# Summary

Astaxanthin, a natural carotenoid found in marine sources including microalgae, krill, shrimp, and salmon, functions as a potent antioxidant and anti-inflammatory agent through multiple interconnected molecular mechanisms. The compound directly scavenges reactive oxygen species including superoxide anions and hydroxyl radicals while activating the Nrf2 signaling cascade to upregulate endogenous antioxidant enzymes such as superoxide dismutase, catalase, and glutathione peroxidase. Additionally, astaxanthin suppresses pro-inflammatory pathways by inhibiting NF-κB nuclear translocation and disrupting IL-6 feedback loops, thereby reducing key pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α, TGF-β1, IL-1β) and protecting mitochondrial integrity by reducing mitochondrial oxidative stress and preserving membrane potential. In women with endometriosis and polycystic ovary syndrome, astaxanthin downregulates endoplasmic reticulum stress-related apoptotic pathways while improving oocyte and embryo quality. Combined interventions with exercise significantly enhance astaxanthin's therapeutic benefits, improving body composition, lipid profiles, insulin sensitivity, adipokine profiles, and cardiometabolic outcomes through synergistic mechanisms involving PPARγ pathway activation and modulation of cellular signaling networks.

title: Targeting the Gut Microbiota: Mechanistic Investigation of Polyphenol Modulation of the Gut–Brain Axis in Alzheimer’s Disease
summary:
# Summary

Dysbiosis of the gut microbiota contributes to Alzheimer's disease pathology by promoting neuroinflammation and amyloid-beta accumulation through disruption of the gut-brain axis. Polyphenolic compounds including EGCG, quercetin, myricetin, and naringenin modulate this pathway through multiple mechanisms: they selectively promote beneficial bacteria such as bifidobacteria and lactobacilli while inhibiting pathogenic species, thereby increasing production of neuroprotective metabolites like GABA and short-chain fatty acids that cross the blood-brain barrier. These polyphenols directly target Alzheimer's pathology by preventing amyloid-beta fibrillogenesis and tau hyperphosphorylation, upregulating α-secretase while downregulating β- and γ-secretases, and neutralizing oxidative stress through activation of the Nrf2 antioxidant pathway and upregulation of endogenous defenses including glutathione and superoxide dismutase. Additionally, polyphenols modulate microglial activation and reduce neuroinflammatory cascades through suppression of pro-inflammatory cytokine secretion. These findings establish the gut microbiota as a key therapeutic target in Alzheimer's disease, with polyphenols representing promising multi-target interventions that operate through bidirectional communication along the gut-brain axis.

title: Phlorotannins from Ecklonia cava Regulate Dual Signaling Pathways, IL-17RA/Act1 and ERK1/2, to Suppress Ovarian Cancer Progression and Tumor-Associated Macrophage Activation
summary:
# Summary

Phlorotannins extracted from the brown alga *Ecklonia cava*—including dieckol, 7-phloroeckol, and 8,8′-bieckol—suppress ovarian cancer progression and tumor-associated macrophage (TAM) activation through dual inhibition of the IL-17RA/Act1 and ERK1/2 signaling pathways. These compounds bind directly to IL-17RA with high affinity and reduce Act1 expression, thereby attenuating the canonical IL-17 signaling cascade that normally promotes pro-inflammatory and pro-invasive gene transcription. In SKOV3 ovarian cancer cells, phlorotannins inhibited cell migration and invasion by 40–60% by downregulating multiple matrix metalloproteinases (MMP2, MMP3, MMP9, and MMP13) while simultaneously upregulating the tissue inhibitor TIMP1 through transient ERK1/2 activation. Additionally, phlorotannins suppressed TAM polarization and pro-tumorigenic cytokine production by reducing expression of CD163, CD209, IL-10, VEGF, and IL-17, alongside chemokines RANTES and MCP-1. Survival analysis of 429 ovarian cancer patients demonstrated that IL-17RA elevation correlates with advanced-stage disease, supporting the therapeutic relevance of targeting this pathway in cancer progression.

title: Therapeutic Advances of Curcumin and Nanocurcumin in Glioblastoma: Molecular Targets, Bioavailability, and Drug Delivery
summary:
# Summary

This review examines curcumin and its nanoformulated derivative as therapeutic agents for glioblastoma, demonstrating multi-targeted mechanisms of action that inhibit tumor progression. Curcumin exerts its anti-glioblastoma effects primarily by suppressing key oncogenic pathways, including PI3K/AKT/mTOR and NF-κB signaling, while simultaneously activating the p53-mediated apoptotic cascade through downregulation of MDM2 and activation of pro-apoptotic proteins Bax and Puma. The compound also reduces angiogenesis and tumor invasiveness by downregulating VEGF and matrix metalloproteinases, inhibits glioblastoma stem cell markers (CD133, Nestin, Sox2), and modulates autophagy through AMPK activation. Additionally, curcumin functions as an epigenetic regulator by inhibiting histone deacetylases and DNA methyltransferases, thereby reactivating tumor suppressors like p21 and PTEN. Nanocurcumin formulations overcome the polyphenol's inherent bioavailability limitations by improving solubility, stability, and blood-brain barrier penetration, while also enhancing sensitivity to standard chemotherapy such as temozolomide, suggesting combined therapeutic potential for this aggressive malignancy.

title: The Multidirectional Biological Activity of Resveratrol: Molecular Mechanisms, Systemic Effects and Therapeutic Potential—A Review
summary:
# Summary

Resveratrol, a polyphenolic compound found abundantly in grapes, nuts, and various fruits, exerts broad anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects through multiple interconnected molecular mechanisms. The compound suppresses the NF-κB inflammatory pathway by inhibiting IκBα and IKK kinase phosphorylation, while simultaneously reducing key pro-inflammatory cytokines including TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-6, and blocking NLRP3 inflammasome activation. Resveratrol simultaneously activates cytoprotective pathways via SIRT1, AMPK, and Nrf2 signaling, enhancing endogenous antioxidant defenses and improving mitochondrial function while reducing oxidative stress. These mechanisms support resveratrol's therapeutic potential in cardiovascular disease, hepatic steatosis, neurodegenerative disorders, metabolic dysfunction, and cancer. However, the compound's low systemic bioavailability due to rapid glucuronidation and sulfation, combined with efflux by ABC transporters, has prompted development of delivery strategies including nanoparticle systems and co-administration with piperine to enhance clinical efficacy.

title: From Tea to Topical Agent: Machine Learning and Bioinformatics Reveal KU DING Tea’s Anti-UV Ingredients and Mechanisms
summary: KU DING tea, a traditional Chinese herbal beverage rich in flavonoids and triterpenes, demonstrates significant protective effects against UV-induced skin damage through multi-target molecular mechanisms. UV radiation causes distinct types of DNA damage—UVB directly induces cyclobutane pyrimidine dimers while UVA generates reactive oxygen species—both contributing to skin cancer risk and keratinocyte injury. Machine learning and network pharmacology analyses identified 21 bioactive compounds from KU DING tea with optimal transdermal permeability, including salicylic acid, methyl salicylate, and p-coumaric acid, which were predicted to exhibit anti-UV activity with high confidence. These compounds inhibit the AGE-RAGE signaling pathway through synergistic mechanisms: 4-methoxybenzoic acid targets MAPK14 to suppress p38 phosphorylation and inflammatory signaling, while salicylic acid and p-coumaric acid cooperatively inhibit NFKB1 nuclear translocation, and salicylic acid additionally inhibits CDK4 to prevent UV-induced cell cycle abnormalities. The integrated multi-target regulation of the MAPK-NF-κB axis within the AGE-RAGE pathway collectively reduces inflammatory mediator expression and oxidative stress, providing a mechanistic rationale for developing KU DING tea-derived compounds as topical photoprotective agents.

title: Identify the therapeutic role and potential mechanism of α-cyperone in diminished ovarian reserve based on network pharmacology, molecular docking, Lip-MS and experimental validation
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that α-cyperone, a natural polyphenolic compound from Cyperi Rhizoma, exhibits therapeutic potential for treating diminished ovarian reserve (DOR) through multi-targeted anti-inflammatory and antioxidative mechanisms. The pathophysiology of DOR involves elevated inflammatory markers and oxidative stress in serum and follicular fluid, which promote NLRP3 inflammasome activation and elevated IL-1β and caspase-1 in oocytes and granulosa cells, leading to granulosa cell apoptosis and reduced oocyte quality. α-Cyperone ameliorates these pathological processes by suppressing the NF-κB and NLRP3 signaling pathways while activating the Nrf2/HO-1 pathway and SIRT1, effects validated through molecular docking showing direct binding to key proteins including MAP2K1, GSK3B, and MAPK14. In a cyclophosphamide-induced DOR granule cell model, α-cyperone enhanced KGN cell proliferation and function while normalizing aberrant expression of genes critical for ovarian physiology (MAP2K1, AKT1, ESR1/2, ERBB2, and CYP19A1), resulting in increased anti-Müllerian hormone levels and reduced reactive oxygen species accumulation. These findings establish α-cyperone as a promising natural therapeutic agent targeting the inflammatory and oxidative stress mechanisms underlying DOR.

title: Parthenolide attenuated the endometriosis-like lesions by activating autophagy and suppressing NLRP3 inflammasome activity
journal: Iranian Journal of Basic Medical Sciences
summary:
# Summary

Parthenolide, a natural sesquiterpene lactone, attenuates endometriosis-like lesions through dual mechanisms involving autophagy activation and NLRP3 inflammasome suppression. The compound activates the AMPK/ULK1 signaling pathway to enhance autophagy, as evidenced by increased phosphorylation of Beclin1, AMPK, and ULK1, along with reduced p62 accumulation. Simultaneously, parthenolide inhibits NLRP3 inflammasome activation by suppressing ASC speck formation, caspase-1 cleavage, and downstream production of IL-1β and GSDMD, thereby blocking pyroptotic cell death. These two pathways work synergistically, as autophagy acts as a negative regulator of NLRP3 inflammasome activity—a mechanism particularly relevant in endometriosis where impaired autophagy and elevated NLRP3 activity are hallmark pathological features. In mouse models, parthenolide treatment significantly reduced ectopic lesion number, surface area, and weight, demonstrating therapeutic efficacy by simultaneously enhancing cellular degradation pathways while suppressing inflammatory signaling.

title: Effect of Astragalus sinicus Ethanol Extract on Menopausal Symptoms in an Ovariectomized (OVX) Mouse Model
summary:
# Summary

Menopause involves estrogen deficiency from ovarian aging, disrupting the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis and causing vasomotor, urogenital, and psychological symptoms while increasing risks for cardiovascular disease and osteoporosis. While hormone replacement therapy effectively treats menopausal symptoms, it carries risks of breast cancer and stroke. Astragalus sinicus ethanol extract (ASE), rich in polyphenols and phytoestrogens, exhibits estrogenic activity by selectively upregulating estrogen receptor beta while downregulating estrogen receptor alpha, thereby enhancing estrogen signaling through AKT and ERK phosphorylation pathways. In an ovariectomized mouse model, ASE treatment dose-dependently alleviated menopausal symptoms by reducing body weight gain, improving lipid profiles (decreasing triglycerides, total cholesterol, and LDL-cholesterol while increasing HDL-cholesterol), and preventing osteoporosis through improved bone mineral density and bone volume fraction. These findings suggest ASE represents a promising natural alternative to hormone replacement therapy for managing menopausal symptoms without the associated adverse effects.

title: Integrated Bioinformatics and Experimental Validation Reveal Fuzheng Yi’ai Formula Induces Immunogenic Cell Death Via the PERK-eIF2α-ATF4 Pathway for Prostate Cancer Treatment
summary:
# Summary

Fuzheng Yi'ai Formula (FZYAF), a traditional Chinese medicine preparation, effectively treats prostate cancer by inducing immunogenic cell death through activation of the PERK-eIF2α-ATF4-CHOP signaling pathway. FZYAF inhibits prostate cancer cell proliferation and motility while upregulating key mediators of immunogenic cell death, including ATF4, HMGB1, calreticulin, and ATP, which are released as danger-associated molecular patterns. These damage signals promote dendritic cell maturation and increased infiltration of IFN-γ-secreting CD8+ T cells, effectively converting the immunologically cold prostate cancer microenvironment into an immune-responsive state capable of generating protective anti-tumor immunity. The critical role of the PERK signaling axis was confirmed by demonstrating that PERK inhibition significantly attenuated the therapeutic and vaccine-induced protective immune effects of FZYAF. This integrated bioinformatics and experimental approach identifies FZYAF as a promising immunotherapeutic strategy that overcomes the inherent immune-suppressive characteristics of prostate cancer through a well-defined molecular mechanism.

title: Natural compounds in ovarian cancer: mechanistic insights and therapeutic potential
summary:
# Summary

Quercetin, EGCG (epigallocatechin gallate), and curcumin are polyphenolic compounds that demonstrate potent anti-ovarian cancer activity through multiple interconnected mechanisms. Quercetin inhibits cell proliferation and induces mitochondrial-mediated apoptosis by downregulating anti-apoptotic proteins (Bcl-2, survivin) while upregulating pro-apoptotic markers (Bax, caspases 3/8/9, cytochrome c), and simultaneously suppresses metastasis by inhibiting matrix metalloproteinases and EMT transcription factors. EGCG exhibits comparable effects through G1-phase cell cycle arrest via upregulation of p21 and downregulation of cyclin D1/CDK4, coupled with activation of the PTEN/AKT/mTOR pathway and reduction of inflammatory mediators (COX-1/2, PGE2). Curcumin modulates proliferation and apoptosis while remodeling the tumor microenvironment through macrophage polarization and EMT suppression, with additional mechanisms including G2/M arrest and NF-κB pathway inhibition. All three compounds enhance chemotherapy efficacy by reversing drug resistance through P-glycoprotein downregulation and enhanced intracellular drug accumulation, rendering chemoresistant cells more sensitive to conventional therapies like cisplatin and paclitaxel. These findings establish polyphenolic compounds as promising adjunctive agents with multi-target mechanisms that synergistically enhance conventional ovarian cancer treatments.

title: Bioinspired and stimuli-responsive nanocomposites for targeted therapy in ovarian and endometrial tumors
summary: This review examines bioinspired nanocomposites as advanced delivery systems for treating ovarian and endometrial cancers, which face challenges of late-stage diagnosis and rising incidence respectively. The authors demonstrate that stimuli-responsive nanocomposites—incorporating natural polysaccharides like chitosan and alginate, carbon nanotubes, and graphene—substantially improve drug delivery efficiency while reducing systemic toxicity compared to conventional chemotherapy. Multiple triggering mechanisms are highlighted, including pH-responsive systems that exploit the acidic tumor microenvironment, enzyme-responsive nanocomposites that activate in response to matrix metalloproteinases, magnetic nanoparticles enabling targeted delivery and hyperthermia therapy, and temperature-sensitive materials. Nanoparticle design parameters critically influence efficacy: rod-shaped architectures achieve prolonged circulation and enhanced tumor uptake, while charge modulation controls cellular internalization and minimizes off-target binding. Three-dimensional scaffolds and hydrogels further enhance therapeutic outcomes by enabling sustained drug release while facilitating cell proliferation, with self-assembly architectures capable of spatiotemporally regulating both drug delivery and cell growth. These multifunctional nanocomposites represent a paradigm shift toward precision oncology, addressing the fundamental limitations of conventional chemotherapy through rational engineering of delivery vehicles.

title: Antimicrobial activity of natural quinone-methide triterpenoids celastrol and pristimerin against pathogenic Neisseria
summary: This study investigates the antimicrobial properties of two natural quinone-methide triterpenoids, celastrol and pristimerin, derived from *Tripterygium wilfordii*, against pathogenic *Neisseria* species. Both compounds effectively inhibited and killed *Neisseria gonorrhoeae* and *Neisseria meningitidis* strains, including multiple serogroups, with antimicrobial activity attributed to disruption of cytoplasmic membranes and covalent modification of cysteine residues in bacterial proteins. While celastrol demonstrated greater potency with lower minimum inhibitory and bactericidal concentrations, it exhibited substantial cytotoxicity and off-target effects including cardiotoxicity, hepatotoxicity, and neurotoxicity, resulting in poor selectivity. In contrast, pristimerin maintained potent antimicrobial activity while demonstrating a favorable safety margin with minimal cytotoxicity and a selectivity index approximately 16-fold higher than celastrol. The *Neisseria* MtrE efflux pump conferred resistance to both compounds, suggesting bacteria may employ active efflux mechanisms to counteract their effects. These findings identify pristimerin as a promising scaffold for developing therapeutics against multidrug-resistant *Neisseria* infections, particularly gonorrhea.

title: Buyang Huanwu Decoction Alleviates Bleomycin‐Induced Pulmonary Fibrosis via Wnt/β‐catenin and TGF‐β1/Smad Pathways
summary:
# Summary

Buyang Huanwu Decoction (BHD), a traditional herbal remedy containing multiple bioactive compounds including amygdalin, formononetin, ferulic acid, kaempferol, and paeoniflorin, effectively alleviates bleomycin-induced pulmonary fibrosis through dual inhibition of the Wnt/β-catenin and TGF-β1/Smad signaling pathways. In a rat model, BHD treatment significantly reduced lung inflammation, collagen deposition, and myofibroblast differentiation by suppressing key pathway components including Wnt3a, glycogen synthase kinase 3β, β-catenin, TGF-β1, and phosphorylated Smad3. The supernatant fraction of BHD demonstrated superior bioavailability of active compounds, particularly amygdalin, formononetin, and ferulic acid, and exhibited stronger therapeutic effects than the precipitation component alone. Beyond the two primary pathways, BHD exerts additional therapeutic mechanisms through anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activities, while also modulating AGE-RAGE and PI3K-Akt signaling pathways. These findings establish BHD as a multi-target therapeutic agent for pulmonary fibrosis, with its diverse polyphenolic and natural compounds working synergistically to prevent fibroblast-to-myofibroblast transformation and excessive extracellular matrix deposition.

title: Anti-Inflammatory Effects of Lipoxin A4 in Salmonella Typhimurium-Induced Enteritis in Wenchang Chickens
journal: Animals : an Open Access Journal from MDPI
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that lipoxin A4 (LXA4), a specialized pro-resolving mediator derived from arachidonic acid, effectively mitigates Salmonella Typhimurium-induced intestinal inflammation in Wenchang chickens through modulation of immune cell activation and suppression of pro-inflammatory pathways. Salmonella infection triggers severe intestinal inflammation, diarrhea, weight loss, and immunosuppression (reduced thymus and bursa indices) accompanied by elevated expression of pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α) and COX-2 in cecal tissues. LXA4 treatment attenuated these pathological effects by downregulating inflammatory mediators and signaling through the ALX/FPR2 receptor to inhibit NF-κB and modulate MAPK pathways, thereby suppressing pro-inflammatory cytokine production. Additionally, LXA4 regulated neutrophil and macrophage functions, inhibiting neutrophil chemotaxis and enhancing M2 macrophage polarization, while promoting tissue repair mechanisms. The antagonist Boc-2 successfully blocked LXA4-mediated anti-inflammatory effects, confirming the essential role of ALX/FPR2 signaling in the protective mechanism. These findings establish LXA4 as a promising therapeutic agent for treating bacterial enteritis by resolving inflammation and restoring intestinal homeostasis.

title: Effects of N-Acetylcysteine and Alpha-Ketoglutarate on OVCAR3 Ovarian Cancer Cells: Insights from Integrative Bioinformatics and Experimental Validation
summary:
# Summary

N-acetylcysteine and alpha-ketoglutarate exhibit potent anti-cancer effects against OVCAR3 ovarian cancer cells, with combined treatment producing synergistic reduction in cell viability, induction of apoptotic death, and suppression of cell migration and colony formation. Mechanistically, these compounds act through overlapping pathways involving 70 shared target genes associated with key regulatory proteins including AKT1, caspase-3, HSP90AA1, and SRC, which converge on apoptosis, cellular stress responses, and cell migration pathways. Alpha-ketoglutarate functions as a central metabolic hub influencing cellular energy production, redox homeostasis, signal transduction, and gene expression, while N-acetylcysteine modulates redox balance through glutathione synthesis enhancement and NRF2 activation. Pathway enrichment analysis identified critical involvement of cancer-related signaling, AGE-RAGE signaling, and oxidative stress response networks in the anti-tumor mechanism. The findings suggest that these natural compounds warrant further investigation as potential therapeutic agents for ovarian cancer treatment, particularly given the epidemiological link between endometriosis and ovarian cancer risk.

title: Recent Advancements in Bioactive Natural Products and Nanoparticle-Mediated Drug Delivery in Cancer Therapy
summary:
# Summary

This comprehensive review examines bioactive natural products and nanoparticle delivery systems as promising strategies in cancer therapy. Natural compounds including polyphenols (curcumin, resveratrol, apigenin), alkaloids (vincristine, berberine), and sesquiterpene lactones (parthenolide, artemisinin) demonstrate potent anticancer mechanisms through apoptosis induction, angiogenesis inhibition, and cell cycle arrest via multiple signaling pathways including MAPK, JAK2/STAT3, and Wnt/β-catenin. Conventional chemotherapeutic agents cause significant systemic toxicities such as nausea, vomiting, alopecia, and neuropathy by disrupting metabolic processes in normal cells. Nanoparticle-mediated drug delivery systems—including liposomes, polymeric, and metallic nanoparticles—effectively overcome these limitations by enhancing tumor accumulation, improving bioavailability and stability of natural compounds, and reducing off-target toxicity. Dietary sources of these bioactive compounds from vegetables, fruits, spices, and grains offer preventive potential by regulating apoptosis and cell cycle progression, suggesting that integrating natural products with advanced nanodelivery platforms represents a viable approach to improving therapeutic efficacy while minimizing adverse effects in cancer treatment.

title: Anti-Inflammatory Effects of Polyphenols in Brain Microvascular Endothelial Cells Stimulated with TNF-α
summary: TNF-α stimulation of brain microvascular endothelial cells induces robust production of pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, IL-8) and upregulates the adhesion molecule VCAM-1, establishing a model of endothelial inflammation. Multiple polyphenols—including chrysin, myricetin, resveratrol, and curcumin—counteract this TNF-α-driven inflammation through overlapping but distinct mechanisms. These polyphenols reduce pro-inflammatory cytokine and enzyme expression (IL-1β, IL-6, IL-8, COX-2, iNOS) and suppress key signaling pathways including NF-κB, MAPK kinases (ERK, JNK, p38), and NLRP3 inflammasome activation. Notably, resveratrol activates the sirtuin-1 deacetylase to suppress downstream inflammatory signaling, while chrysin activates PPAR-γ; myricetin additionally targets TLR2/mTOR signaling. Beyond inflammation, chrysin, resveratrol, and curcumin enhance endothelial cell viability and reduce reactive oxygen species production. These findings suggest polyphenols offer multi-targeted anti-inflammatory potential relevant to protecting microvascular endothelial barrier function in pathologies associated with chronic inflammation.

title: Optimization of Extraction Process by Response Surface Methodology, Composition Analysis and Antioxidant Activity of Total Flavonoids from Scutellaria baicalensis Georgi
summary:
# Summary

This study optimized the extraction of total flavonoids from Scutellaria baicalensis Georgi, a traditional Chinese medicinal herb rich in bioactive compounds including baicalin, baicalein, wogonoside, and wogonin. Using response surface methodology, the researchers identified key extraction parameters—ethanol concentration, temperature, time, and liquid-to-solid ratio—that significantly influence flavonoid yield. The herb's extract contains multiple flavonoid subclasses with diverse biological activities: flavones demonstrate antioxidant effects through free radical scavenging and Nrf2/ARE pathway activation alongside anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and immunomodulatory properties; isoflavones regulate endocrine function and inhibit hormone-dependent tumor cell proliferation while reducing menopausal symptoms and cholesterol; flavonols scavenge free radicals and enhance cardiovascular function by reducing blood pressure and vascular inflammation; and dihydroflavonoids inhibit inflammatory responses via NF-κB and MAPK pathway regulation while reducing oxidative stress and inducing cancer cell apoptosis. These findings support the traditional use of S. baicalensis and suggest its potential therapeutic application across multiple disease conditions through complementary mechanisms of polyphenolic compounds.

title: Xanthohumol: Mechanistic Actions and Emerging Evidence as a Multi-Target Natural Nutraceutical
summary: Xanthohumol, a prenylated chalcone flavonoid from hops, functions as a multi-target natural compound that modulates inflammation, oxidative stress, metabolism, and cell survival through coordinated inhibition of pro-oncogenic pathways and activation of cytoprotective mechanisms. The compound suppresses critical signaling cascades including NF-κB, PI3K/Akt/mTOR, STAT3, HIF-1α, and MAPK while simultaneously activating the Nrf2/ARE and AMPK pathways, thereby regulating redox homeostasis, mitochondrial integrity, apoptosis, autophagy, and ferroptosis. In cancer models, xanthohumol demonstrates broad-spectrum anticancer activity by inhibiting cell proliferation, inducing G1 and G2/M cell cycle arrest through modulation of cyclins and p21/p53, and triggering both intrinsic and extrinsic apoptotic pathways involving caspase activation and altered Bax/Bcl-2 ratios. The compound exhibits selective toxicity toward malignant cells over normal counterparts and suppresses the tumor microenvironment by reducing angiogenesis through NF-κB-dependent downregulation of VEGF and IL-8, while also inhibiting migration and metastasis. Beyond oncology, preclinical evidence supports xanthohumol's therapeutic potential in neurodegenerative diseases, metabolic disorders, cardiovascular dysfunction, and inflammatory skin conditions. Human studies confirm xanthohumol is well tolerated and reduces oxidative DNA damage and inflammatory cytokine release, supporting its development as a therapeutic nutraceutical across multiple disease contexts.

title: Anticancer Potential of Thieno[2,3-d]pyrimidine Derivatives in Oral Carcinoma Models
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that thieno[2,3-d]pyrimidine derivatives represent a promising class of anticancer agents against oral squamous cell carcinoma (OSCC), a disease characterized by aggressive progression and high recurrence rates. The most potent compounds, particularly Compounds 1, 5, and 6, inhibited OSCC cell growth through multiple mechanisms including S-phase cell cycle arrest, apoptosis induction, and disruption of the actin cytoskeleton. These thienopyrimidines exert their anticancer effects by functioning as tyrosine kinase inhibitors that target epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) and suppress critical signaling pathways including PI3K/Akt, MAPK/ERK, and NF-κB. Compound 6 demonstrated superior selectivity for low-metastatic cancer cells while maintaining strong activity against high-metastatic variants, suggesting potential clinical utility. The findings establish thieno[2,3-d]pyrimidine scaffolds as viable foundations for developing targeted therapies against OSCC, with structural modifications such as halogenation further enhancing compound cytotoxicity.

title: Beneficial Effects of Fisetin, a Senotherapeutic Compound, in Women’s Reproductive Health and Diseases: Evidence from In Vitro to Clinical Studies
summary:
# Summary

Fisetin, a dietary flavonol found abundantly in strawberries, apples, persimmons, and onions, demonstrates potent senotherapeutic and antioxidant properties relevant to women's reproductive health disorders including endometriosis. The compound exerts its protective effects through multiple interconnected mechanisms: it enhances antioxidant enzyme activity (SOD1 and CAT), reduces reactive oxygen species generation, and suppresses pro-inflammatory signaling pathways including NF-κB and MAPK while maintaining intracellular glutathione levels and promoting Nrf2-mediated antioxidant defense gene expression. Beyond oxidative stress mitigation, fisetin functions as a senolytic agent capable of eliminating senescent cells and suppressing the senescence-associated secretory phenotype, thereby modulating key pathogenic processes across multiple reproductive contexts including ovarian aging, fibrosis, angiogenesis, and endometriosis. Evidence from preclinical and clinical studies indicates that fisetin can support ovarian function and hormonal balance while simultaneously suppressing pathological cell growth in gynecologic conditions, offering a promising non-hormonal intervention for endometriosis and related reproductive disorders characterized by chronic pain and infertility.

title: Phytocannabinoids and Male Fertility: Implications of Cannabis sativa and the Endocannabinoid System in Reproductive Regulation
summary:
# Summary

Cannabis sativa produces multiple phytocannabinoids, particularly THC and CBD, which interact with the endocannabinoid system through CB1 and CB2 receptors expressed in reproductive tissues including the testes and epididymis. THC disrupts the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis, causing reduced luteinizing hormone and testosterone levels while impairing mitochondrial activity and inducing abnormal sperm morphology. Cannabinoids broadly modulate key male fertility parameters including testosterone synthesis, sperm motility, morphology, and capacitation through receptor signaling. While CBD exhibits anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties with potential therapeutic applications for anxiety, psychosis, and epilepsy, chronic or excessive cannabis exposure adversely affects male fertility. The complex interplay between exogenous cannabinoids from Cannabis sativa and the endogenous endocannabinoid system—which regulates multiple physiological processes including mood, energy balance, and glucose metabolism—underscores the importance of understanding these phytochemicals' long-term reproductive consequences.

title: Cannabidiol mitigates high-fat-diet-induced early-stage inflammation in two adipose tissue fat depots of Wistar rats
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that cannabidiol (CBD) mitigates early-stage inflammation induced by high-fat diet consumption in adipose tissue through multiple mechanisms targeting lipid metabolism and inflammatory pathways. High-fat diet promotes obesity-associated inflammation by causing adipose tissue hypertrophy, which triggers hypoxia, oxidative stress, and necrosis, ultimately leading to elevated production of pro-inflammatory cytokines including IL-6, TNF-α, and MCP-1. CBD reduces the accumulation of key lipid mediators—particularly arachidonic acid, free fatty acids, diacylglycerols, and triacylglycerols—across both visceral and subcutaneous adipose tissue depots, thereby decreasing substrate availability for inflammatory eicosanoid synthesis. Additionally, CBD suppresses the expression of enzymes critical for eicosanoid production, namely cyclooxygenase-1 and -2 (COX-1 and COX-2) and 5-lipoxygenase (5-LOX) and 12/15-lipoxygenase (12/15-LOX), while simultaneously upregulating anti-inflammatory and immunomodulatory cytokines such as IL-4, IL-12 p70, and GM-CSF. These findings indicate that CBD's anti-inflammatory efficacy operates through simultaneous reduction of pro-inflammatory lipid precursors and enzyme expression while promoting protective immune responses, positioning it as a potential therapeutic agent for metabolic inflammation.

title: Myrrh ameliorates endometriosis by enhancing ER stress-related apoptotic cell death
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that myrrh extract ameliorates endometriosis by triggering endoplasmic reticulum (ER) stress-induced apoptosis in endometriotic cells. Oral administration of myrrh significantly reduced endometriotic foci weight in vivo at low doses (0.7–3.5 mg/kg/day) and selectively targeted endometriotic stromal cells (12Z) over normal stromal cells in vitro. The therapeutic mechanism involves myrrh upregulating key components of the unfolded protein response pathway, including critical transcription factors PPP1R15A, DDIT3, ATF6, ATF4, and ERN1, which collectively activate ER stress-dependent apoptosis. At the molecular level, myrrh promotes mitochondrial apoptosis through Bax activation, reduces anti-apoptotic Bcl-2 expression, and activates caspase-3 and caspase-9 to cleave their substrates. The selectivity of myrrh for pathological endometrial cells, combined with its documented anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties via bioactive compounds like oleanolic acid, positions it as a promising natural therapeutic agent for endometriosis with potentially fewer side effects than conventional treatments.

title: Repurposing metformin for cardioprotection: mechanisms and therapeutic potential across cardiovascular pathologies
summary:
# Summary

Metformin, traditionally a first-line antidiabetic agent, exerts cardioprotective effects through multiple interconnected mechanisms centered on AMPK activation. Upon binding presenilin enhancer 2 on lysosomal membranes and inhibiting mitochondrial complex I, metformin activates AMPK, which enhances endothelial nitric oxide synthase activity, stabilizes mitochondrial membrane potential, and prevents permeability transition pore opening—collectively preserving cardiac energy metabolism and structure. Metformin's cardioprotective actions extend to suppressing pro-inflammatory pathways including the NLRP3 inflammasome, NF-κB signaling, and production of IL-1β, TNF-α, and IL-6, while simultaneously upregulating anti-apoptotic factors like Bcl-2 and Bcl-xL and antioxidant defenses through Nrf2 and Klotho. Clinical and preclinical evidence demonstrates that metformin reduces myocardial infarction risk, major adverse cardiovascular events, left ventricular hypertrophy, and oxidative stress in patients with diabetes and heart failure, while also protecting against doxorubicin-induced cardiotoxicity and ischemia-reperfusion injury. These findings establish metformin as a promising therapeutic agent for diverse cardiovascular pathologies beyond its conventional glucose-lowering indication.

title: Mechanism, Dose, and Administration of Dexmedetomidine in Managing Visceral Pain Associated With Surgery: A Narrative Review
journal: Medical Science Monitor: International Medical Journal of Experimental and Clinical Research
summary: Dexmedetomidine (DEX), a highly selective alpha-2 adrenoreceptor agonist, effectively manages visceral pain associated with surgery through multiple complementary mechanisms. DEX activates alpha-2 adrenoreceptors in the locus coeruleus and spinal cord, leading to decreased release of pain-signaling neuropeptides including substance P, glutamate, and calcitonin gene-related peptide, while simultaneously suppressing peripheral and central sensitization of pain pathways. The drug induces potent sedative, analgesic, anxiolytic, and anti-inflammatory effects by reducing production of inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6, IL-8), promoting macrophage polarization from pro-inflammatory M1 to anti-inflammatory M2 phenotypes, and enhancing intestinal barrier integrity through upregulation of tight junction proteins claudin and occludin. Clinical evidence demonstrates that DEX effectively attenuates visceral pain responses, prolongs thermal withdrawal latency, and reduces visceromotor responses to abdominal distension, with enhanced efficacy when combined with esketamine. The drug's preemptive use at doses of 40 μg/kg significantly decreases inflammatory cytokine release and cervical vagal nerve activity, while also improving sleep quality through sympatholytic effects that facilitate postoperative recovery.

title: Exploring the Mechanism of Therapeutic Effects of Polydatin in Lung Ischemia-Reperfusion Injury by Network Pharmacology and Experiment Validation
summary:
# Summary

Polydatin, a natural polyphenolic compound isolated from *Polygonum cuspidatum*, effectively treats lung ischemia-reperfusion injury (LIRI) by simultaneously attenuating oxidative stress, inflammatory responses, and apoptosis. The compound activates antioxidant defenses by increasing superoxide dismutase (SOD) activity while reducing reactive oxygen species (ROS) and malondialdehyde (MDA) levels, thereby protecting lung epithelial cells from H/R-induced damage. Polydatin exerts its multi-target protective effects through modulation of key signaling pathways including NF-κB and AKT1, upregulating pro-survival genes (AKT1, ALB) while downregulating pro-apoptotic genes (TP53), and suppressing pro-inflammatory cytokines including TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-1β. Network pharmacology and experimental validation in A549 lung cells confirmed that polydatin enhances cell viability and reduces apoptotic cell death, establishing it as a promising therapeutic agent for ischemia-reperfusion injuries. These findings suggest polydatin's therapeutic potential extends beyond LIRI and may have relevance for other pathological conditions involving oxidative stress and inflammatory dysregulation.

title: Micro/nanoplastics induce thyroid follicular cell pyroptosis to trigger thyrotoxicity by activating NF-κB signaling
summary:
# Summary

Micro and nanoplastics (MNP) induce thyrotoxicity by triggering pyroptosis in thyroid follicular cells through activation of the NF-κB signaling pathway. Exposure to both microplastics and nanoplastics upregulates key pyroptotic mediators including NLRP3 inflammasome, caspase-1, and gasdermin-D (GSDMD), leading to dramatic increases in inflammatory cytokines IL-1β, IL-18, and TNF-α. This MNP-induced cell death results in significant disruption of thyroid architecture, characterized by reduced follicle diameter and epithelial cell thickness, suppressed thyroid hormone production (decreased fT3 and fT4 levels), and compensatory elevation of TSH through negative feedback mechanisms. Additionally, MNP exposure simultaneously triggers apoptotic pathways, evidenced by downregulation of anti-apoptotic BCL-2 and upregulation of pro-apoptotic BAX and caspase-3 proteins. These findings establish micro and nanoplastics as environmental toxins that compromise thyroid function through dual mechanisms of pyroptotic and apoptotic cell death pathways, with nanoplastics demonstrating more severe effects on hormone suppression and inflammatory responses.

title: Ginsenoside Rg1 delays the senescence of adipose-derived stem cells: network pharmacology and experimental validation
summary: Ginsenoside Rg1, a polyphenol compound from ginseng, effectively delays senescence in adipose-derived stem cells (ADSCs) by activating the PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling pathway. Treatment with Rg1 enhances ADSC viability and stemness while reducing senescence markers including p16, p21, and senescence-associated β-galactosidase positivity, alongside decreased DNA damage and restored telomere length. Additionally, Rg1 suppresses pro-inflammatory cytokine expression (IL-6, TNF-α, IL-1β) and upregulates immune checkpoint proteins CD59 and CD73, thereby promoting immune evasion capacity critical for MSC therapeutic efficacy. The compound restores phosphorylation of PI3K, AKT, and mTOR that are suppressed during oxidative stress-induced senescence, while promoting cell cycle progression and upregulating pluripotency factors OCT4, Nanog, and SOX2. Network pharmacology identified AKT1 as a central hub protein targeted by Rg1, with molecular docking confirming strong binding affinity. These findings suggest Rg1 holds therapeutic potential for enhancing MSC quality and regenerative capacity in cell-based therapies.

title: From nutrition to therapeutics: the diverse inflammopharmacological and biomedical roles of astaxanthin
summary: Astaxanthin, a xanthophyll carotenoid primarily derived from the microalga *Hematococcus lacustris* and naturally found in marine crustaceans, exhibits potent anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that exceed those of α-tocopherol by approximately tenfold. The compound exerts its therapeutic effects through regulation of critical signaling pathways including NF-κB, Nrf2, MAPK, and TGF-β/Smad, while enhancing endogenous antioxidant defenses, promoting mitochondrial health, and downregulating inflammatory mediators and proinflammatory cytokines. Preclinical and clinical evidence demonstrates astaxanthin's efficacy in treating diverse conditions spanning cardiovascular disease (atherosclerosis, myocardial ischemia, hypertension), hepatic disease (nonalcoholic fatty liver disease), neurodegenerative disorders (Alzheimer's and Parkinson's disease), and female reproductive health conditions including polycystic ovary syndrome. Additionally, astaxanthin supplementation enhances immune and antioxidant defenses, increases antioxidant enzyme activity including SOD and hepatic HSP70, and reduces lipid accumulation and body weight while promoting beneficial gut microbiota composition. The compound's unique structural characteristics enable integration into lipid bilayers, facilitating ROS quenching, cholesterol efflux from macrophages, and prevention of endothelial-mesenchymal transition, establishing astaxanthin as a multifaceted inflammopharmacological agent with broad therapeutic potential across multiple organ systems.

title: Adolescent exposure to polystyrene nanoplastics induces male reproductive damage via the microbiome-gut-testis axis
summary:
# Summary

Adolescent exposure to polystyrene nanoplastics (PS-NPs) causes dose-dependent male reproductive damage through a microbiome-gut-testis axis mechanism. PS-NPs penetrate the intestinal epithelium and blood-testis barrier, triggering gut microbial dysbiosis that compromises intestinal barrier integrity and elevates circulating lipopolysaccharide levels, which activate the TLR4/MyD88/NF-κB signaling pathway and propagate inflammatory responses to testicular tissues. Within the testes, PS-NPs activate NF-κB/IL-17/HIF-1 signaling while inhibiting PPAR-γ signaling, leading to spermatogenic arrest, increased DNA damage, apoptosis, and a compromised blood-testis barrier characterized by disrupted spermatogenesis and reduced sperm quality parameters. The study demonstrates that the dysbiotic microbiota itself is causally linked to reproductive toxicity, as fecal microbiota transplantation from PS-NPs-exposed donors reproduced damage in healthy recipients. Importantly, dimethyl itaconate, an immunometabolite with anti-inflammatory properties, effectively mitigated reproductive toxicity by restoring gut barrier integrity and inhibiting inflammatory cytokine cascades, offering a potential therapeutic intervention for PS-NPs-induced reproductive harm.

title: Targeting cancer hallmarks using resveratrol: isolation, formulation, and mechanisms of action
summary: Resveratrol, a polyphenolic compound found in grapes and other plants, exerts multifaceted anticancer effects by targeting multiple hallmark pathways involved in tumor development and progression. The compound reduces reactive oxygen species and lipid peroxidation by activating the JNK/c-JUN signaling pathway and enhancing antioxidant enzyme expression, while simultaneously inhibiting pro-cancer pathways including PI3K/Akt and NF-κB signaling, which normally promote cell proliferation and survival. Resveratrol also downregulates key metastasis-associated proteins including MMP-9, CXCR4, and FAK, and activates the SIRT1 pathway to regulate cellular metabolism and longevity. Additionally, resveratrol modulates the tumor microenvironment by inhibiting angiogenic pathways and counteracting inflammatory mediators that promote genetic instability and immunosuppression. The compound's ability to induce apoptosis through both intrinsic and extrinsic pathways, combined with its low toxicity profile, positions resveratrol as a promising natural therapeutic agent for cancer prevention and treatment. These anticancer properties are complemented by well-documented anti-inflammatory, cardioprotective, and anti-aging effects, making resveratrol a multifunctional bioactive compound worthy of further clinical investigation.

title: The dual roles of natural cannabidiol in combating oxidative stress and inflammation: A potential intestinal guardian
summary: Cannabidiol (CBD), a non-psychoactive phytocannabinoid derived from Cannabis sativa, exerts dual protective effects in the gastrointestinal tract through potent antioxidant and anti-inflammatory mechanisms. CBD combats oxidative stress by directly scavenging reactive oxygen species, chelating transition metals, and inhibiting ROS-generating enzymes (xanthine oxidase and NADPH oxidase), while simultaneously activating the Nrf2–Keap1 antioxidant pathway through AMPK/SIRT3 signaling to upregulate cytoprotective enzymes including catalase, superoxide dismutase, and glutathione. On the inflammatory front, CBD suppresses the nuclear factor-κB (NF-κB) and NLRP3 inflammasome pathways, thereby reducing production of pro-inflammatory cytokines TNF-α and interleukin-1β, and preserves intestinal barrier integrity through endocannabinoid system engagement. Additionally, CBD modulates the gut microbiota composition and elevates short-chain fatty acids (butyrate, acetate, and propionate), which further support redox homeostasis and intestinal barrier maintenance. These integrated mechanisms demonstrate CBD's therapeutic potential in inflammatory bowel disease and colorectal cancer, positioning it as a promising intestinal guardian through correction of oxidative stress and redox-driven inflammation.

title: Ocular and Systemic Pharmacokinetics of Baicalein and Baicalin After Intravitreal Injection and Oral Administration in Mice
summary:
# Summary

Baicalein (BA), a natural polyphenol from Scutellaria baicalensis, demonstrates therapeutic potential for glaucoma through dual intraocular pressure-lowering and neuroprotective mechanisms. In this pharmacokinetic study, baicalein and its glucuronidated metabolite baicalin (BG) were tracked following both intravitreal injection and oral administration in mice, revealing that the compound rapidly accumulates in ocular tissues with peak concentrations at 5 minutes post-injection and exhibits notable anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. Oral baicalein administration achieved ocular concentrations comparable to invasive intravitreal injection, with both delivery routes resulting in local metabolic conversion to baicalin, suggesting a circulating reservoir mechanism via enterohepatic recirculation. Despite a short ocular half-life of approximately 29 minutes, baicalein demonstrated sustained neuroprotective effects in a retinal ischemia/reperfusion injury model by preserving retinal ganglion cells and inhibiting microglia activation. These findings establish non-invasive oral administration of baicalein as an effective alternative to intravitreal injection for treating glaucoma, combining favorable pharmacokinetics with low cytotoxicity and potent biological activity.

title: Correlation between NFκB Signaling and Na+, K+‑ATPase Inhibition in Vincristine-Induced
summary: This study demonstrates that vincristine (VCR), a chemotherapy agent, induces neurotoxicity through a coordinated mechanism involving nuclear factor kappa B (NFκB) hyperactivation and sodium-potassium ATPase (Na+,K+-ATPase) inhibition. VCR significantly increased NFκB expression and suppressed Na+,K+-ATPase activity in the cerebral cortex and spinal cord, leading to impaired axonal depolarization and neuronal dysfunction. These molecular alterations correlated directly with behavioral deficits, including impaired short- and long-term memory, depressive-like behavior, and anxiety-like behavior in both male and female mice, with females showing additional p21 overexpression as a unique neurotoxicity marker. The natural compound 4-phenylselanyl quinoline (4-PSQ), possessing antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, effectively reversed VCR-induced neurotoxicity by normalizing NFκB expression, restoring Na+,K+-ATPase activity, and rescuing cognitive and emotional functions through serotonergic modulation of 5-HT receptors and upregulation of the neuroplasticity marker NCAM. This research reveals the central role of NFκB-Na+,K+-ATPase cross-talk in chemotherapy-related cognitive and emotional dysfunction, while establishing 4-PSQ as a promising neuroprotective agent against vincristine-induced neurological damage.

title: Resveratrol supplementation improves uterine immune resolution in mares susceptible to persistent breeding-induced endometritis
summary: Persistent breeding-induced endometritis (PBIE) in mares, which accounts for approximately 15% of broodmare infertility, is characterized by a defective uterine immune response that fails to transition from pro-inflammatory to anti-inflammatory signaling. The polyphenolic compound resveratrol was evaluated as a therapeutic intervention, demonstrating that oral supplementation successfully modulates the uterine inflammatory environment by reducing neutrophilia and decreasing pro-inflammatory cytokine production, particularly IL-1β, while increasing IL-6—a pleiotropic cytokine with documented anti-inflammatory and endometrial repair functions that PBIE-susceptible mares fail to produce adequately. The reduction in IL-1β and IL-8 (a key neutrophil chemotactic signal) was particularly significant, as these cytokines normally amplify uterine inflammation and drive persistent neutrophil infiltration, the central mechanism underlying PBIE-associated infertility. Notably, resveratrol exerts these effects through its well-established anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, including reduction of reactive oxygen species and pro-inflammatory mediators like interferon-gamma and tumor necrosis factor. These findings suggest that resveratrol supplementation improves uterine immune resolution in PBIE-susceptible mares by shifting cytokine production toward a resolution phenotype, though the supplement did not alter uterine fluid accumulation in this study.

title: Anti-Inflammatory Lindolin Alkaloids Repress the Transcription of the Microsomal Prostaglandin E2 Synthase‑1 Gene in Macrophages
summary:
# Summary

Lindolin alkaloids, natural compounds isolated from the early-diverging fungus Linderina pennispora, function as selective inhibitors of prostaglandin E2 (PGE2) biosynthesis by specifically downregulating microsomal prostaglandin E2 synthase-1 (mPGES-1) gene expression in macrophages. Unlike conventional NSAIDs that broadly inhibit cyclooxygenase enzymes, lindolins—particularly those bearing ortho-substituted carboxylic acid or ester groups—suppress mPGES-1 transcription and protein levels without affecting COX-2 expression or activity, resulting in markedly decreased PGE2 production while leaving other lipid mediators largely unaffected. This selective targeting of mPGES-1 represents a promising therapeutic approach since mPGES-1 has emerged as a PGE2-specific drug target that avoids the side effects associated with broad COX inhibition. The structure-activity relationship reveals that an ortho-positioned carboxylic acid moiety is critical for anti-inflammatory potency, a feature shared with clinically used compounds like tranilast and plant-derived avenanthramides, suggesting a common molecular basis for selective mPGES-1 inhibition across diverse natural product scaffolds.

title: Catalpol: an natural multifunctional iridoid glycoside with promising therapeutic properties
summary: Catalpol, an iridoid glycoside extracted from the medicinal plant Rehmannia glutinosa, demonstrates broad therapeutic potential across multiple organ systems through its ability to modulate key inflammatory and oxidative stress pathways. The compound exerts protective effects in kidney disease by suppressing the TLR4/NF-κB inflammatory axis and activating the Nrf2 and SIRT1 antioxidant pathways, thereby reducing fibrosis markers and inflammatory cytokines including TNF-α and IL-6. In cardiovascular and hepatic contexts, catalpol activates complementary cytoprotective pathways including SIRT-mediated signaling, PPAR-γ, and AKT/mTOR cascades while simultaneously inhibiting pro-fibrotic Wnt/β-catenin and TGF-β signaling, thereby reducing tissue damage from various etiologies including drug-induced toxicity. The compound also addresses metabolic dysfunction in diabetes and fatty liver disease by enhancing AMPK and promoting mitochondrial biogenesis through PGC-1α/NRF1 activation, which improves glucose metabolism and insulin sensitivity. Additionally, catalpol protects against pulmonary and hepatic fibrosis while mitigating liver-lung cross-organ injury through bile acid regulation and simultaneous blockade of Ang II and TGF-β pathways. These multifaceted mechanisms position catalpol as a promising therapeutic agent warranting further clinical investigation.

title: Exploring the Molecular Mechanism of Shaoyao Gancao Decoction for Trigeminal Neuralgia Based on Network Pharmacology, Molecular Docking, and Molecular Dynamics Simulation
summary:
# Summary

This study employed network pharmacology, molecular docking, and molecular dynamics simulations to elucidate the therapeutic mechanisms of Shaoyao Gancao Decoction (SGD), a traditional herbal formula comprising Paeonia lactiflora and Glycyrrhiza glabra, in treating trigeminal neuralgia. The research identified seven hub genes (ESR1, ESR2, JUN, TP53, STAT3, BCL2, and AKT1) as key therapeutic targets involved in the disease pathogenesis. SGD demonstrated potent anti-inflammatory effects by downregulating pro-inflammatory cytokines TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-6 in lipopolysaccharide-stimulated microglial cells, thereby reducing neuroinflammation, which is central to trigeminal neuralgia pathogenesis. Multiple active polyphenolic compounds from SGD, including naringenin, quercetin, licochalcone a, and shinpterocarpin, exhibited strong binding affinities to estrogen receptors (ESR1 and ESR2) with binding energies below −8.0 kcal/mol, suggesting estrogen signaling as a critical mechanism for pain relief. These findings provide molecular evidence supporting SGD's therapeutic potential as an alternative to conventional carbamazepine treatment and highlight the role of phytoestrogens and estrogen-mediated anti-inflammatory pathways in managing trigeminal neuralgia.

title: Triptonide-mediated PTGS2 Inhibition Induces Autophagic Cell Death to Suppress the Progression of Triple-negative Breast Cancer and Epithelial Ovarian Cancer
summary:
# Summary

Triptonide (TN), a natural compound, exhibits potent anticancer activity against triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC) and epithelial ovarian cancer (EOC) through a dual mechanism targeting PTGS2 (cyclooxygenase-2). TN binds directly to His-207 in PTGS2 and induces its proteasomal degradation via recruitment of the E3 ubiquitin ligase NEDD4, ultimately downregulating the JAK/STAT3/c-Myc signaling axis that drives tumor cell proliferation and stemness. This PTGS2 inhibition triggers autophagic cell death in cancer cells, as evidenced by formation of autophagic vacuoles, decreased p62 expression, and increased LC3-II levels; notably, blocking autophagy partially rescues cells from TN-induced death, confirming autophagy as a critical component of its mechanism. In preclinical models, TN effectively suppresses tumor growth, reduces cell viability and clonogenic ability, induces apoptosis, and inhibits metastasis in both TNBC and EOC, with responses correlating strongly to baseline PTGS2 expression levels. Additionally, TN sensitizes cancer cells to chemotherapy (carboplatin and cisplatin), overcoming platinum resistance in EOC by preventing carboplatin-induced PTGS2 upregulation. These findings establish triptonide as a promising therapeutic candidate for TNBC and EOC with potential to enhance conventional chemotherapy efficacy.

title: Rosa roxburghii Tratt fruit polysaccharides inhibit proliferation and induce apoptosis in human cervical cancer cell line
summary: Rosa roxburghii Tratt fruit polysaccharides (RTFP) demonstrate potent anti-cancer activity against cervical cancer cells through multiple mechanisms that collectively suppress proliferation and induce apoptotic cell death. RTFP inhibits HeLa cell proliferation, migration, and invasion in a dose- and time-dependent manner while simultaneously arresting cells at the G2/M phase of the cell cycle through downregulation of the cell cycle regulators CDK-1 and cyclin B1.
