The compound promotes apoptosis by upregulating pro-apoptotic proteins including Bax, caspase-3, caspase-8, and caspase-9 while simultaneously downregulating the anti-apoptotic protein Bcl-2, thus shifting the balance toward programmed cell death. Beyond its direct cytotoxic effects, RTFP exhibits multiple bioactive properties including antioxidant, hypoglycemic, and anti-inflammatory activities that may contribute to its overall therapeutic potential. These findings position natural polysaccharides like RTFP as promising candidates for cervical cancer treatment, particularly as part of broader approaches combining direct apoptotic mechanisms with immune-enhancing and anti-inflammatory properties.

title: Mechanism of quercetin in the treatment of endometriosis based on network pharmacology and transcriptome sequencing
summary:
# Summary

This study elucidates quercetin's therapeutic mechanism in endometriosis through network pharmacology and transcriptome sequencing, identifying PDGFRB as a central target. Transcriptome analysis and immunohistochemistry revealed that PDGFRB, RAS, RAF1, and ERK1/2 are significantly elevated in ectopic endometrial lesions, where PDGFRB activates the RAS-MAPK signaling cascade to promote disease pathology. In an endometriosis mouse model, quercetin treatment dose-dependently reduced ectopic lesion volume and adhesion scores while downregulating PDGFRB expression and its downstream MAPK pathway components (RAS, RAF1, and ERK1/2). In vitro studies demonstrated that quercetin suppressed endometriosis cell viability, proliferation, invasion, and migration, with enhanced effects when combined with PDGFRB knockdown, which independently inhibited these cellular processes. These findings indicate that quercetin exerts its therapeutic effects on endometriosis by targeting PDGFRB and inhibiting the MAPK signaling pathway, supporting quercetin as a potential natural compound for treating this condition characterized by chronic pain and infertility.

title: Leonurine: A Multifaceted Bioactive Alkaloid—From Pharmacological Mechanisms to Clinical Translation: A Narrative Review
summary:
# Summary

Leonurine is a distinctive alkaloid alkaloid derived from *Leonurus japonicus* that exhibits multifaceted pharmacological properties with potential clinical applications in endometriosis and related gynecological conditions. The compound demonstrates potent antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects through multiple mechanisms, including reduction of reactive oxygen species and DNA fragmentation, downregulation of inflammatory signaling molecules (JNK, cFOS, cJUN, p65, IκB), and upregulation of MST1-mediated NF-κB inhibition. Leonurine also exhibits uterotonic effects and shows promise in treating endometriosis by addressing the oxidative stress and inflammatory pathways underlying ectopic endometrial growth. Additionally, the compound displays protective effects in cardiovascular, neurological, and reproductive disorders through activation of antioxidant pathways (PI3K/Akt/eNOS) and inhibition of inflammasome-mediated pyroptosis. While leonurine undergoes hepatic metabolism via CYP450 enzymes (CYP2D6, CYP1A2, CYP3A4) and exhibits competitive inhibition of these enzymes, its potent bioactivity and broad therapeutic potential warrant further clinical translation studies.

title: Multidimensional mechanisms of quercetin in diabetic kidney disease
journal: Frontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology
summary:
# Summary

Diabetic kidney disease develops through multiple interconnected mechanisms including chronic hyperglycemia, oxidative stress, chronic inflammation, cellular senescence, and various cell death pathways. Quercetin, a natural flavonoid, exhibits substantial therapeutic potential for diabetic kidney disease through its antidiabetic, antioxidant, and anti-inflammatory properties. The compound exerts its effects by binding to key disease-associated proteins including MMP9, EGFR, MMP2, AKT1, and GSK3β, thereby modulating critical signaling pathways such as PI3K-AKT and MAPK. Notably, the combination of quercetin with dasatinib effectively eliminates senescent cells in diabetic kidney disease patients and aged mice by reducing senescence markers (p16, p21) and suppressing senescence-associated secretory phenotypes including pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1α, IL-6, TNF-α) and matrix metalloproteinases. Through these multidimensional mechanisms targeting cellular senescence, oxidative stress, and inflammation, quercetin represents a promising polyphenolic therapeutic approach to mitigating diabetic kidney disease progression.

title: Systems-level actions of luteolin in female reproductive disorders: from molecular mechanisms to clinical translation
summary: Luteolin, a dietary flavone found abundantly in herbs like celery, parsley, and thyme, demonstrates potent therapeutic potential across multiple female reproductive disorders through coordinated engagement of antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and anti-fibrotic molecular networks. The compound reduces oxidative stress and chronic inflammation—core pathological mechanisms shared by endometriosis, polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), uterine leiomyomas, and primary ovarian insufficiency—by activating the Nrf2/ARE antioxidant axis and suppressing NF-κB/MAPK inflammatory cascades while modulating PI3K/AKT/PTEN and TGF-β/Smad signaling pathways. In endometriosis specifically, luteolin disrupts macrophage-lesion interactions, decreases angiogenesis, and suppresses chemokine-driven inflammation to inhibit lesion growth, while in leiomyomas it attenuates fibrotic remodeling and restores apoptotic balance. Clinical translation is supported by emerging human studies demonstrating that luteolin reduces key inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, IL-1β, TNF-α), mast cell mediators, and oxidative stress biomarkers, while improving insulin sensitivity in PCOS and preserving ovarian reserve in primary ovarian insufficiency. The compound also exhibits antiproliferative and chemosensitizing effects in gynecological malignancies, though bioavailability is limited by extensive phase II conjugation via UDP-glucuronosyltransferases and sulfotransferases, highlighting the need for formulation optimization to enhance clinical efficacy.

title: Effects of epigallocatechin-3-gallate on left ventricular remodeling and functional recovery following aortic coarctation surgery
summary:
# Summary

This study investigated epigallocatechin-3-gallate (EGCG), a polyphenol from green tea, as a cardioprotective agent following aortic coarctation surgery using a transverse aortic constriction (TAC) mouse model. EGCG treatment significantly improved left ventricular function by increasing fractional shortening and ejection fraction while reducing ventricular volumes and the heart weight-to-body weight ratio, demonstrating reversal of pressure overload-induced remodeling. At the cellular level, EGCG reduced cardiomyocyte hypertrophy, decreased myocardial fibrosis and collagen deposition, and restored mitochondrial morphology and density while inhibiting cardiomyocyte apoptosis. The compound's cardioprotective effects were attributed to its antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, combined with its ability to modulate calcium handling and promote mitochondrial function, thereby preventing both acute myocardial damage and chronic maladaptive remodeling after pressure unloading. These findings suggest EGCG may enhance cardiac recovery in patients undergoing aortic coarctation repair.

title: Pharmacological and clinical properties of curcumin
summary: Curcumin, a major polyphenolic compound derived from turmeric (Curcuma longa), exhibits diverse pharmacological properties with significant therapeutic potential across multiple disease states. The compound exerts anticancer effects by inhibiting carcinogenesis through multiple mechanisms, including induction of cell cycle arrest and apoptosis, while suppressing cellular proliferation. These effects are mediated through interactions with key signaling pathways, particularly NF-κB, JNK, COX-2, Akt, and EGFR, as well as emerging targets like mTOR. Beyond oncology, curcumin functions as a potent anti-inflammatory and antioxidant agent, suppressing pro-inflammatory cytokines and adhesion molecules (ICAM-1, VCAM-1, MCP-1), while demonstrating efficacy against viral infections including HIV-1 and herpes simplex virus-1 through inhibition of reverse transcriptase and viral replication pathways. Curcumin also provides cardiovascular benefits by preventing vascular dysfunction, reducing blood pressure and atherosclerotic lesions, and decreasing oxidative stress through suppression of superoxide production. Despite its therapeutic promise, curcumin's clinical utility is limited by poor gastrointestinal absorption and bioavailability, with the compound being metabolized to glucuronide and sulfate conjugates, though nanocurcumin formulations show improved antimicrobial activity and aqueous dispersion.

title: Vitamin D3 as an immunomodulatory agent: molecular mechanisms, clinical translation, and precision therapeutic strategies
summary:
# Summary

Vitamin D3 functions as a potent immunomodulatory agent through vitamin D receptor (VDR) signaling, orchestrating immune homeostasis by simultaneously suppressing pro-inflammatory pathways and enhancing antimicrobial and antioxidant defenses. The active metabolite 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D3 inhibits the NF-κB and NLRP3 inflammasome signaling axes while activating the Nrf2-mediated antioxidant system, thereby reducing production of inflammatory cytokines including TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6, and IL-18. At the cellular level, vitamin D3 promotes M2 macrophage polarization, suppresses Th1 and Th17 differentiation while promoting regulatory T cell formation, inhibits B cell maturation and antibody production, and attenuates natural killer cell activity. The therapeutic efficacy of vitamin D3 supplementation in autoimmune and inflammatory diseases is highly dependent on patient-specific factors including serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D levels and VDR gene polymorphisms, with emerging evidence indicating its preventive potential and additional mechanisms involving autophagy activation, the Hippo-YAP1 pathway, the SIRT3-SOD2 axis, and gut microbiome modulation. These multifaceted immunoregulatory mechanisms establish vitamin D3 as a precision therapeutic candidate for autoimmune and inflammatory conditions, though individualized dosing strategies based on biochemical and genetic profiling are essential for optimal clinical translation.

title: Boswellic Acid and Carnosine Ameliorate Vanadyl-Sulfate-Induced Renal Damage via Regulating Nrf2/HO-1 and PI3K/AKT Pathways
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that boswellic acid and carnosic acid, two natural polyphenolic compounds, protect against vanadyl sulfate-induced nephrotoxicity by modulating oxidative stress and inflammatory signaling pathways. Vanadyl sulfate exposure impairs renal function by elevating serum creatinine and urea while simultaneously increasing oxidative damage markers (malondialdehyde) and suppressing antioxidant enzyme activities (SOD, GSH, GR, CAT), alongside downregulation of the cytoprotective Nrf2/HO-1 pathway. Treatment with either boswellic acid or carnosic acid reversed these harmful effects by restoring antioxidant defenses, upregulating the Nrf2/HO-1 cytoprotective pathway, and suppressing pro-inflammatory gene expression including iNOS, PI3K, and AKT activation. These findings reveal that the renoprotective mechanisms of these natural compounds operate through simultaneous activation of the Nrf2-mediated antioxidant response and inhibition of inflammatory PI3K/AKT signaling. The study suggests that boswellic acid and carnosic acid may serve as therapeutic candidates for preventing oxidative and inflammatory kidney damage caused by environmental toxins.

title: Synergistic Effects of Morin and Doxorubicin Overcome Chemoresistance in Ovarian Cancer: Preclinical Insights From in vitro and in vivo Models
summary:
# Summary

This preclinical study demonstrates that morin, a naturally occurring flavonoid found in fruits, vegetables, and herbs, synergistically enhances doxorubicin's anticancer efficacy against ovarian cancer cells, including doxorubicin-resistant lines. The combination therapy overcomes chemoresistance through multiple complementary mechanisms: morin inhibits anti-apoptotic BCL-2 and activates pro-apoptotic caspases (3 and 9), while also modulating autophagy via LC3-II and Beclin-1 upregulation, achieving significantly higher apoptosis rates (61% versus 40% for doxorubicin alone). Additionally, the combination suppresses cell cycle progression by reducing cyclins A2 and D1 while simultaneously reducing oxidative stress through morin's antioxidant properties. In vivo tumor xenograft models confirmed the synergistic effect with reduced tumor volume, and combination therapy maintained normal body weight compared to doxorubicin monotherapy, suggesting improved tolerability. These findings suggest morin could be developed as an adjuvant to overcome doxorubicin resistance in platinum-resistant ovarian cancer.

title: The Prospects of Palmitylethanolamide in Tumor Prevention and Treatment
summary:
# Summary

Palmitylethanolamide (PEA), an endogenous fatty acid amide, demonstrates potent anti-tumor potential through multiple interconnected mechanisms involving inflammatory suppression, immune modulation, and cell death induction. PEA exerts anti-inflammatory, pro-apoptotic, anti-angiogenic, and antioxidant effects primarily by activating the nuclear receptor PPAR-α and the transcription factor Nrf2, while simultaneously inhibiting the NF-κB signaling pathway and reducing COX-2 expression and prostaglandin production. By suppressing pro-inflammatory cytokines such as TNF-α and IL-6, and promoting M1 macrophage polarization toward a pro-inflammatory phenotype, PEA enhances anti-tumor immune responses while preventing the tumor microenvironment from supporting tumor growth, metastasis, and drug resistance. These multifaceted mechanisms position PEA as a promising natural compound for cancer prevention and treatment, particularly as an adjunct to conventional therapies that carry significant side effects including immunosuppression, nausea, vomiting, and hair loss.

title: Matrine in Liver Diseases: Mechanistic Insights and Therapeutic Potential
summary: Matrine, a quinolizidine alkaloid derived from Sophora flavescens and related legumes, exhibits broad hepatoprotective effects against multiple liver disorders including injury, metabolic-associated fatty liver disease, fibrosis, and hepatocellular carcinoma. The compound exerts its protective mechanisms through inhibition of inflammatory responses and oxidative stress, reduction of endoplasmic reticulum stress, suppression of hepatic stellate cell activation, and induction of autophagy and apoptosis in cancer cells. Matrine's therapeutic effects are mediated by multiple signaling pathways including NF-κB, Nrf2, PI3K/AKT/mTOR, JAK2/STAT3, HSF1/PGC-1α, and p53/AMPK, with additional mechanisms involving pregnane X receptor-mediated upregulation of CYP3A4 for cholestasis treatment. In specific disease models such as acetaminophen-induced liver injury and carbon tetrachloride-induced toxicity, matrine significantly lowered serum transaminases while enhancing antioxidant enzyme activities and ameliorating anxiety-like and depression-like behaviors through suppression of pro-inflammatory cytokines. These findings establish matrine as a promising natural compound with multifaceted hepatoprotective potential warranting further clinical development.

title: Combined Antineoplastic Effects of Metformin, Boric Acid and Resveratrol in SKOV3 Ovarian Cancer Cells
summary:
# Summary

Metformin, boric acid, and resveratrol each independently reduce SKOV3 ovarian cancer cell viability and migration in a concentration- and time-dependent manner, with combination treatments—particularly metformin plus boric acid or metformin plus resveratrol—producing significantly enhanced anti-proliferative effects. These three agents suppress critical inflammatory and tumor-associated biomarkers including IL-17, NF-κB, and midkine (MDK), while simultaneously upregulating the cell cycle inhibitor p21, indicating coordinated suppression of pathways that drive tumor growth and metastatic capacity. The underlying mechanisms involve resveratrol's modulation of multiple signaling cascades (PI3K/AKT/mTOR, p53, MAPKs, STAT3), boric acid's antioxidant and immunomodulatory properties, and metformin's regulation of AMPK and p21 pathways. These findings are particularly relevant given that chronic inflammatory conditions such as endometriosis and pelvic inflammatory disease are established risk factors for ovarian cancer development and progression, suggesting that combined natural compound therapy may offer a therapeutic strategy to target the inflammation-cancer axis in ovarian malignancy.

title: Wild Edible Fruits: A Structured Narrative Review on Bioactive Composition and Bioactivity
summary:
# Summary

Wild edible fruits possess diverse bioactive compounds that confer significant health benefits through multiple biological mechanisms. Phenolic compounds and carotenoids actively modulate oxidative stress via free radical scavenging, while terpenoids and essential oils provide antimicrobial activity. Specific fruits such as Tamarindus indica offer concentrated protein and essential minerals like calcium and zinc, whereas Solanum torvum and Terminalia citrina are notable sources of vitamin C, and wild orange provides important electrolytes including potassium and magnesium. The comprehensive phytochemical composition of wild edible fruits underpins their antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, cardioprotective, and neuroprotective activities, positioning them as valuable dietary supplements for preventing micronutrient deficiencies and reducing diet-related diseases. These findings demonstrate that wild edible fruits represent underutilized nutritional resources capable of complementing conventional diets and supporting metabolic and immune function through their rich bioactive profiles.

title: Research Progress on Anticancer Mechanism of Ginsenoside Regulating Tumor Microenvironment
summary:
# Summary

Ginsenosides, particularly the protopanaxadiol (PPD)-type saponins Rg3, Rh2, and Rb2, demonstrate potent anticancer mechanisms by regulating the tumor microenvironment through multiple signaling pathways. Ginsenoside Rg3 exhibits the most comprehensive activity, inhibiting tumor growth, invasion, and metastasis by downregulating pro-tumoral mediators such as VEGF, HIF-1α, and matrix metalloproteinases while simultaneously suppressing epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT) through E-cadherin upregulation and N-cadherin/vimentin downregulation. Other ginsenosides work through distinct mechanisms: Rh2 targets angiogenesis via CNNM1 reduction and enhances immunotherapy by activating the TBK1-IRF3 pathway to promote CD8+ T cell infiltration, while Rg1 induces reactive oxygen species-mediated apoptosis and inhibits TGF-β1-induced invasion. Ginsenoside Rb1 specifically antagonizes hypoxia-induced EMT markers and suppresses cancer stem cell self-renewal in ovarian cancer. Meta-analyses confirm that Rg3 combined with chemotherapy improves overall survival and enhances CD4/CD8 T-cell ratios, indicating synergistic immunomodulatory effects. These findings establish ginsenosides as promising natural compounds for combination cancer therapeutics targeting multiple aspects of the tumor microenvironment.

title: A Study on the Mechanism of Acetyl Tributyl Citrate-Induced Infertility Toxicity and the Protective Action of Icariin Based on Network Toxicology, Network Pharmacology, Molecular-Docking Technology and Molecular Dynamics Simulation
summary: This study employed network toxicology and pharmacology approaches to elucidate how acetyl tributyl citrate (ATBC), a plasticizer that leaches from food packaging, induces infertility through ovarian dysfunction and apoptosis. ATBC exerts toxic effects by binding to and activating key proteins including HSP90AA1, PIK3CA, CASP3, and HRAS, which converge on the PI3K-Akt signaling pathway to trigger follicular DNA fragmentation, increased apoptosis, and depletion of primordial, primary, and secondary follicles while disrupting reproductive hormone activity. Icariin, a polyphenolic compound derived from the herbal medicine Epimedium, counteracts ATBC-induced infertility by binding with high affinity to IL6, TNF, INS, and STAT3 and subsequently downregulating the same PI3K-Akt pathway to promote testosterone synthesis, spermatogenesis, and restoration of ovarian and uterine tissue structure. Molecular docking simulations confirmed these binding interactions, with icariin demonstrating particularly strong affinities (−6.7 to −7.3 kcal/mol) for its therapeutic targets. These findings establish the PI3K-Akt signaling pathway as the central mechanism linking environmental plasticizer exposure to reproductive toxicity and highlight icariin's therapeutic potential through pathway modulation.

title: Flavonoids as a Potential Antifungal Alternative Against Candida auris (Candidozyma auris) from Clades III and IV
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that flavonoids represent a promising antifungal strategy against multidrug-resistant *Candida auris*, particularly clades III and IV, which exhibit high mortality and resistance to echinocandins. The primary mechanism of action involves inhibition of efflux pumps (MDR and CDR), which are major contributors to azole resistance in this pathogen, with quercetin, fisetin, hesperetin, luteolin, and apigenin showing the strongest inhibitory effects. Testing on two clinical isolates revealed that strain CJ97 (clade III) displayed dose-dependent growth inhibition by multiple flavonoids, while strain 20-1498 (clade IV) proved more resistant to both flavonoids and standard antifungals, though several compounds including rutin, luteolin, hesperetin, and naringenin achieved minimum inhibitory concentrations lower than fluconazole. Notably, hesperetin and luteolin demonstrated activity against the highly resistant clade IV strain at 500 μM concentrations, reducing colony-forming units and displaying comparable or lower toxicity than conventional antifungals. While flavonoids exhibited primarily fungistatic rather than fungicidal activity, these findings support their potential as a therapeutic alternative for treating infections caused by these critically resistant *C. auris* clades where conventional antifungals have failed.

title: Exploring the Anti-Cervical Cancer Effect and Hepatotoxicity Risk of Gossypol Based on Untargeted Metabolomics and Network Toxicology
summary: Gossypol, a polyphenol compound, demonstrates significant anti-cervical cancer effects through suppression of tumor growth in vivo and dose-dependent downregulation of key signaling proteins PIK3R2, GRB2, and MAPK1, which mediate the PI3K/AKT pathway critical for cancer cell survival and proliferation. Beyond cervical cancer, gossypol exhibits broad therapeutic potential for gynecological disorders including endometriosis, uterine fibroids, and abnormal uterine bleeding, alongside documented antiviral, antioxidant, and antitumor properties. However, gossypol treatment is associated with hepatotoxicity risk, with network toxicology analysis identifying mTOR, TNF-α, CASP3, and anti-apoptotic BCL2/BCL2L1 proteins as key hepatotoxicity-related targets. High-dose gossypol administration increased hepatic index and TNF-α levels while reducing splenic index and IL-10 levels in treated mice, suggesting inflammatory hepatic changes and potential hepatocyte apoptosis. These findings highlight gossypol's dual nature as a promising anti-cancer agent for gynecological malignancies while necessitating careful dosing strategies to minimize hepatic toxicity and warrant further investigation into safe therapeutic windows for clinical application.

title: Imperatorin: A Furanocoumarin with Potential in Combating Cancer Development and Progression—A Comprehensive Review
summary: Imperatorin, a furanocoumarin compound found in medicinal plants including Angelica dahurica, Archangelica officinalis, and Pastinaca sativa, exhibits potent multi-targeted anticancer mechanisms through both direct cellular effects and pathway modulation. The compound induces apoptosis via mitochondrial pathways involving cytochrome c release and caspase activation, with p53 activation playing a critical role, while simultaneously inhibiting cell proliferation and suppressing angiogenesis through HIF-1α inhibition. Imperatorin disrupts multiple oncogenic signaling cascades including PI3K/Akt, MAPK/ERK, mTOR, and NF-κB pathways, while reducing oxidative stress and pro-inflammatory mediators while enhancing immune-cell-mediated cytotoxicity. Related furanocoumarins, including isoimperatorin and phellopterin, demonstrate similar anticancer activities with additional capacity to overcome chemoresistance. The biosynthesis of imperatorin involves sequential enzymatic steps coordinated by phenylpropanoid pathway enzymes (PAL, C4H, 4CL) and specialized cytochrome P450 enzymes (CYP71AZ18, CYP71AZ19) that generate critical intermediates, with prenyl transferases catalyzing final prenylation steps. Epigenetic regulation through chromatin accessibility further modulates imperatorin production, suggesting multiple layers of control over this therapeutically promising natural compound.

title: Brewed Coffee and Its Components Act Through Orphan Nuclear Receptor 4A1 (NR4A1)
summary: Brewed coffee and its polyphenolic components, including caffeic acid, ferulic acid, chlorogenic acid, and cinnamic acid derivatives, bind to and act as ligands for the orphan nuclear receptor NR4A1. These coffee-derived polyphenolics function as NR4A1-dependent inverse agonists that inhibit cell growth in NR4A1-responsive cancer cells, with loss of NR4A1 exacerbating tissue injury and implicating this receptor in stress response and inflammation protection. Beyond cancer biology, several polyphenolics including resveratrol, quercetin, and kaempferol have demonstrated inhibitory effects on endometriosis progression, suggesting a broader role for NR4A1 ligands in protecting against disease states. Coffee consumption is associated with decreased mortality and protection from aging-related diseases including metabolic disease, certain cancers, Parkinson's disease, dementia, and cardiovascular disease, effects likely mediated through NR4A1-dependent mechanisms. The antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties of coffee polyphenolics, combined with their capacity to modulate NR4A1 signaling, establish a molecular basis for coffee's documented health-protective effects and suggest NR4A1 as a key target linking dietary polyphenols to disease prevention.

title: Synergistic anticancer activity of frankincense aqueous extract with sorafenib in HepG2 cells and its UHPLC–QTOF–MS/MS-based metabolomic profiling
summary: This study demonstrates that frankincense aqueous extract (FrAE) synergistically enhances the anticancer activity of sorafenib, the standard targeted therapy for advanced hepatocellular carcinoma, in HepG2 liver cancer cells. The combination treatment induces integrated anticancer effects through multiple mechanisms including enhanced apoptosis, necrosis, autophagic signaling activation, cell cycle disruption, and inhibition of cancer cell migration, while reducing the effective concentration of sorafenib required for cytotoxicity. Phytochemical profiling identified that FrAE contains diterpenoids and triterpenoids, including boswellic acids and other compounds such as Boscartin C and Boscartol L, which exhibit inherent cytotoxic properties. Molecular docking analysis revealed that FrAE-derived terpenoids favorably bind to key apoptotic and autophagic regulators including BCL-2, p53, mTOR, and LC3C, providing mechanistic support for the observed synergistic anticancer effects. These findings suggest that combining natural polyphenolic compounds from frankincense with conventional kinase inhibitors represents a promising therapeutic strategy to improve hepatocellular carcinoma treatment efficacy while potentially reducing drug dosing requirements.

title: Ameliorative effects of mulberry fruit anthocyanin extract on gut microbiota and liver metabolites in high-fat and high-cholesterol diet-fed ApoE−/− mice
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that mulberry fruit anthocyanin extract (MFAE) and its major component cyanidin-3-glucoside (C3G) exert protective metabolic and anti-inflammatory effects in atherosclerosis-prone ApoE−/− mice fed a high-fat, high-cholesterol diet. The treatments reduced circulating low-density lipoprotein and the inflammatory cytokine interleukin-1β while simultaneously increasing hepatic glutathione peroxidase activity, enhancing antioxidant defenses. Anthocyanins modulated the gut microbiota composition by expanding beneficial butyrate-producing bacteria (Anaerotruncus, Tyzzerella, Butyricicoccus, and Roseburia) while reducing potentially pathogenic taxa associated with atherosclerosis (Sphingomonas, Odoribacter, and Rikenella). At the metabolite level, MFAE treatment downregulated pro-atherogenic metabolites (ATP and indole-3-butyric acid) while upregulating beneficial compounds including glutamine and phosphatidylglycerol. These findings suggest that anthocyanin-rich foods ameliorate dyslipidemia and inflammation through coordinated effects on lipid metabolism, hepatic antioxidant capacity, and microbiota-derived metabolite production, highlighting a polyphenol-mediated mechanism linking diet, microbiota, and cardiometabolic health.

title: Cisplatin Induces Inflammation by Activating IL-6 via Suppressing rno-let-7g-5p and rno-let-7f-5p Expression in Intestinal Epithelial Cells
summary:
# Summary

Cisplatin induces severe intestinal inflammation in epithelial cells primarily through a microRNA-mediated mechanism. The chemotherapeutic agent activates p38 and ERK signaling pathways, which suppress the expression of let-7f-5p and let-7g-5p microRNAs, thereby de-repressing interleukin-6 (IL-6) production in a dose-dependent manner. These let-7 microRNAs normally inhibit IL-6 expression by directly binding to its 3′UTR, so their cisplatin-induced downregulation leads to marked increases in IL-6 mRNA and protein levels. The resulting elevated IL-6 orchestrates chemotherapy-induced intestinal mucositis by disrupting the epithelial barrier and increasing intestinal permeability. Natural compounds including curcumin and ascorbic acid effectively attenuate this inflammatory cascade, suggesting potential protective strategies against cisplatin-induced gastrointestinal toxicity through restoration of let-7 microRNA expression or direct p38/ERK pathway inhibition.

title: Piperine protects ovarian follicles and stromal cells against doxorubicin-induced adverse effects in mouse ovaries
summary: Doxorubicin causes severe ovarian damage through oxidative stress and inflammation, reducing normal follicles, inducing granulosa cell apoptosis, and ultimately leading to premature ovarian failure and infertility. Piperine, a bioactive polyphenol from black and long pepper, protects against these adverse effects by leveraging its potent antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, including inhibition of reactive oxygen species, upregulation of antioxidant enzymes (superoxide dismutase and catalase), and downregulation of pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β and TNF-α). When combined with doxorubicin, piperine preserves follicle integrity, maintains stromal cell density and collagen levels, and upregulates catalase mRNA expression—effects superior to piperine or N-acetylcysteine alone. The protective mechanism involves activation of the NRF2 signaling pathway, which counteracts doxorubicin's inhibition of NRF2 expression and function, thereby restoring cellular redox homeostasis. These findings suggest that piperine could serve as a chemotherapy adjuvant to mitigate ovarian toxicity and preserve fertility in cancer patients receiving doxorubicin-based treatments.

title: Consumption of Cashew Nuts on Oxidative Stress and Neuroinflammation Markers in Methionine‐Induced Hyperhomocysteinemia in Rats
summary:
# Summary

Methionine supplementation induces hyperhomocysteinemia, which triggers oxidative stress and neuroinflammation through increased reactive oxygen species production, elevated malondialdehyde levels, and reduced glutathione availability, ultimately leading to neuronal damage, apoptosis, and cognitive impairment. Hyperhomocysteinemia impairs the Nrf2/HO-1 antioxidant pathway and activates microglia and astrocytes, increasing expression of neuroinflammatory markers GFAP and Iba-1 while reducing the neuronal marker MAP2. Daily cashew nut consumption, rich in polyphenols, flavonoids, and anthocyanins, effectively counteracted these pathological effects by restoring Nrf2/HO-1 signaling, reducing both plasma and brain oxidative markers, and suppressing neuroinflammatory responses. Cashew nuts significantly reduced apoptosis by restoring favorable Bcl-2/Bax ratios and partially restored MAP2 expression in affected brain regions. These findings demonstrate that polyphenol-rich natural compounds can serve as therapeutic agents against hyperhomocysteinemia-induced neurotoxicity and neuroinflammation.

title: Abrine targets ERK to suppress EMT and lung metastasis model via MAPKs and Nrf2/Keap-1/HO-1 signaling
summary: Abrine, an indole alkaloid natural compound, exerts potent anti-metastatic effects by directly binding ERK and inhibiting MAPK signaling (phospho-ERK, phospho-JNK, and phospho-p38), thereby reversing TGF-β1-induced epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT) in lung cancer cells. The compound suppresses TGF-β1-induced migration and invasion while restoring epithelial markers (E-cadherin) and downregulating mesenchymal markers (N-cadherin, vimentin, Snail, and Slug) without cytotoxic effects. In addition to MAPK inhibition, Abrine modulates the Nrf2/Keap-1/HO-1 antioxidant pathway, contributing to its anti-tumor activity. In vivo studies using B16-F10 lung metastasis models demonstrated that Abrine (20–40 mg/kg) significantly reduced metastatic burden, lung wet weight, and Ki67 proliferation while improving body weight. Systemically, Abrine reduced pro-inflammatory and immunomodulatory cytokines (IL-17, IL-10, TNF-α, and IFN-γ), suggesting combined direct anti-metastatic and immunoregulatory mechanisms that suppress cancer progression.

title: H2S‐Releasing Aspirin Nanoparticles Alleviate Endometriosis and Associated Anxiety
summary:
# H2S-Releasing Aspirin Nanoparticles Alleviate Endometriosis and Associated Anxiety

This study presents ACS14@BSA, a novel hydrogen sulfide-releasing nanoparticle formulation based on aspirin encapsulated in albumin, as a dual-action therapy for endometriosis and its associated psychiatric comorbidity. The nanoparticles selectively accumulate at ectopic lesions via neutrophil hitchhiking and locally release hydrogen sulfide, which inhibits ectopic cell proliferation and reduces inflammation by suppressing the PI3K/Akt and NF-κB signaling pathways. Systemically, the hydrogen sulfide crosses the blood-brain barrier and reaches the anterior cingulate cortex, where it upregulates glutamate transporter 1, thereby reducing extracellular glutamate levels and suppressing glutamatergic neuron hyperactivity—a key mechanism driving endometriosis-associated anxiety. In a mouse model, ACS14@BSA treatment significantly reduced lesion volume, decreased proliferating cells (Ki67+), increased apoptotic cells (TUNEL+), and lowered pro-inflammatory cytokine IL-6 expression in ectopic tissues while simultaneously alleviating anxiety-related behaviors. This non-hormonal, non-invasive gasotransmitter-based approach uniquely addresses both the local pathology of endometriosis and the central nervous system dysfunction underlying its psychiatric complications, offering a therapeutic advance over current symptomatic management strategies.

title: Bioactivities of Alchemilla alpina L. Extract on Women’s Reproductive and Metabolic Health: Antioxidant, Enzyme Inhibitory, Receptor Modulatory Properties and Potential Cytotoxic Effects
summary:
# Summary

Alchemilla alpina L. extract, rich in 39 distinct polyphenolic compounds including rutin, catechin, caffeic acid, and kaempferol derivatives, demonstrates multiple bioactivities relevant to women's reproductive health and metabolic regulation. The methanolic extract exhibits strong antioxidant properties through DPPH and ABTS radical scavenging, reduces reactive oxygen species in fibroblasts, and inhibits key metabolic enzymes including α-glucosidase and acetylcholinesterase. Notably, the extract selectively binds to estrogen receptor α and inhibits AKR1C3, an enzyme involved in androgen synthesis, while also suppressing COX-1 and COX-2 activity by over 70%, supporting its anti-inflammatory potential. These mechanistic findings align with traditional medicinal uses and emerging evidence for treating gynecological conditions including endometriosis, menstrual irregularities, dysmenorrhea, and polycystic ovary syndrome. The extract demonstrated moderate but concentration-dependent cytotoxic effects against cervical and ovarian cancer cell lines, suggesting potential therapeutic applications beyond reproductive health. Collectively, the pleiotropic bioactivities of A. alpina—encompassing antioxidant, enzyme inhibitory, and receptor-modulatory effects—provide a biochemical rationale for its traditional use in managing hormonal imbalance and women's health disorders.

title: Taxifolin Attenuates Remote Lung Injury Induced by Hepatic Ischemia–Reperfusion in Rats
summary:
# Summary

Hepatic ischemia-reperfusion injury triggers systemic inflammatory responses and oxidative stress that cause remote lung injury through activation of the NF-κB and HMGB1 signaling pathways. Taxifolin, a naturally occurring flavonoid with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, attenuates this remote lung damage by directly binding to TLR4 and NF-κB p65 and subsequently downregulating both NF-κB and HMGB1 expression. In rat models, taxifolin pretreatment significantly reduced markers of oxidative stress (malondialdehyde and total oxidative status), inflammatory cytokines (interleukin-6), and pathological lung alterations including pulmonary edema, septal thickening, and inflammatory cell infiltration. The protective mechanism involves inhibition of the TLR4-mediated HMGB1-NF-κB signaling cascade, which normally amplifies innate immune responses and promotes inflammatory gene expression in distant organs. These findings demonstrate that taxifolin represents a promising therapeutic compound for preventing systemic remote organ injury associated with hepatic ischemia-reperfusion events.

title: Causal Effects of Diet on Atopic Dermatitis: A Mendelian Randomization Study Implicating Lipid Pathways and Clinical Implications
summary:
# Summary

This Mendelian randomization study demonstrates that dietary patterns causally influence atopic dermatitis risk through lipid metabolism pathways. Diets rich in unsaturated fats, particularly from oil-based spreads, lamb, and muesli, protect against atopic dermatitis, while refined grain intake from brown bread and wholemeal bread increases disease risk. The protective effects of unsaturated fat-rich foods are mechanistically mediated by specific lipids—sphingomyelin SM C20:2 and phosphatidylcholine species—that regulate skin barrier integrity and immune responses, whereas the pathogenic effects of refined grains operate through pro-inflammatory VLDL particle elevation. Dysregulated lipid metabolism, including elevated omega-6 fatty acids and aberrant sphingolipid and lipoprotein profiles, represents a hallmark mechanism underlying dietary contributions to atopic dermatitis pathogenesis. These findings provide causal evidence that modifying dietary fat quality and grain processing can reduce atopic dermatitis risk by normalizing systemic lipid homeostasis and associated inflammatory immune responses.

title: Nutrition in the prevention and treatment of pelvic inflammatory disease: a review
summary:
# Summary

This review demonstrates that nutritional interventions substantially reduce pelvic inflammatory disease (PID) risk through multiple anti-inflammatory mechanisms. Vitamins C, A, and E function as potent antioxidants and signaling modulators, inhibiting NF-κB pathways, suppressing proinflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6) while promoting anti-inflammatory IL-10, and reducing inflammatory mediators like prostaglandins and phospholipase A2. Minerals including zinc, magnesium, copper, selenium, and phosphorus further suppress inflammatory responses through NF-κB inhibition, cytokine suppression, and macrophage polarization toward anti-inflammatory M2 phenotypes. Carotenoids modulate beneficial gut microbiota composition by enriching protective bacteria like Akkermansia while depleting pathogenic species such as Helicobacter. Dietary fiber and choline activate anti-inflammatory pathways by promoting short-chain fatty acid production—which strengthens intestinal barriers, inhibits pathogens, and drives regulatory T-cell differentiation—and by activating cholinergic receptors that suppress TNF-α and IL-6 release. Collectively, these findings establish that comprehensive nutritional strategies targeting oxidative stress, microbial dysbiosis, and immune dysregulation effectively prevent and treat PID caused by ascending bacterial pathogens.
